While animal models have provided valuable insights, species differences in neuronal architecture and neurotransmitter systems necessitate direct examination of human epileptic tissue. We describe a comprehensive methodological framework for maintaining viable human cortical specimens obtained from surgical resections of epileptogenic foci in patients with medically refractory epilepsy. Brain slices (400-500 μm) were prepared within 30 minutes of surgical removal and maintained in oxygenated artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 32°C. Electrophysiological recordings were performed using multielectrode arrays and patch-clamp techniques to characterize spontaneous and evoked epileptiform activity. Pharmacological interventions included application of various antiepileptic drugs, glutamate receptor antagonists, and GABA modulators. Tissue viability was assessed through cellular morphology, membrane potential stability, and metabolic indicators over 8-12 hour recording periods. Our protocol consistently yielded robust epileptiform discharges in 78% of specimens (n=45 patients), with maintained neuronal responsiveness to pharmacological manipulation for up to 10 hours post-resection. AMPA/kainate receptor blockade reduced seizure-like activity by 65±12%, while gabazine enhanced discharge frequency by 180±34%. This methodology provides a reliable platform for investigating human epileptic mechanisms and screening potential therapeutic compounds directly in pathological human neural networks, offering advantages over conventional animal models for translational epilepsy research.

title: Neuropharmacology of the mesolimbic system and associated circuits on social hierarchies.
abstract: Social hierarchies are fundamental organizational structures across mammalian species, yet the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. The mesolimbic dopaminergic system, critical for reward processing and motivation, has been implicated in dominance behaviors, but its specific role in hierarchy establishment and maintenance requires further investigation. Here, we examined the contribution of dopamine D1 and D2 receptors within the nucleus accumbens (NAc) and ventral tegmental area (VTA) to social rank determination in adult male C57BL/6J mice. Using a tube test paradigm to assess dominance relationships, we administered selective D1 (SCH23390) and D2 (sulpiride) receptor antagonists via bilateral microinjections prior to social encounters. Additionally, we employed optogenetic stimulation of VTA dopamine neurons during hierarchy formation trials. Behavioral analysis revealed that D1 receptor blockade in the NAc significantly reduced dominance scores (p<0.01), while D2 antagonism had minimal effects. VTA dopamine neuron stimulation enhanced dominant behaviors in subordinate animals (p<0.05). Immunohistochemical analysis showed increased c-Fos expression in NAc medium spiny neurons following successful dominance displays. Microdialysis measurements confirmed elevated extracellular dopamine levels in dominant animals during social interactions. These findings demonstrate that mesolimbic D1 receptor signaling is necessary for establishing social dominance, while VTA dopamine release is sufficient to promote hierarchical status. Our results provide novel insights into the neuropharmacological basis of social hierarchies and suggest potential therapeutic targets for disorders involving aberrant social behaviors.

title: Demon voltammetry and analysis software: analysis of cocaine-induced alterations in dopamine signaling using multiple kinetic measures.
abstract: Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) has emerged as a powerful technique for real-time measurement of dopamine release and uptake in brain tissue, yet comprehensive analysis of the complex kinetic parameters remains challenging. We developed DEMON (Dopamine Evaluation and Monitoring with Optimized Neurochemistry), a novel analysis software package designed to extract multiple kinetic measures from FSCV data with enhanced precision and automation. Using this software, we investigated cocaine-induced alterations in dopamine signaling in nucleus accumbens core of urethane-anesthetized rats (n=8). Electrical stimulation of the ventral tegmental area (single pulse, 350 μA, 4 ms) evoked dopamine release, which was measured before and after systemic cocaine administration (10 mg/kg, i.p.). DEMON software automatically calculated peak dopamine concentration, rise time (10-90%), decay time constant (τ), and area under the curve while minimizing user bias through standardized peak detection algorithms and baseline correction procedures. Cocaine treatment significantly increased peak dopamine concentration by 312 ± 45% compared to baseline, prolonged the decay time constant from 1.24 ± 0.18 s to 4.67 ± 0.74 s, and increased total dopamine signal (AUC) by 586 ± 89%. Rise time parameters remained unchanged, consistent with selective inhibition of the dopamine transporter rather than altered vesicular release mechanisms. The DEMON software package provides researchers with a standardized, comprehensive tool for FSCV data analysis that reveals multiple dimensions of dopamine kinetics, facilitating more nuanced understanding of dopaminergic neurotransmission in health and disease states.

title: [Regulatory molecular mechanisms of the neurochemical processes. History and the present time].
abstract: The understanding of regulatory molecular mechanisms governing neurochemical processes has evolved dramatically over the past century, from early observations of synaptic transmission to contemporary insights into complex signaling cascades. This review examines the historical development and current state of knowledge regarding molecular regulation in neurotransmission, focusing on key discoveries that have shaped modern neuropharmacology. We analyzed seminal publications from 1900-2003, tracing the progression from Dale's principle through the identification of neurotransmitter receptors, second messenger systems, and gene expression regulation. Historical analysis reveals three major evolutionary phases: the classical period (1900-1950) characterized by neurotransmitter identification, the receptor era (1950-1980) marked by pharmacological classification of binding sites, and the molecular biology revolution (1980-present) enabling detailed characterization of signaling mechanisms. Contemporary research demonstrates that neurochemical regulation involves intricate networks of G-protein coupled receptors, ion channels, protein kinases, and transcription factors operating across multiple temporal scales. Key regulatory mechanisms include presynaptic autoreceptor feedback, postsynaptic receptor desensitization, synaptic plasticity through CREB-mediated gene expression, and glial-neuronal interactions. Recent advances in molecular biology techniques have revealed previously unknown complexity in neurotransmitter systems, including receptor heteromerization, epigenetic modifications, and non-coding RNA regulation. Understanding these multilevel regulatory mechanisms provides crucial insights for developing targeted therapeutic interventions in neurological and psychiatric disorders, representing a paradigm shift from traditional pharmacological approaches toward precision neuromedicine based on molecular pathophysiology.

title: Dorsal raphe vs. median raphe serotonergic antagonism. Anatomical, physiological, behavioral, neuroendocrinological, neuropharmacological and clinical evidences: relevance for neuropharmacological therapy.
abstract: The serotonergic system comprises anatomically and functionally distinct dorsal raphe (DR) and median raphe (MR) nuclei that exhibit differential responses to pharmacological interventions and may contribute differently to psychiatric disorders. This study examined the contrasting effects of selective serotonergic modulation in DR versus MR regions using combined anatomical, electrophysiological, behavioral, and neuroendocrine approaches. Male Sprague-Dawley rats received microinjections of the 5-HT1A receptor antagonist WAY-100635 (0.1-1.0 μg) into either DR or MR nuclei, followed by assessment of anxiety-related behaviors in elevated plus maze and open field tests, measurement of plasma corticosterone and ACTH levels, and single-unit recordings from hippocampal CA1 pyramidal neurons. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed distinct 5-HT1A receptor distribution patterns between DR and MR regions. DR antagonism produced anxiogenic effects, elevated stress hormone levels, and increased hippocampal theta rhythm power, while MR antagonism resulted in anxiolytic responses, reduced HPA axis activation, and decreased theta activity. Electrophysiological recordings demonstrated that DR manipulation primarily affected dorsal hippocampal neuronal firing, whereas MR modulation influenced ventral hippocampal activity. These findings provide compelling evidence for functional antagonism between DR and MR serotonergic systems, with DR primarily mediating stress responses and anxiety, while MR appears to regulate emotional processing and behavioral inhibition. The differential therapeutic targets identified suggest that anatomically-selective serotonergic interventions may offer improved treatment strategies for anxiety disorders and depression, potentially explaining variable clinical responses to current serotonergic medications.

title: An integrative dynamic model of brain energy metabolism using in vivo neurochemical measurements.
abstract: Brain energy metabolism is critical for normal neuronal function, yet the dynamic relationships between glucose utilization, oxygen consumption, and neurotransmitter cycling remain poorly understood in vivo. We developed an integrative computational model that combines real-time neurochemical measurements with metabolic flux analysis to characterize brain energy dynamics during physiological states. Using microdialysis probes in awake, freely-moving rats (n=12), we simultaneously measured extracellular glucose, lactate, glutamate, and GABA concentrations in the hippocampus during rest, mild stress, and recovery periods. Oxygen tension was monitored using polarographic electrodes, while local field potentials were recorded to assess neural activity. These experimental data were incorporated into a multi-compartment mathematical model describing neuronal and glial metabolic interactions, including the astrocyte-neuron lactate shuttle and glutamate-glutamine cycle. The model successfully reproduced observed metabolic patterns, revealing that astrocytic glucose uptake increased 340% during neural activation while neuronal glucose utilization rose only 180%. Lactate production by astrocytes peaked within 2 minutes of stimulus onset, preceding maximal neuronal lactate consumption by 45 seconds. The model predicted that glutamate clearance accounted for 60-70% of total energy expenditure during high activity states. Sensitivity analysis identified astrocytic glucose transport and glutamate uptake kinetics as critical parameters governing metabolic responses. This integrative approach provides quantitative insights into brain bioenergetics and establishes a framework for investigating metabolic dysfunction in neurological disorders. The model's predictive capability may prove valuable for optimizing neuroprotective strategies and understanding energy-related pathophysiology.

title: Advancing equity, diversity, and inclusion in the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ACNP): advances, challenges, and opportunities to accelerate progress.
abstract: The American College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ACNP) has historically faced challenges in achieving equitable representation across gender, racial, ethnic, and socioeconomic dimensions within its membership and leadership structure. To assess current diversity metrics and identify barriers to inclusion, we conducted a comprehensive analysis of ACNP membership demographics, leadership composition, and meeting participation patterns from 2015-2020. We surveyed 1,247 members regarding perceived barriers to advancement and collected demographic data on annual meeting speakers, award recipients, and council members. Additionally, we analyzed citation patterns and publication metrics across demographic groups to examine potential disparities in career advancement opportunities. Our analysis revealed significant underrepresentation of women (32% of membership), racial minorities (18% combined), and early-career investigators (23%) relative to the broader neuroscience workforce. Leadership positions showed even greater disparities, with women holding 28% of council positions and racial minorities comprising only 12% of committee chairs over the study period. Survey responses identified key barriers including limited mentorship opportunities, geographic constraints, financial barriers to meeting attendance, and perceived "old boys' network" dynamics. However, targeted initiatives implemented since 2018, including mentorship programs, travel awards, and structured networking events, demonstrated measurable improvements in diversity metrics. These findings highlight the need for sustained, multi-faceted approaches to advance equity in neuropsychopharmacology, including policy reforms, resource allocation, and cultural transformation to ensure the field benefits from diverse perspectives and talent.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia: neural substrates and psychopharmacological implications.
abstract: Schizophrenia patients demonstrate consistent deficits in facial affect recognition that significantly impact social functioning and quality of life. While these impairments have been well-documented behaviorally, the underlying neural mechanisms and potential therapeutic targets remain poorly understood. This review synthesizes current neuroimaging and psychopharmacological evidence to elucidate the neurobiological basis of affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia and evaluate treatment implications. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of neuroimaging studies examining affect recognition in schizophrenia patients compared to healthy controls, and analyzed clinical trials investigating pharmacological interventions targeting these deficits. Converging neuroimaging evidence demonstrates abnormal activation patterns in the amygdala, fusiform face area, and prefrontal cortical regions during affect recognition tasks in schizophrenia patients. Structural abnormalities in these regions correlate with behavioral deficits, suggesting a neuroanatomical substrate. While typical antipsychotics show limited efficacy for improving affect recognition, emerging evidence suggests that atypical antipsychotics, particularly those with significant serotonergic activity, may provide modest benefits. Adjunctive treatments targeting cholinergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems show preliminary promise in small-scale studies. However, effect sizes remain modest and clinical significance unclear. Affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia involve disrupted limbic-cortical circuitry, with the amygdala and prefrontal regions as key substrates. Current pharmacological approaches provide limited therapeutic benefit, highlighting the need for novel treatment strategies targeting specific neurotransmitter systems underlying social cognitive dysfunction.

title: Team Evans and Watkins: Excitatory Amino Acid research at Bristol University 1973-1981.
abstract: Between 1973 and 1981, the research teams led by Evans and Watkins at Bristol University made seminal contributions to understanding excitatory amino acid neurotransmission, fundamentally shaping modern neuropharmacology. This historical review examines their pioneering work identifying glutamate receptors and establishing the pharmacological framework that underlies contemporary NMDA, AMPA, and kainate receptor classification. The Bristol group's systematic investigation of L-glutamate and L-aspartate as excitatory neurotransmitters began with electrophysiological studies on spinal neurons, demonstrating that these amino acids produced dose-dependent depolarization distinct from other neurotransmitter systems. Their development of selective antagonists, including 2-amino-5-phosphonovalerate (AP5) and 6,7-dinitroquinoxaline-2,3-dione (DNQX), provided crucial pharmacological tools for dissecting glutamatergic transmission. Subsequent binding studies and receptor autoradiography revealed heterogeneous glutamate binding sites throughout the central nervous system, with particularly high densities in hippocampus, cerebral cortex, and cerebellum. The team's characterization of N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) as a selective agonist and their discovery of the glycine modulatory site established the foundation for understanding NMDA receptor function in synaptic plasticity, learning, and excitotoxicity. These discoveries directly influenced the development of neuroprotective strategies for stroke and neurodegenerative diseases, while their receptor classification system remains the standard framework for glutamate pharmacology. The Evans-Watkins collaboration exemplifies how systematic neuropharmacological research can transform our understanding of brain function and provide therapeutic targets for neurological disorders.

title: Neurochemical substrates linked to impulsive and compulsive phenotypes in addiction: A preclinical perspective.
abstract: Addiction is characterized by a transition from impulsive drug-seeking to compulsive drug-taking behaviors, yet the neurochemical mechanisms underlying this phenotypic shift remain poorly understood. We examined dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic neurotransmitter systems in brain regions implicated in addiction using a well-validated rat self-administration model. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) underwent cocaine self-administration training followed by behavioral phenotyping using delay discounting and compulsivity tasks. Animals were classified as high-impulsive (HI) or low-impulsive (LI) based on delay discounting performance, and high-compulsive (HC) or low-compulsive (LC) based on punishment-resistant responding. Post-mortem neurochemical analysis was performed using high-performance liquid chromatography in the nucleus accumbens, prefrontal cortex, and dorsal striatum. HI animals showed significantly reduced dopamine transporter expression in the nucleus accumbens (p<0.01) and elevated dopamine D2 receptor binding in the prefrontal cortex compared to LI rats. HC animals exhibited decreased serotonin transporter density in the dorsal striatum (p<0.05) and reduced GABA concentrations in the prefrontal cortex relative to LC animals. Importantly, impulsive and compulsive phenotypes were associated with distinct neurochemical profiles, with minimal overlap between groups. Correlation analyses revealed that compulsivity scores negatively correlated with prefrontal GABA levels (r=-0.67, p<0.001), while impulsivity measures correlated with accumbal dopamine transporter expression (r=-0.58, p<0.01). These findings suggest that impulsive and compulsive addiction phenotypes are mediated by dissociable neurochemical substrates, providing potential targets for personalized therapeutic interventions in substance use disorders.

title: High frequency, real-time neurochemical and neuropharmacological measurements in situ in the living body.
abstract: Real-time monitoring of neurochemical dynamics in living neural tissue remains a critical challenge in neuropharmacology research, limiting our understanding of drug action mechanisms and therapeutic optimization. Traditional sampling methods lack the temporal resolution necessary to capture rapid neurotransmitter fluctuations and drug-target interactions occurring on millisecond timescales. We developed and validated a novel carbon fiber microelectrode array system coupled with fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) for simultaneous, high-frequency detection of dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine in freely-moving rodents. The biosensor platform achieved sub-second temporal resolution (100 Hz sampling) with nanomolar sensitivity and minimal tissue damage. We tested the system by monitoring real-time neurochemical responses to acute administration of cocaine (10 mg/kg, i.p.), fluoxetine (20 mg/kg, i.p.), and L-DOPA (100 mg/kg, i.p.) in the striatum and nucleus accumbens of awake rats (n=8 per group). Results demonstrated rapid dopamine transporter blockade within 2.3 ± 0.4 seconds of cocaine administration, with peak concentrations occurring at 47 ± 12 seconds. Fluoxetine selectively elevated serotonin levels with a delayed onset of 23 ± 6 seconds, while L-DOPA produced sustained dopamine elevation lasting 180 ± 25 minutes. Cross-correlation analysis revealed previously undetected interactions between monoamine systems during pharmacological perturbation. This technology enables unprecedented temporal resolution for studying neuropharmacological mechanisms in vivo, providing critical insights for rational drug design and personalized medicine approaches in neurological and psychiatric disorders.
journal: Translational research : the journal of laboratory and clinical medicine

title: Curcumin ameliorates reserpine-induced pain-depression dyad: behavioural, biochemical, neurochemical and molecular evidences.
abstract: Pain and depression frequently co-occur in clinical practice, sharing common neurobiological pathways involving monoaminergic dysfunction and neuroinflammation. Reserpine, a monoamine-depleting agent, has been shown to induce both nociceptive hypersensitivity and depressive-like behaviors in rodents, providing a valuable model for studying pain-depression comorbidity. Curcumin, the active constituent of turmeric, possesses anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and monoamine-modulating properties. The present study investigated the therapeutic potential of curcumin in ameliorating reserpine-induced pain-depression dyad in male Wistar rats. Animals received reserpine (1 mg/kg, s.c.) for three consecutive days, followed by curcumin treatment (25, 50, or 100 mg/kg, p.o.) for 14 days. Behavioral assessments included thermal hyperalgesia (hot plate test), mechanical allodynia (von Frey filaments), and depressive-like behaviors (forced swim test, sucrose preference). Biochemical analyses measured oxidative stress markers, pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), and monoamine levels (5-HT, DA, NE) in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Molecular studies examined BDNF, p-CREB, and NF-κB expression. Reserpine administration induced significant thermal hyperalgesia, mechanical allodynia, increased immobility time, and anhedonia. Curcumin dose-dependently reversed these behavioral alterations. Biochemically, curcumin restored depleted monoamine levels, reduced oxidative stress markers and pro-inflammatory cytokines, while upregulating BDNF and p-CREB expression and suppressing NF-κB activation. These findings suggest that curcumin effectively ameliorates reserpine-induced pain-depression comorbidity through multiple mechanisms involving monoaminergic restoration, anti-inflammatory actions, and neuroprotective effects, supporting its potential as a therapeutic intervention for pain-depression dyad.

title: Closed-loop Neuropharmacology for Epilepsy: Distant Dream or Future Reality?
abstract:
Epilepsy affects over 65 million people worldwide, with approximately 30% of patients experiencing drug-resistant seizures despite optimal medical management. Traditional antiepileptic drug therapy relies on continuous systemic administration, often resulting in subtherapeutic or toxic levels and significant side effects. Closed-loop neuropharmacology represents a paradigm shift toward precision medicine, combining real-time seizure detection with automated, localized drug delivery. This review examines current technological advances and clinical feasibility of closed-loop systems for epilepsy management.

We conducted a comprehensive analysis of recent developments in seizure prediction algorithms, implantable drug delivery platforms, and biocompatible reservoir systems. Key studies from 2015-2019 were evaluated, including preclinical models and early-phase clinical trials utilizing responsive neurostimulation data, microfluidic drug delivery devices, and machine learning-based seizure forecasting.

Current closed-loop systems demonstrate 70-85% accuracy in seizure detection within 10-30 seconds of onset, with response latencies of 2-5 seconds for drug delivery. Microinfusion pumps can deliver precise drug volumes (nanoliters to microliters) directly to epileptogenic zones, achieving local concentrations 10-100 times higher than systemic administration while minimizing off-target effects. Battery life and biocompatibility remain technical challenges, with current devices requiring replacement every 3-5 years. Clinical pilot studies show 40-60% seizure reduction in drug-resistant patients with minimal systemic side effects.

Closed-loop neuropharmacology for epilepsy represents a promising therapeutic frontier that addresses limitations of conventional treatment. While technical hurdles remain, rapid advances in biosensors, drug delivery systems, and artificial intelligence suggest clinical implementation within the next decade is increasingly realistic rather than aspirational.

title: Comparative analysis of inhibitory effects of caged ligands for the NMDA receptor.
abstract: Caged ligands have emerged as powerful tools for studying fast synaptic transmission, yet their potential inhibitory effects on N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors remain poorly characterized. We systematically compared the inhibitory properties of six commonly used caged compounds - caged glutamate (CNB-glutamate), caged glycine (CNB-glycine), caged GABA (CNB-GABA), caged ATP (caged-ATP), caged calcium (NP-EGTA), and caged cyclic AMP (caged-cAMP) - on recombinant NMDA receptors expressed in Xenopus oocytes and native receptors in acute hippocampal slices. Electrophysiological recordings were performed using two-electrode voltage clamp in oocytes and whole-cell patch clamp in CA1 pyramidal neurons. NMDA-evoked currents were measured in the presence of varying concentrations (10-500 μM) of each caged compound before photolysis. CNB-glutamate and CNB-glycine exhibited the strongest inhibitory effects, reducing NMDA currents by 45±8% and 38±6% respectively at 100 μM concentrations (n=12-15 cells, p<0.001). Caged-ATP showed moderate inhibition (22±4%), while NP-EGTA, caged-cAMP, and CNB-GABA produced minimal effects (<10%). The inhibition was voltage-independent and non-competitive, suggesting binding to allosteric sites rather than the glutamate or glycine recognition sites. These inhibitory effects were completely reversible upon washout and were not observed after UV photolysis, confirming that intact caged compounds were responsible for the inhibition. Our findings demonstrate that caged ligands can significantly modulate NMDA receptor function independent of their photolytic products, with important implications for experimental design and data interpretation in studies employing flash photolysis techniques.

title: The neuropharmacology of sleep paralysis hallucinations: serotonin 2A activation and a novel therapeutic drug.
abstract: Sleep paralysis (SP) is a parasomnia characterized by temporary inability to move accompanied by vivid and often terrifying hallucinations, affecting 7-8% of the general population. While the cholinergic mechanisms underlying SP atonia are well-established, the neuropharmacological basis of SP hallucinations remains poorly understood. Recent evidence suggests involvement of serotonergic pathways, particularly 5-HT2A receptors, which mediate hallucinogenic effects of psychedelics. This study investigated the role of 5-HT2A receptor activation in SP hallucinations and evaluated a novel therapeutic intervention. We administered the 5-HT2A agonist 25I-NBOMe (0.3 mg/kg) to sleep-deprived C57BL/6 mice during REM sleep rebound periods and measured behavioral correlates of hallucination-like states using the head-twitch response (HTR) paradigm modified for paralyzed animals. SP episodes were induced using the REM sleep fragmentation protocol combined with physostigmine administration. 5-HT2A activation significantly increased HTR frequency during SP episodes (p<0.001) and enhanced c-Fos expression in visual and auditory cortices. The selective 5-HT2A antagonist MDL 11939 (2 mg/kg) reduced both HTR responses and hallucination-associated cortical activity by 68% compared to vehicle controls. Chronic MDL 11939 treatment over 14 days decreased SP episode frequency from 4.2±0.8 to 1.6±0.4 episodes per week in a clinical pilot study of 24 recurrent SP patients. These findings demonstrate that 5-HT2A receptor hyperactivation contributes to SP hallucinations and suggest that selective 5-HT2A antagonists may represent a promising therapeutic approach for managing this distressing parasomnia.

title: Utilizing resources of neuropsychopharmacology to address the opioid overdose crisis.
abstract: The ongoing opioid overdose crisis represents a critical public health emergency requiring immediate implementation of evidence-based neuropsychopharmacological interventions. Current approaches remain insufficient to address the scope of overdose mortality, necessitating comprehensive utilization of existing pharmacological tools and development of novel therapeutic strategies. This review synthesizes current neuropsychopharmacological resources and proposes a multi-pronged approach to overdose prevention and treatment. We conducted a systematic analysis of FDA-approved medications, emerging compounds, and harm reduction strategies with demonstrated efficacy in opioid use disorder and overdose prevention. Key interventions include expanded naloxone distribution programs, evidence-based medication-assisted treatment (MAT) utilizing methadone, buprenorphine, and naltrexone, and implementation of novel alpha-2 adrenergic agonists for withdrawal management. Emerging approaches include partial opioid receptor modulators, abuse-deterrent formulations, and combination therapies targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems. Analysis of epidemiological data demonstrates that regions with comprehensive MAT implementation show 25-40% reductions in overdose mortality. Naloxone distribution programs have prevented an estimated 26,463 deaths between 2013-2017. Novel formulations combining buprenorphine with abuse-deterrent technologies show promise in reducing diversion while maintaining therapeutic efficacy. Integration of neuropsychopharmacological resources through coordinated clinical protocols, expanded access to evidence-based treatments, and continued research into novel therapeutic targets represents the most viable approach to mitigating the opioid crisis. Immediate policy implementation supporting these interventions is essential to prevent continued escalation of overdose deaths.

title: Effects of triiodothyronine and fluoxetine on 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptor activity in rat brain: regional differences.
abstract: Thyroid hormones have been implicated in the modulation of serotonergic neurotransmission and may enhance the therapeutic efficacy of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in treating depression. However, the regional specificity of thyroid hormone effects on serotonin autoreceptor function remains poorly understood. We investigated the combined effects of triiodothyronine (T3) and fluoxetine on 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptor activity across different brain regions in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received daily injections of T3 (25 μg/kg, i.p.), fluoxetine (10 mg/kg, i.p.), or their combination for 14 days. Autoreceptor function was assessed using in vivo microdialysis coupled with local perfusion of selective agonists 8-OH-DPAT (5-HT1A) and CP-93129 (5-HT1B) in the dorsal raphe nucleus, hippocampus, and frontal cortex. Extracellular 5-HT levels were measured by HPLC with electrochemical detection. Fluoxetine alone produced modest desensitization of 5-HT1A autoreceptors in all regions examined, while 5-HT1B autoreceptor sensitivity remained unchanged. T3 monotherapy had minimal effects on autoreceptor function. However, combined T3 and fluoxetine treatment resulted in marked desensitization of both 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptors, with the most pronounced effects observed in the dorsal raphe nucleus and frontal cortex compared to hippocampus. The regional heterogeneity in autoreceptor desensitization correlated with differential expression of thyroid hormone receptors. These findings suggest that T3 enhances fluoxetine-induced autoreceptor desensitization in a region-specific manner, providing mechanistic insight into thyroid hormone augmentation strategies in antidepressant therapy.

title: CSF neurochemicals during tryptophan depletion in individuals with remitted depression and healthy controls.
abstract: Tryptophan depletion has been shown to induce depressive relapse in individuals with remitted major depression, suggesting persistent alterations in serotonergic function. However, the neurochemical mechanisms underlying this vulnerability remain poorly understood. This study examined cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) concentrations of serotonin metabolites and related neurotransmitter systems during acute tryptophan depletion in remitted depressed patients compared to healthy controls. Twenty-four medication-free individuals with remitted major depression (≥6 months symptom-free) and 22 matched healthy controls underwent lumbar puncture before and 7 hours after consuming either a tryptophan-depleting amino acid mixture or balanced placebo mixture in a randomized, double-blind, crossover design. CSF samples were analyzed for 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), homovanillic acid (HVA), and 3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylglycol (MHPG). Mood ratings were obtained using the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale. Tryptophan depletion significantly reduced CSF 5-HIAA concentrations in both groups, but the reduction was 40% greater in remitted patients compared to controls (p=0.012). This differential response correlated with transient mood deterioration (r=0.48, p=0.019). CSF HVA and MHPG levels remained unchanged following depletion in both groups. No significant changes occurred during placebo sessions. These findings demonstrate that individuals with remitted depression exhibit heightened sensitivity to tryptophan depletion at the central serotonergic level, providing neurochemical evidence for persistent alterations in serotonin metabolism that may confer vulnerability to depressive relapse. The results support the serotonin hypothesis of depression and suggest that CSF 5-HIAA response to tryptophan depletion may serve as a biomarker for depression susceptibility.
journal: European neuropsychopharmacology : the journal of the European College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: The role of 5-HT₁A receptors in fish oil-mediated increased BDNF expression in the rat hippocampus and cortex: a possible antidepressant mechanism.
abstract: Omega-3 fatty acids have demonstrated antidepressant-like effects in both clinical and preclinical studies, yet the underlying neurobiological mechanisms remain poorly understood. Brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) upregulation and serotonergic system modulation have been proposed as potential mediators of these effects. This study investigated whether fish oil-induced increases in hippocampal and cortical BDNF expression are mediated through 5-HT₁A receptor signaling pathways. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received daily oral administration of fish oil (300 mg/kg) or vehicle for 21 days. To examine 5-HT₁A receptor involvement, separate groups received co-treatment with the selective 5-HT₁A antagonist WAY-100635 (0.5 mg/kg, s.c.). BDNF mRNA and protein levels were quantified using qRT-PCR and Western blot analysis in hippocampal and prefrontal cortical tissues. Immunohistochemical analysis was performed to assess regional BDNF distribution. Fish oil treatment significantly increased BDNF mRNA expression in the hippocampus (2.3-fold, p<0.01) and prefrontal cortex (1.8-fold, p<0.05) compared to controls. Corresponding increases in BDNF protein levels were observed in both regions. Co-administration of WAY-100635 completely abolished fish oil-induced BDNF upregulation in the hippocampus and partially attenuated the response in the prefrontal cortex. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed that fish oil enhanced BDNF immunoreactivity primarily in CA1 and CA3 pyramidal neurons and dentate gyrus granule cells. These findings suggest that 5-HT₁A receptors play a critical role in mediating fish oil-induced BDNF expression, providing novel mechanistic insight into the antidepressant properties of omega-3 fatty acids.

title: Getting a Handle on Neuropharmacology by Targeting Receptor-Associated Proteins.
abstract: Traditional neuropharmacological approaches have focused primarily on direct modulation of neurotransmitter receptors, yet mounting evidence suggests that receptor-associated proteins (RAPs) represent underexplored therapeutic targets with potentially superior selectivity and reduced side effects. Here, we investigated the druggability of RAPs using a comprehensive screen of 147 small molecules against key scaffolding and regulatory proteins associated with NMDA, AMPA, and GABA_A receptors in primary hippocampal neurons. We employed a combination of high-content imaging, electrophysiology, and proximity ligation assays to assess compound effects on receptor trafficking, synaptic localization, and functional modulation. Our screen identified 12 compounds that selectively disrupted specific protein-protein interactions without affecting receptor pharmacology directly. Notably, compound SN-2847, which targets the PSD-95/NMDA receptor interaction, produced antidepressant-like effects in behavioral assays while avoiding the psychotomimetic side effects associated with direct NMDA receptor antagonists. Mechanistic studies revealed that SN-2847 enhanced AMPA receptor surface expression through compensatory trafficking mechanisms. Similarly, compound GB-1203, targeting gephyrin-GABA_A receptor clustering, demonstrated anxiolytic properties with improved therapeutic windows compared to benzodiazepines. Proteomic analysis confirmed target specificity and revealed minimal off-target effects. These findings establish proof-of-principle that pharmacological targeting of receptor-associated proteins can achieve therapeutic efficacy with enhanced selectivity, opening new avenues for neuropsychiatric drug development. Our approach provides a framework for systematic exploration of the "RAP-ome" as a source of next-generation neuropharmacological targets.

title: Neuroethics and psychiatry.
abstract: The rapid advancement of neuroscience technologies and their clinical applications in psychiatry has generated unprecedented ethical challenges that require systematic examination. This review synthesizes current literature on neuroethical considerations in psychiatric practice, focusing on emerging diagnostic and therapeutic modalities. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of peer-reviewed publications from 2000-2008, examining ethical frameworks applied to neuroimaging-based diagnosis, deep brain stimulation, transcranial magnetic stimulation, and pharmacogenomic approaches in psychiatric treatment. Key databases including PubMed, PsycINFO, and Cochrane Library were searched using terms related to neuroethics, psychiatric treatment, and moral considerations in neuroscience applications. Our analysis reveals five primary ethical domains: patient autonomy and informed consent in the context of altered mental states, privacy and confidentiality of neurobiological data, justice in access to expensive neurotechnologies, potential for coercion in involuntary treatment scenarios, and the fundamental question of personal identity when directly modulating brain function. Neuroimaging presents particular challenges regarding predictive information and its implications for insurance coverage and employment. Deep brain stimulation and other invasive procedures raise concerns about personality changes and authentic selfhood. The integration of pharmacogenomics introduces questions about genetic discrimination and therapeutic obligation. Current regulatory frameworks appear insufficient to address these evolving challenges. We conclude that psychiatry requires specialized neuroethical guidelines that balance therapeutic innovation with protection of vulnerable populations, emphasizing the need for interdisciplinary collaboration between clinicians, ethicists, and policymakers to establish comprehensive governance structures.

title: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptor redux: Discovery of accessories opens therapeutic vistas.
abstract: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) have long been considered promising therapeutic targets for neurological and psychiatric disorders, yet clinical translation has been hampered by limited selectivity and off-target effects. Recent structural and functional studies have revealed that nAChRs exist in dynamic complexes with auxiliary proteins that modulate their pharmacology, trafficking, and signaling properties. Here, we systematically characterized the composition and functional impact of nAChR-associated protein complexes in human brain tissue and heterologous systems using proximity labeling proteomics, electrophysiology, and pharmacological profiling. We identified 47 high-confidence nAChR-interacting proteins, including previously uncharacterized chaperones, scaffolding proteins, and regulatory enzymes. Notably, the auxiliary protein NACHO (neuronal acetylcholine receptor-associated protein) enhanced α7 nAChR surface expression 3.2-fold and shifted sensitivity to the positive allosteric modulator PNU-120596 by 15-fold in hippocampal neurons. Co-expression of the scaffolding protein PSD-95 with α4β2 nAChRs increased receptor clustering and altered desensitization kinetics, reducing IC50 values for novel selective antagonists by 8-fold. Viral-mediated knockdown of key auxiliary proteins in mouse prefrontal cortex significantly impaired working memory performance and altered nicotine reward behaviors. These findings demonstrate that nAChR auxiliary proteins represent unexplored pharmacological targets that could enable development of more selective therapeutic interventions. Our results suggest that targeting nAChR-accessory protein interactions, rather than the receptors themselves, may overcome historical limitations in nAChR-based drug discovery and open new therapeutic avenues for addiction, cognitive disorders, and neuropsychiatric diseases.

title: The nature of dopamine dysfunction in schizophrenia and what this means for treatment.
abstract: Dopamine dysfunction has long been implicated in schizophrenia pathophysiology, yet the precise nature of these alterations and their therapeutic implications remain incompletely understood. We conducted a comprehensive meta-analysis of neuroimaging studies examining dopamine function in schizophrenia patients and investigated the relationship between dopamine dysfunction patterns and treatment response. We analyzed data from 847 patients with schizophrenia and 623 healthy controls across 34 positron emission tomography and single-photon emission computed tomography studies measuring dopamine synthesis capacity, receptor availability, and synaptic dopamine levels in striatal and extrastriatal regions. Additionally, we examined treatment response data from 312 patients receiving antipsychotic medications over 12 weeks. Results demonstrated significant elevations in presynaptic dopamine synthesis capacity in the associative striatum (effect size = 0.79, p < 0.001) and reduced dopamine D1 receptor availability in prefrontal cortex (effect size = -0.65, p < 0.001) in patients versus controls. Dopamine dysfunction was most pronounced in treatment-resistant patients, who showed 23% greater striatal dopamine synthesis compared to treatment-responsive patients (p = 0.008). Prefrontal D1 receptor deficits correlated significantly with negative symptom severity (r = -0.42, p < 0.001) and cognitive impairment (r = 0.38, p = 0.003). These findings suggest schizophrenia involves a dual dopamine dysfunction pattern characterized by striatal hyperactivity and prefrontal hypoactivity. The magnitude of these alterations predicts treatment resistance and symptom domains, supporting the development of regionally-targeted therapeutic approaches that address both striatal dopamine excess and prefrontal dopamine deficiency in schizophrenia treatment.

title: Pathological laughing and crying : epidemiology, pathophysiology and treatment.
abstract: Pathological laughing and crying (PLC), also known as pseudobulbar affect, is a neuropsychiatric syndrome characterized by involuntary, inappropriate emotional outbursts that are incongruent with underlying mood. This condition significantly impacts quality of life in patients with various neurological disorders, yet remains underdiagnosed and undertreated. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of epidemiological studies, neuroimaging investigations, and clinical trials published between 1990-2007 to synthesize current understanding of PLC pathophysiology and therapeutic approaches. Epidemiological data indicate PLC prevalence ranges from 10-53% in stroke patients, 10-15% in traumatic brain injury, 23-49% in multiple sclerosis, and up to 95% in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Neuroanatomical studies demonstrate that disruption of cortico-pontine-cerebellar pathways, particularly involving the anterior limb of the internal capsule and basis pontis, underlies PLC pathophysiology. Lesions affecting serotonergic and glutamatergic neurotransmission appear critical in symptom development. Treatment efficacy has been demonstrated with tricyclic antidepressants, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, and combination dextromethorphan/quinidine therapy. Double-blind, placebo-controlled trials show significant symptom reduction with amitriptyline (50-75mg daily), citalopram (20-30mg daily), and dextromethorphan/quinidine (20/10mg twice daily). Response rates typically range from 60-85% across different therapeutic agents. Recognition of PLC as a distinct neurological entity with specific anatomical substrates and effective pharmacological treatments should encourage clinicians to screen for this treatable condition in at-risk neurological populations.

title: Astra Award Lecture. The psychopharmacology of 5-HT3 receptors.
abstract: The 5-HT3 receptor represents a unique member of the serotonin receptor family, distinguished by its ligand-gated ion channel structure rather than the G-protein coupling characteristic of other 5-HT receptor subtypes. Recent advances in molecular pharmacology have enabled detailed characterization of this receptor's distribution, function, and therapeutic potential. This review synthesizes current knowledge of 5-HT3 receptor psychopharmacology based on radioligand binding studies, electrophysiological recordings, and behavioral pharmacology in rodent models. 5-HT3 receptors demonstrate highest density in limbic structures including the hippocampus and amygdala, with moderate expression in cortical areas and brainstem nuclei involved in emesis and autonomic control. Selective antagonists such as ondansetron and tropisetron exhibit nanomolar affinity and demonstrate anxiolytic-like effects in conflict paradigms, reduce ethanol consumption in voluntary drinking models, and block chemotherapy-induced emesis in ferret studies. Conversely, 5-HT3 receptor agonists including 2-methyl-5-HT produce anxiogenic responses and facilitate dopamine release in mesolimbic pathways. Electrophysiological studies reveal that 5-HT3 activation produces rapid membrane depolarization through sodium and calcium influx, particularly in GABAergic interneurons. These findings suggest 5-HT3 receptors modulate anxiety, addiction, and emetic responses through distinct neural circuits. The development of highly selective 5-HT3 antagonists represents a promising therapeutic approach for anxiety disorders, substance abuse, and chemotherapy-induced nausea, warranting continued investigation of this receptor system's clinical potential.

title: Measurement of kinetically resolved vesicular dopamine uptake and efflux using rotating disk electrode voltammetry.
abstract: The measurement of vesicular dopamine dynamics has been limited by the temporal resolution of existing techniques, hampering our understanding of presynaptic mechanisms underlying dopaminergic neurotransmission. We developed a novel approach using rotating disk electrode voltammetry (RDEV) to achieve kinetically resolved measurements of vesicular dopamine uptake and efflux in isolated synaptic vesicle preparations from rat striatum. Carbon fiber microelectrodes were incorporated into a rotating disk configuration and operated at 2000 rpm to enhance mass transport, enabling detection of rapid concentration changes with sub-second temporal resolution. Vesicular preparations were incubated with varying dopamine concentrations (1-100 μM) in the presence and absence of reserpine (10 μM) or bafilomycin A1 (100 nM) to pharmacologically characterize vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) activity. RDEV measurements revealed biphasic dopamine uptake kinetics with a rapid initial phase (t1/2 = 2.3 ± 0.4 s) followed by a slower equilibration phase (t1/2 = 18.7 ± 3.2 s), consistent with VMAT2-mediated transport. Reserpine completely abolished uptake, while bafilomycin A1 reduced uptake by 89 ± 7%. Efflux measurements using weak bases (NH4Cl, methylamine) demonstrated rapid dopamine release with kinetics matching the collapse of vesicular pH gradients. The technique achieved detection limits of 50 nM dopamine with signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 20:1. This RDEV approach provides unprecedented temporal resolution for studying vesicular dopamine handling and offers a valuable tool for investigating presynaptic dysfunction in neurological disorders.

title: Neuropharmacology of memory consolidation and reconsolidation: Insights on central cholinergic mechanisms.
abstract: Memory consolidation and reconsolidation represent distinct neurobiological processes that transform labile memories into stable engrams and render retrieved memories susceptible to modification, respectively. While considerable evidence implicates cholinergic neurotransmission in memory formation, the specific roles of central cholinergic mechanisms in consolidation versus reconsolidation remain poorly understood. Here we investigated the differential contributions of muscarinic and nicotinic acetylcholine receptors to these memory processes using auditory fear conditioning in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received bilateral infusions of the muscarinic antagonist scopolamine (10 μg/side), the nicotinic antagonist mecamylamine (5 μg/side), or vehicle into the basolateral amygdala either immediately post-training (consolidation) or post-retrieval (reconsolidation). Long-term memory was assessed 24 hours later through freezing behavior measurements. Scopolamine administration impaired both consolidation and reconsolidation, reducing freezing responses by 65% and 58%, respectively, compared to vehicle controls (p<0.001). Conversely, mecamylamine selectively disrupted consolidation (47% reduction, p<0.01) while leaving reconsolidation intact. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed increased c-Fos expression in cholinergic neurons of the medial septal complex following both consolidation and reconsolidation phases. These findings demonstrate that muscarinic receptors are critically involved in both memory consolidation and reconsolidation, whereas nicotinic receptors appear specifically required for initial memory stabilization. Our results provide novel insights into the neurochemical basis of memory processing and suggest that cholinergic modulation of memory involves receptor subtype-specific mechanisms that differentially regulate distinct phases of memory formation and updating.

title: The future of acute care and prevention in headache.
abstract: Headache disorders represent a significant burden on healthcare systems worldwide, with current therapeutic approaches often providing suboptimal outcomes in acute management and prevention. This review examines emerging pharmacological strategies and technological innovations that may reshape headache treatment paradigms. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of recent clinical trials, experimental studies, and technological developments in headache medicine published between 2002-2007. Novel acute interventions include selective 5-HT1F receptor agonists showing efficacy without vasoconstriction, inhaled dihydroergotamine formulations for rapid onset, and targeted CGRP antagonists demonstrating promising results in migraine termination. Preventive strategies have expanded beyond traditional approaches to include botulinum toxin type A injections for chronic migraine, topiramate for episodic prevention, and emerging anti-epileptic drugs with dual mechanisms. Technological advances encompass transcranial magnetic stimulation devices, implantable occipital nerve stimulators for refractory cases, and telemedicine platforms enabling remote monitoring and early intervention. Pharmacogenomic approaches are beginning to personalize treatment selection based on individual metabolic profiles and drug responsiveness patterns. Early clinical data suggest that CGRP pathway modulation may provide breakthrough efficacy with improved tolerability profiles compared to current triptans and ergot derivatives. The integration of biomarker identification, personalized medicine approaches, and novel drug delivery systems represents a paradigm shift toward precision headache medicine. These developments promise to transform acute care through more effective, rapidly acting interventions and prevention through targeted, individualized prophylactic strategies, potentially reducing both attack frequency and healthcare utilization in headache populations.
journal: Neurological sciences : official journal of the Italian Neurological Society and of the Italian Society of Clinical Neurophysiology

title: New treatments for ocular hypertension.
abstract: Ocular hypertension remains a significant risk factor for the development of primary open-angle glaucoma, necessitating effective therapeutic interventions to reduce intraocular pressure (IOP). Current first-line treatments, including topical beta-blockers and prostaglandin analogs, demonstrate limitations in efficacy and tolerability in certain patient populations. This study investigated the ocular hypotensive effects of a novel dual-action compound, NK-347, which combines selective alpha-2 adrenergic agonism with nitric oxide donation properties. Twenty-four New Zealand white rabbits were randomized to receive topical NK-347 (0.1%, 0.2%, or 0.5%), timolol 0.5%, or vehicle control twice daily for 14 days. IOP measurements were obtained using Goldmann applanation tonometry at baseline and at 2, 4, 6, and 8 hours post-dosing on days 1, 7, and 14. Aqueous humor dynamics were assessed using fluorophotometry, and autonomic innervation of ciliary tissues was evaluated through immunohistochemistry for tyrosine hydroxylase and neuronal nitric oxide synthase. NK-347 0.2% and 0.5% demonstrated significant IOP reduction compared to vehicle (p<0.001), with maximal effects observed at 4 hours post-dosing (mean reduction: 6.2±1.4 mmHg and 8.1±1.8 mmHg, respectively). The compound enhanced aqueous humor outflow facility by 34% (p<0.01) and reduced aqueous humor production by 18% (p<0.05). Immunohistochemical analysis revealed preserved sympathetic and nitrergic innervation patterns. These findings suggest NK-347 represents a promising therapeutic approach for ocular hypertension management through dual mechanisms targeting both aqueous humor dynamics and vascular regulation.

title: The psychopharmacology of violence: making sensible decisions.
abstract: Violence represents a complex behavioral phenotype with significant public health implications, yet evidence-based psychopharmacological interventions remain limited and controversial. Current clinical practice often relies on off-label use of various psychotropic medications without clear mechanistic rationale or systematic evaluation of risk-benefit profiles. This review synthesizes existing literature on pharmacological approaches to violence reduction across psychiatric populations and examines neurobiological substrates underlying aggressive behavior to inform clinical decision-making. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of randomized controlled trials and observational studies published between 1990-2013 examining psychopharmacological interventions for violence and aggression. Studies were categorized by medication class, patient population, and outcome measures. Additionally, we reviewed neuroimaging and preclinical studies elucidating neurochemical pathways involved in aggressive behavior, particularly focusing on serotonergic, dopaminergic, and GABAergic systems. Evidence supports modest efficacy for several medication classes in reducing aggressive behavior, including atypical antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, though effect sizes remain small to moderate. Atypical antipsychotics showed the most consistent evidence across diverse populations, while anticonvulsants demonstrated particular utility in personality disorder patients. However, significant methodological limitations exist across studies, including heterogeneous outcome measures, short follow-up periods, and limited assessment of functional outcomes. The neurobiological basis for violence involves dysregulation of prefrontal-limbic circuits and multiple neurotransmitter systems. Clinical decision-making should integrate individual risk factors, comorbid psychiatric conditions, and medication side effect profiles. Future research requires standardized outcome measures and longer-term safety evaluations to establish evidence-based treatment algorithms.

title: A primer on confidence intervals in psychopharmacology.
abstract: The interpretation of clinical trial results in psychopharmacology has traditionally relied heavily on p-values and statistical significance testing, often leading to oversimplified binary conclusions about treatment efficacy. Confidence intervals (CIs) provide a more nuanced approach to data interpretation by estimating the range of plausible values for treatment effects, yet their application remains underutilized in psychiatric research. This primer examines the theoretical foundations and practical applications of confidence intervals in psychopharmacological studies. We reviewed methodological approaches for constructing and interpreting CIs across common study designs in psychopharmacology, including randomized controlled trials, meta-analyses, and dose-response studies. Using examples from recent antidepressant and antipsychotic trials, we demonstrate how CIs can enhance interpretation of treatment effects beyond traditional significance testing. Key applications include assessing clinical meaningfulness of observed differences, evaluating equivalence and non-inferiority in comparative effectiveness research, and improving transparency in meta-analytic syntheses. Our analysis reveals that CIs provide crucial information about precision of estimates and potential clinical significance that p-values alone cannot convey. For instance, a statistically non-significant result may still indicate clinically meaningful benefit when CI bounds encompass relevant effect sizes. Furthermore, CIs facilitate better communication of uncertainty to clinicians and patients, supporting evidence-based decision-making. We conclude that routine reporting and interpretation of confidence intervals alongside traditional statistical measures would substantially improve the quality and clinical utility of psychopharmacological research. Training programs should emphasize CI interpretation to enhance statistical literacy among psychiatric researchers and clinicians.

title: Neurobiological models of obsessive-compulsive disorder.
abstract: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) represents a prevalent psychiatric condition characterized by intrusive thoughts and repetitive behaviors, yet its underlying neurobiological mechanisms remain poorly understood. Recent advances in neuroimaging and pharmacological research have implicated dysfunction within specific brain circuits, particularly involving the orbitofrontal cortex, anterior cingulate cortex, and caudate nucleus. The present review synthesizes current neurobiological models of OCD based on clinical, pharmacological, and emerging neuroimaging evidence. We examined data from positron emission tomography studies, cerebrospinal fluid analyses, and treatment response patterns to selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) in OCD patients. Converging evidence supports a model in which hyperactivity within the orbitofrontal-subcortical circuit, specifically the orbitofrontal cortex-caudate-thalamic loop, underlies the pathophysiology of OCD. Neurochemical investigations reveal dysregulation of serotonergic transmission, with secondary involvement of dopaminergic and GABAergic systems. PET studies consistently demonstrate increased metabolic activity in the orbitofrontal cortex and caudate nucleus that normalizes following successful treatment with SSRIs or behavioral therapy. Additionally, pharmacological challenge studies with meta-chlorophenylpiperazine suggest heightened sensitivity of postsynaptic serotonin receptors in OCD patients. These findings support a neurobiological model wherein serotonergic dysfunction leads to disinhibition of the orbitofrontal-subcortical circuit, resulting in the repetitive thoughts and behaviors characteristic of OCD. This model provides a framework for understanding treatment mechanisms and developing novel therapeutic interventions targeting specific neurotransmitter systems and brain circuits in obsessive-compulsive disorder.

title: Noradrenergic dysfunction and the psychopharmacology of posttraumatic stress disorder.
abstract: Posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is characterized by dysregulation of multiple neurotransmitter systems, with growing evidence implicating noradrenergic hyperactivity in core symptom manifestation. While selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors remain first-line pharmacotherapy, their limited efficacy suggests involvement of additional neurochemical pathways. This study examined noradrenergic system alterations in PTSD and evaluated targeted pharmacological interventions. We conducted a comprehensive review of preclinical and clinical literature from 1990-2007, analyzing noradrenergic biomarkers, neuroimaging findings, and treatment response data. Additionally, we performed a 12-week open-label trial of prazosin, an α1-adrenergic antagonist, in 28 veterans with combat-related PTSD experiencing prominent hyperarousal symptoms and sleep disturbances. Literature analysis revealed consistent evidence of elevated norepinephrine levels in cerebrospinal fluid and plasma of PTSD patients, along with altered α2-adrenergic receptor sensitivity and enhanced noradrenergic responsivity to trauma-related stimuli. Neuroimaging studies demonstrated hyperactivation in noradrenergic projection areas including the amygdala and prefrontal cortex. In our clinical trial, prazosin treatment (mean dose 6.8±2.4 mg/day) resulted in significant improvements in Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale scores (baseline 78.3±12.1 vs. endpoint 52.7±15.8, p<0.001), with particularly robust effects on sleep quality and nightmare frequency. Treatment was well-tolerated with minimal adverse effects. These findings support a central role for noradrenergic dysfunction in PTSD pathophysiology and suggest that α1-adrenergic antagonism represents a promising therapeutic strategy, particularly for hyperarousal symptoms resistant to conventional serotonergic interventions.

title: Psychopharmacological treatment of schizophrenia: what do we have, and what could we get?
abstract: Current antipsychotic medications for schizophrenia primarily target dopamine D2 receptors, providing modest efficacy for positive symptoms but limited benefits for negative symptoms and cognitive deficits. This comprehensive review examines existing pharmacotherapeutic approaches and emerging therapeutic targets for schizophrenia treatment. We conducted a systematic analysis of published clinical trials, preclinical studies, and mechanistic investigations spanning 1990-2012, focusing on both established antipsychotics and novel compounds targeting alternative neurotransmitter systems. Current first- and second-generation antipsychotics demonstrate 60-70% response rates for positive symptoms but show minimal improvement in functional outcomes. Clozapine remains the gold standard for treatment-resistant cases, though its use is limited by serious adverse effects. Emerging approaches targeting glutamatergic hypofunction through NMDA receptor glycine transport inhibition (e.g., bitopertin) and mGluR2/3 agonism show promise in early trials. Cholinergic enhancement via α7 nicotinic receptor modulation demonstrates potential for cognitive improvement, while muscarinic M1/M4 receptor agonists offer antipsychotic efficacy with reduced extrapyramidal side effects. Additionally, compounds targeting histamine H3 receptors and trace amine-associated receptor 1 (TAAR1) represent novel therapeutic avenues. Despite these advances, effect sizes for cognitive enhancement remain modest, and functional improvement continues to lag behind symptom reduction. Future therapeutic development should prioritize multi-target approaches addressing the complex pathophysiology of schizophrenia, with particular emphasis on procognitive agents and treatments that meaningfully improve quality of life and social functioning.

title: Comparative Neuropharmacology and Pharmacokinetics of Methamphetamine and Its Thiophene Analog Methiopropamine in Rodents.
abstract: The escalating prevalence of novel psychoactive substances (NPS) has created significant challenges for both clinical toxicology and drug policy. Methiopropamine (MPA), a thiophene analog of methamphetamine (METH), emerged as a "legal high" alternative with limited pharmacological characterization. This study investigated the comparative neuropharmacological profiles and pharmacokinetic properties of METH and MPA in rodent models. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of METH or MPA (1, 3, or 10 mg/kg). Locomotor activity was monitored using automated activity chambers, while monoamine transporter interactions were assessed through in vitro binding assays and ex vivo neurochemical analyses. Pharmacokinetic parameters were determined using LC-MS/MS analysis of plasma and brain samples collected at multiple time points. Both compounds demonstrated dose-dependent hyperlocomotion, with MPA producing 65-70% of METH's peak stimulant effects. In vitro binding studies revealed MPA exhibited moderate affinity for dopamine (DAT) and norepinephrine transporters (NET) with Ki values of 245 ± 32 nM and 189 ± 28 nM, respectively, compared to METH (Ki = 125 ± 18 nM for DAT, 89 ± 15 nM for NET). MPA demonstrated slower brain penetration (Tmax = 45 min vs. 20 min for METH) but prolonged elimination half-life (t½ = 4.2 ± 0.6 h vs. 2.8 ± 0.4 h). These findings suggest MPA functions as a weaker but longer-acting psychostimulant compared to METH, providing crucial pharmacological data for forensic identification and clinical management of MPA intoxication cases.

title: Cholinergic effects on fear conditioning II: nicotinic and muscarinic modulations of atropine-induced disruption of the degraded contingency effect.
abstract: **Background and rationale:** Previous studies have demonstrated that systemic atropine administration disrupts the degraded contingency effect in auditory fear conditioning, suggesting that cholinergic signaling is critical for detecting predictive relationships between conditioned and unconditioned stimuli. However, the relative contributions of nicotinic and muscarinic receptor subtypes to this phenomenon remain unclear. **Objectives:** We investigated whether selective nicotinic or muscarinic receptor manipulations could modulate atropine-induced deficits in degraded contingency learning. **Methods:** Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received auditory fear conditioning under either paired or degraded contingency conditions (50% CS-US pairing probability with additional unpaired USs). Animals received systemic injections of atropine (50 mg/kg), nicotine (0.5 mg/kg), scopolamine (1.0 mg/kg), mecamylamine (3.0 mg/kg), or vehicle 30 minutes prior to training. Freezing responses were measured during CS presentations 24 hours post-training. **Results:** Vehicle-treated rats showed significantly reduced freezing to the CS under degraded versus paired conditions, confirming intact contingency detection. Atropine eliminated this discrimination, with degraded contingency animals showing freezing levels comparable to paired controls. Co-administration of nicotine partially rescued the degraded contingency effect in atropine-treated rats, while scopolamine alone replicated atropine's effects. Mecamylamine had no significant impact on contingency learning. **Conclusions:** These findings suggest that muscarinic receptors are primarily responsible for contingency detection in fear learning, while nicotinic receptor activation can partially compensate for broad cholinergic blockade. This supports a model where cholinergic modulation of attention and associative learning involves complementary muscarinic and nicotinic mechanisms.

title: Tobacco psychopharmacology and public health policy: it takes a community.
abstract: Public health policies targeting tobacco use have achieved significant population-level reductions in smoking prevalence, yet individual neurobiological mechanisms underlying nicotine dependence remain critical barriers to cessation success. This review synthesizes current psychopharmacological evidence with community-based intervention strategies to examine how multilevel approaches may optimize tobacco control outcomes. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of randomized controlled trials published between 2005-2010 examining nicotine replacement therapies, bupropion, and varenicline in community settings, alongside population-based studies evaluating policy interventions including taxation, smoke-free laws, and media campaigns. Neurobiological studies demonstrate that chronic nicotine exposure produces persistent alterations in dopaminergic and cholinergic signaling pathways, particularly within mesocorticolimbic reward circuits, contributing to sustained craving and relapse vulnerability. Pharmacotherapies targeting these systems show 1.5-3.0 fold increases in quit rates compared to placebo in clinical trials. However, real-world effectiveness varies substantially across populations, with lower success rates observed in socioeconomically disadvantaged communities. Community-wide interventions combining pharmacotherapy access with environmental policy changes demonstrate enhanced outcomes, with population quit rates increasing 15-25% beyond individual treatment approaches alone. Smoke-free policies amplify pharmacotherapy effectiveness by reducing environmental cue exposure and social reinforcement of smoking behavior. These findings suggest that optimal tobacco control requires integrated strategies addressing both neurobiological dependence mechanisms through evidence-based pharmacotherapies and environmental factors through comprehensive community-level policies. Future research should prioritize implementation science approaches examining how to effectively deploy combined psychopharmacological and policy interventions across diverse populations.

title: The psychopharmacology of Huntington disease.
abstract: Huntington disease (HD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by motor dysfunction, cognitive decline, and psychiatric manifestations that significantly impact patient quality of life. While no disease-modifying treatments exist, psychopharmacological interventions remain crucial for managing the complex neuropsychiatric symptoms. This comprehensive review examines current evidence for pharmacological management of psychiatric comorbidities in HD, including depression, anxiety, psychosis, and behavioral disturbances. We analyzed randomized controlled trials, observational studies, and case series published between 2000-2019, focusing on efficacy and safety profiles of psychotropic medications in HD populations. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, particularly sertraline and citalopram, demonstrate modest efficacy for depression with acceptable tolerability profiles. Atypical antipsychotics, especially olanzapine and quetiapine, show benefit for psychotic symptoms and agitation, though careful monitoring for extrapyramidal side effects is essential given underlying motor pathology. Benzodiazepines provide short-term anxiety relief but carry risks of cognitive impairment and falls. Novel approaches including lithium and antiepileptic drugs show preliminary promise for mood stabilization and neuroprotection, though larger controlled trials are needed. The unique pathophysiology of HD, involving progressive striatal degeneration and altered neurotransmitter systems, necessitates modified prescribing approaches compared to primary psychiatric disorders. Drug-drug interactions, altered pharmacokinetics in advanced disease stages, and heightened sensitivity to adverse effects require careful consideration. Future research should prioritize developing HD-specific treatment guidelines and investigating neuroprotective agents that may address both psychiatric symptoms and underlying disease progression.

title: Neuropharmacological modulation of cognitive deficits after brain damage.
abstract: Cognitive impairments following traumatic brain injury, stroke, and neurodegenerative diseases represent a major clinical challenge with limited therapeutic options. Recent advances in understanding the neurochemical basis of cognitive dysfunction have identified potential pharmacological targets for intervention. This review examines current evidence for neuropharmacological approaches to treating post-injury cognitive deficits, focusing on cholinergic, dopaminergic, and glutamatergic systems. We analyzed data from randomized controlled trials and case studies published between 1995-2004 involving patients with acquired brain injury. Cholinesterase inhibitors, particularly donepezil and rivastigmine, demonstrated modest but significant improvements in attention and memory function in 60-70% of patients with traumatic brain injury-related cognitive impairment. Dopaminergic agents including bromocriptine and methylphenidate showed efficacy in enhancing executive function and processing speed, with effect sizes ranging from 0.3-0.6. Modafinil emerged as a promising treatment for post-injury fatigue and attention deficits, with response rates approaching 65% in preliminary studies. AMPA receptor potentiators and NMDA receptor modulators showed encouraging results in animal models but remain in early-phase human trials. Combination therapies targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems demonstrated superior outcomes compared to monotherapy in limited studies. While current pharmacological interventions provide meaningful but moderate cognitive benefits, optimal treatment protocols remain undefined. Future research should focus on biomarker-guided personalized medicine approaches and novel neuroprotective strategies to maximize therapeutic potential in this vulnerable population.

title: Pharmacological treatment in behavioural medicine: the importance of neurochemistry, molecular biology and mechanistic hypotheses.
abstract: The integration of neurochemical and molecular biological principles into veterinary behavioural medicine has become increasingly important for developing rational therapeutic approaches to companion animal behavioural disorders. Traditional empirical treatment methods often lack mechanistic foundations, limiting their efficacy and predictability. This review examines the current understanding of neurotransmitter systems implicated in common behavioural pathologies and evaluates the therapeutic potential of targeted pharmacological interventions based on underlying neurobiological mechanisms. We analysed published literature from 1990-2000 regarding serotonergic, dopaminergic, and GABAergic systems in relation to anxiety, aggression, and compulsive disorders in dogs and cats. Particular attention was given to studies employing selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), tricyclic antidepressants, and anxiolytics with well-defined receptor binding profiles. Evidence indicates that dysfunction in serotonergic neurotransmission underlies many anxiety-related disorders, with SSRIs demonstrating superior efficacy compared to non-selective agents. Dopaminergic abnormalities appear central to stereotypic behaviours, while GABA receptor modulation shows promise for acute anxiety management. Molecular studies reveal species-specific differences in receptor expression and drug metabolism that significantly impact treatment outcomes. The application of mechanistic hypotheses based on neurochemical principles enables more precise therapeutic targeting and improved clinical outcomes. Future research should focus on developing species-specific pharmacokinetic models and identifying biomarkers for treatment response. This mechanistic approach represents a paradigm shift towards evidence-based behavioural pharmacotherapy in veterinary medicine.

title: Current state of the use of neuroimaging techniques to understand and alter appetite control in humans.
abstract: The neural mechanisms underlying appetite regulation have become increasingly accessible through advanced neuroimaging techniques, offering unprecedented insights into human feeding behavior and potential therapeutic targets. Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), positron emission tomography (PET), and single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) have emerged as powerful tools to investigate the complex interplay between homeostatic and hedonic appetite control systems. This review synthesizes recent evidence from neuroimaging studies examining appetite-regulating brain circuits and interventions designed to modulate these networks. Key findings demonstrate that the hypothalamus, insula, orbitofrontal cortex, and striatum show distinct activation patterns in response to food cues, nutritional states, and pharmacological interventions. Resting-state connectivity analyses reveal altered network dynamics in obesity and eating disorders, particularly involving default mode and salience networks.
