Recent intervention studies utilizing real-time fMRI neurofeedback, transcranial magnetic stimulation, and deep brain stimulation show promising results in modulating appetite-related brain activity. Pharmacological neuroimaging studies of GLP-1 agonists, naltrexone-bupropion combinations, and novel neuropeptide targets demonstrate measurable changes in central appetite circuits that correlate with clinical outcomes. However, significant methodological challenges remain, including standardization of hunger states, food stimuli, and analysis approaches across studies. Future directions include the development of personalized neuroimaging biomarkers for treatment selection and the integration of multimodal imaging approaches with metabolic measurements to better understand individual differences in appetite control mechanisms.
journal: Current opinion in clinical nutrition and metabolic care

title: Lobeline inhibits the neurochemical and behavioral effects of amphetamine.
abstract: Lobeline, an alkaloid derived from Lobelia inflata, has been shown to interact with neuronal nicotinic acetylcholine receptors and vesicular monoamine transporters. Given the structural similarities between lobeline and amphetamine, we investigated whether lobeline could modulate amphetamine's neurochemical and behavioral effects. Male Sprague-Dawley rats received intraperitoneal injections of lobeline (1, 3, or 10 mg/kg) 30 minutes prior to d-amphetamine (2 mg/kg). Extracellular dopamine levels in the nucleus accumbens were measured using in vivo microdialysis, while locomotor activity was assessed in automated activity chambers. Additionally, we examined lobeline's effects on amphetamine-induced stereotypy and conditioned place preference. Pretreatment with lobeline dose-dependently attenuated amphetamine-induced increases in accumbal dopamine release, with the highest dose reducing peak dopamine levels by 68% compared to amphetamine alone. Correspondingly, lobeline significantly inhibited amphetamine-induced hyperlocomotion and stereotypic behaviors in a dose-related manner. The 10 mg/kg dose of lobeline completely blocked the development of amphetamine-conditioned place preference. When administered alone, lobeline produced minimal effects on dopamine release or locomotor activity. These inhibitory effects were not attributable to general motor impairment, as lobeline did not affect rotarod performance or food-motivated behavior. Our findings suggest that lobeline acts as a functional antagonist of amphetamine's rewarding and stimulant properties, possibly through interference with vesicular dopamine storage or release mechanisms. These results support the potential therapeutic application of lobeline or related compounds in treating psychostimulant abuse disorders.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: Developmental exposure to a serotonin agonist produces subsequent behavioral and neurochemical changes in the adult male prairie vole.
abstract: Prairie voles (Microtus ochrogaster) exhibit complex social behaviors mediated by multiple neurotransmitter systems, including serotonin, which plays a critical role in social attachment and anxiety-related behaviors. Early developmental exposure to pharmacological agents can produce lasting alterations in adult behavior and neurochemistry. The present study examined whether neonatal exposure to the serotonin 1A receptor agonist 8-OH-DPAT affects adult behavioral phenotypes and serotonergic function in male prairie voles. Male pups received daily subcutaneous injections of 8-OH-DPAT (0.5 mg/kg) or saline vehicle from postnatal days 1-14. At 8-10 weeks of age, subjects underwent behavioral testing including elevated plus maze, partner preference, and allogrooming assays. Subsequently, brains were analyzed for serotonin and 5-HIAA concentrations in the prefrontal cortex, nucleus accumbens, and hypothalamus using HPLC. 8-OH-DPAT-treated males exhibited significantly increased anxiety-like behavior in the elevated plus maze, spending less time in open arms compared to controls (P < 0.01). These animals also demonstrated impaired partner preference formation and reduced allogrooming duration toward familiar partners. Neurochemical analysis revealed decreased serotonin concentrations in the prefrontal cortex (32% reduction, P < 0.05) and nucleus accumbens (28% reduction, P < 0.05) of treated animals, while 5-HIAA/serotonin ratios were elevated in all brain regions examined. These findings demonstrate that developmental serotonergic manipulation produces persistent alterations in adult social behavior and anxiety, accompanied by region-specific changes in serotonergic neurotransmission. Such developmental programming may contribute to individual variation in social behavioral phenotypes.

title: The engagement of brain cytochrome P450 in the metabolism of endogenous neuroactive substrates: a possible role in mental disorders.
abstract: Brain cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzymes have been increasingly recognized as key regulators of central nervous system homeostasis through their involvement in the metabolism of endogenous neuroactive compounds. While peripheral CYP function in drug metabolism is well-established, the role of brain-expressed CYP isoforms in neuropsychiatric pathophysiology remains poorly understood. This review synthesizes current evidence regarding brain CYP-mediated metabolism of endogenous substrates including neurosteroids, fatty acid derivatives, and biogenic amines, with particular emphasis on their potential contribution to mental disorders. We systematically analyzed published literature from 1995-2017 examining brain CYP expression, enzymatic activity, and substrate specificity using PubMed and Web of Science databases. Critical evaluation focused on CYP2D6, CYP2E1, CYP46A1, and CYP19A1 isoforms based on their neuronal expression patterns and substrate profiles. Evidence indicates that brain CYP enzymes metabolize cholesterol, arachidonic acid metabolites, neurosteroids such as pregnenolone and allopregnanolone, and catecholamine neurotransmitters. Dysregulation of these metabolic pathways correlates with altered levels of neuroactive metabolites in depression, schizophrenia, and anxiety disorders. CYP46A1-mediated cholesterol hydroxylation influences synaptic plasticity, while CYP19A1 aromatase activity affects local estrogen synthesis and neuroprotection. Genetic polymorphisms in brain-expressed CYP genes show associations with psychiatric phenotypes and treatment responses. These findings suggest that brain CYP enzymes represent previously underappreciated therapeutic targets for mental health disorders, warranting further investigation into their pharmacological modulation as novel treatment strategies.

title: An improved method for analyzing coenzyme Q homologues and multiple detection of rare biological samples.
abstract: Coenzyme Q homologues play critical roles in mitochondrial electron transport and neuroprotection, yet their analysis in rare biological samples such as cerebrospinal fluid and small brain tissue specimens remains technically challenging. Traditional HPLC methods suffer from poor sensitivity and require large sample volumes, limiting their utility in clinical neuroscience research. We developed an improved analytical method combining reversed-phase HPLC with electrochemical and UV detection systems to simultaneously quantify CoQ9, CoQ10, and their reduced forms (ubiquinols) in samples as small as 50 μL. The method utilizes a C18 column with methanol-ethanol gradient elution and post-column reduction with sodium borohydride to enhance detection sensitivity. Sample preparation involves protein precipitation with methanol followed by lipid extraction with hexane-isopropanol. We validated this approach using rat brain homogenates, human cerebrospinal fluid, and neuronal cell cultures. The detection limits were 0.5 pmol for CoQ10 and 0.8 pmol for CoQ9, representing a 10-fold improvement over conventional methods. Intra-assay and inter-assay coefficients of variation were below 8% and 12%, respectively. Recovery rates ranged from 87-94% across different sample types. The method successfully detected age-related changes in CoQ levels in mouse brain regions and demonstrated altered CoQ10/CoQ9 ratios in cerebrospinal fluid from patients with neurodegenerative disorders. This enhanced analytical approach provides researchers with a sensitive, reproducible tool for investigating coenzyme Q metabolism in limited clinical samples, facilitating studies of mitochondrial dysfunction in neurological diseases.

title: Laboratory of receptor pharmacology.
abstract: G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs) represent the largest class of membrane proteins targeted by therapeutic agents, yet the molecular mechanisms underlying ligand-receptor interactions and downstream signaling remain incompletely understood. We investigated the pharmacological properties and signaling pathways of dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors using a combination of radioligand binding assays, functional cAMP measurements, and calcium mobilization studies in HEK293 cells stably transfected with human receptor constructs. Saturation binding experiments with [³H]spiperone and [³H]ketanserin revealed Kd values of 0.89 ± 0.12 nM and 2.3 ± 0.4 nM for D2 and 5-HT2A receptors, respectively. Competition binding studies with a panel of 15 structurally diverse ligands demonstrated significant differences in binding affinity profiles between receptor subtypes. Functional assays showed that D2 receptor activation by quinpirole inhibited forskolin-stimulated cAMP accumulation with an EC50 of 45 ± 8 nM, while 5-HT2A receptor stimulation by DOI increased intracellular calcium with an EC50 of 127 ± 23 nM. Pre-treatment with pertussis toxin abolished D2-mediated cAMP inhibition, confirming Gi/o protein coupling. Interestingly, several atypical antipsychotic drugs exhibited partial agonist properties at both receptors with varying efficacy profiles. These findings provide detailed pharmacological characterization of D2 and 5-HT2A receptors and support the hypothesis that functional selectivity at these targets may contribute to the therapeutic profiles of antipsychotic medications. Our receptor pharmacology platform offers valuable tools for drug discovery and mechanistic studies of GPCR signaling.

title: Traditional Chinese medicine and Western psychopharmacology: building bridges.
abstract: Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) and Western psychopharmacology have historically operated under distinct theoretical frameworks, yet both systems target neuropsychiatric disorders through modulation of neurotransmitter systems. This review examines potential integration points between TCM herbal formulations and evidence-based Western psychopharmacological approaches. We conducted a systematic analysis of 127 peer-reviewed studies published between 2000-2012, focusing on TCM compounds with demonstrated effects on major neurotransmitter pathways including serotonergic, dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems. Particular attention was given to frequently prescribed TCM formulations for depression, anxiety, and cognitive disorders, including Gan Mai Da Zao Tang, An Shen Ding Zhi Wan, and Ding Zhi Wan. Analysis revealed that 68% of examined TCM formulations contained bioactive compounds with measurable effects on Western-defined neurotransmitter targets. Hypericum perforatum constituents showed significant serotonin reuptake inhibition comparable to conventional SSRIs, while Paeonia lactiflora demonstrated GABA receptor modulation similar to benzodiazepine mechanisms. Ginkgo biloba extracts exhibited acetylcholinesterase inhibition and neuroprotective properties relevant to cognitive enhancement. Notably, 43% of TCM formulations demonstrated multi-target activity, potentially offering advantages over single-target Western pharmaceuticals. However, standardization challenges, herb-drug interactions, and limited pharmacokinetic data remain significant barriers to integration. These findings suggest that systematic investigation of TCM compounds using Western pharmacological methodologies may identify novel therapeutic targets and combination strategies, potentially bridging traditional wisdom with contemporary neuropharmacological practice while maintaining rigorous safety and efficacy standards.

title: Role of GIRK channels on the noradrenergic transmission in vivo: an electrophysiological and neurochemical study on GIRK2 mutant mice.
abstract: G protein-coupled inwardly rectifying potassium (GIRK) channels play a crucial role in neuronal excitability and neurotransmitter release regulation. While GIRK2 subunits are abundantly expressed in noradrenergic neurons, their specific contribution to norepinephrine transmission remains poorly understood. This study investigated the functional role of GIRK2 channels in noradrenergic neurotransmission using GIRK2 knockout mice and their wild-type littermates. In vivo electrophysiological recordings were performed in the locus coeruleus (LC) of anesthetized mice to assess noradrenergic neuronal firing patterns and responses to α2-adrenoceptor stimulation. Microdialysis experiments measured extracellular norepinephrine levels in the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus under basal conditions and following pharmacological challenges. Additionally, high-performance liquid chromatography quantified tissue norepinephrine content in multiple brain regions. GIRK2 knockout mice exhibited significantly increased spontaneous firing rates of LC neurons (2.8 ± 0.3 Hz) compared to wild-types (1.9 ± 0.2 Hz, p<0.01). The inhibitory response to clonidine, an α2-adrenoceptor agonist, was markedly attenuated in knockout animals (32% vs 68% firing rate reduction, p<0.001). Microdialysis revealed elevated basal norepinephrine levels in both prefrontal cortex and hippocampus of GIRK2-deficient mice. Tissue norepinephrine content was reduced by 25-40% across examined brain regions in knockouts, suggesting compensatory downregulation. These findings demonstrate that GIRK2 channels are essential modulators of noradrenergic neurotransmission, critically involved in maintaining physiological firing patterns and enabling proper α2-adrenoceptor-mediated feedback inhibition in norepinephrine neurons.

title: Pharmacotherapy of mental illness--a historical analysis.
abstract: The treatment of mental illness has evolved dramatically over the past century, with major paradigm shifts occurring in our understanding of neurobiological mechanisms and therapeutic approaches. This historical analysis examines the development of psychopharmacological interventions from the pre-pharmacological era through contemporary practice. We conducted a comprehensive review of psychiatric treatment modalities from 1900-2000, analyzing primary literature, clinical trial data, and epidemiological studies to trace the evolution of mental health pharmacotherapy. The pre-1950 era was characterized by non-specific treatments including barbiturates, insulin coma therapy, and early electroconvulsive approaches. The discovery of chlorpromazine in 1952 marked the beginning of modern psychopharmacology, leading to the development of phenothiazines and the first effective antipsychotic medications. The 1950s-1960s witnessed the introduction of tricyclic antidepressants and benzodiazepines, fundamentally altering treatment approaches for mood and anxiety disorders. The 1980s-1990s brought selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and atypical antipsychotics, offering improved efficacy and tolerability profiles. Our analysis reveals three distinct phases: the empirical period (1900-1950), characterized by trial-and-error approaches; the serendipitous discovery phase (1950-1980), marked by accidental findings leading to major therapeutic breakthroughs; and the rational drug design era (1980-present), driven by advancing neurobiological understanding. This historical perspective demonstrates the critical importance of continued neuroscience research in developing more effective and targeted psychiatric medications, while highlighting persistent challenges in treatment-resistant conditions and the need for personalized therapeutic approaches.

title: The Texas Children's Medication Algorithm Project: revision of the algorithm for pharmacotherapy of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder.
abstract: **Background:** The Texas Children's Medication Algorithm Project (TCMAP) was developed to provide evidence-based pharmacotherapy guidelines for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) in children and adolescents. Since the original algorithm's publication in 2001, new medications have been approved and additional efficacy and safety data have emerged, necessitating algorithmic revision. **Objective:** To update the TCMAP ADHD algorithm incorporating recent clinical trial data and expert consensus recommendations. **Method:** A multidisciplinary panel of child psychiatrists, developmental pediatricians, and clinical researchers reviewed controlled trials of ADHD medications published between 2001-2006, focusing on stimulants, atomoxetine, and alpha-2 agonists. The panel utilized a modified Delphi consensus process to integrate new evidence with clinical expertise. Safety profiles, tolerability data, and real-world effectiveness studies were systematically evaluated. **Results:** The revised algorithm maintains stimulants as first-line treatment but incorporates atomoxetine as an alternative first-line option for patients with comorbid tic disorders, substance abuse concerns, or stimulant intolerance. Extended-release formulations are prioritized to improve adherence. The algorithm includes specific recommendations for preschool children, emphasizing lower starting doses and more frequent monitoring. Alpha-2 agonists are positioned as adjunctive therapy for patients with partial response to stimulants. Updated safety monitoring guidelines address cardiovascular screening and growth parameters. **Conclusions:** The revised TCMAP ADHD algorithm reflects current evidence-based practice, providing clinicians with updated guidance for medication selection, dosing strategies, and safety monitoring. Implementation of these guidelines may improve treatment outcomes and reduce practice variability in pediatric ADHD pharmacotherapy.
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry

title: CNS Targets for multi-functional drugs in the treatment of Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases.
abstract: The progressive neurodegenerative nature of Alzheimer's disease (AD) and Parkinson's disease (PD) involves multiple pathological mechanisms including cholinergic dysfunction, oxidative stress, protein aggregation, and neuroinflammation. Current monotherapeutic approaches provide limited clinical benefits, suggesting that multi-target drug strategies may offer superior therapeutic outcomes. This study investigated the potential CNS targets for developing multi-functional compounds that could simultaneously address multiple pathogenic pathways in both diseases. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of shared molecular targets between AD and PD using computational modeling and in vitro screening approaches. Primary neuronal cultures from transgenic mouse models expressing mutant APP/presenilin-1 (AD) and α-synuclein (PD) were treated with a library of 147 small molecules designed to interact with multiple targets including acetylcholinesterase, monoamine oxidase-B, glutamate receptors, and antioxidant pathways. Cell viability, neurite outgrowth, protein aggregation, and mitochondrial function were assessed. Results identified 23 compounds showing significant neuroprotective effects in both disease models. The most promising candidates demonstrated dual acetylcholinesterase/monoamine oxidase-B inhibition coupled with antioxidant properties and reduced β-amyloid and α-synuclein aggregation. Lead compound MT-7741 showed 67% reduction in protein aggregation, 45% improvement in mitochondrial function, and enhanced neurite density compared to controls. These findings support the feasibility of multi-functional drug approaches targeting shared pathogenic mechanisms in AD and PD, potentially offering more effective therapeutic strategies than current single-target interventions for these devastating neurodegenerative disorders.

title: Corticosterone urinalysis and nicotinic receptor modulation in rats.
abstract: Chronic stress and nicotine exposure produce complex neuroadaptations that are difficult to assess using invasive sampling methods. Non-invasive biomarkers would facilitate longitudinal studies of stress-nicotine interactions in rodent models. We developed and validated a urinalysis protocol for measuring corticosterone levels and examined the relationship between urinary corticosterone and nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) expression following chronic nicotine treatment. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were housed in metabolic cages and received either saline or nicotine (0.4 mg/kg, s.c.) twice daily for 14 days. Urine samples were collected every 48 hours, and corticosterone concentrations were determined using enzyme immunoassay. Following treatment, brain tissues were processed for α4β2 and α7 nAChR binding using [³H]-epibatidine and [¹²⁵I]-α-bungarotoxin autoradiography, respectively. Urinary corticosterone showed significant positive correlation with plasma levels (r=0.82, p<0.001) and exhibited circadian variation consistent with the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis rhythm. Chronic nicotine treatment elevated mean urinary corticosterone by 34% compared to controls (p<0.01). Nicotine-treated animals displayed upregulated α4β2 nAChR binding in cortical and hippocampal regions (25-40% increase, p<0.05) but unchanged α7 nAChR binding. Importantly, individual differences in urinary corticosterone levels significantly predicted the magnitude of α4β2 upregulation (r=0.71, p<0.01). These findings demonstrate that urinalysis provides a reliable, non-invasive method for monitoring corticosterone in rats and reveals a novel relationship between stress hormone levels and nicotinic receptor plasticity that may inform addiction vulnerability mechanisms.

title: How human electrophysiology informs psychopharmacology: from bottom-up driven processing to top-down control.
abstract: Recent advances in human electrophysiology have provided unprecedented insights into the neural mechanisms underlying psychoactive drug effects, bridging the gap between preclinical models and clinical outcomes. Understanding how pharmacological interventions modulate both bottom-up sensory processing and top-down cognitive control represents a critical frontier in neuropsychopharmacology. We reviewed electrophysiological studies employing event-related potentials (ERPs) and oscillatory measures to characterize drug effects across multiple neurotransmitter systems. A comprehensive analysis of 127 studies published between 2005-2010 examined how psychoactive compounds alter early sensory components (P50, N100, P300) reflecting bottom-up processing, and later cognitive components (N200, P300, error-related negativity) indexing top-down control mechanisms. Results demonstrate that dopaminergic agents primarily enhance early sensory gating and P300 amplitude, while GABAergic compounds attenuate both early and late ERP components in a dose-dependent manner. Glutamatergic modulators, particularly NMDA antagonists, selectively impair mismatch negativity and gamma oscillations without affecting primary sensory responses. Cholinergic enhancement selectively improved top-down attentional control as indexed by enhanced P300 and reduced distractor-related N200. Importantly, individual differences in baseline electrophysiological measures predicted treatment response magnitude across multiple drug classes. These findings establish human electrophysiology as a translational bridge between molecular pharmacology and behavioral outcomes, providing mechanistic insights into bottom-up versus top-down drug effects. Such approaches may facilitate more precise medication selection and dosing strategies in psychiatric and neurological disorders by identifying electrophysiological biomarkers of drug response.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Pitfalls in presenting and interpreting clinical trial data.
abstract: Clinical trials in psychopharmacology have become increasingly complex, yet significant methodological and interpretive issues continue to compromise the validity of reported findings. This review examines common pitfalls encountered in the presentation and analysis of clinical trial data in neuropharmacological research. We analyzed 127 published clinical trials of psychotropic medications from 1990-1994, focusing on study design, statistical methodology, and data interpretation practices. Major deficiencies identified include inadequate sample size calculations (found in 43% of studies), inappropriate statistical analyses such as multiple comparisons without correction (38%), selective reporting of favorable outcomes (31%), and misinterpretation of statistical significance versus clinical significance (29%). Post-hoc analyses were frequently presented without appropriate caveats in 47% of trials. Dropout rates exceeding 30% were inadequately addressed in 52% of studies, with last-observation-carried-forward methods applied inappropriately. Placebo response rates varied dramatically (15-65%) across similar conditions, suggesting heterogeneous patient populations or study conduct issues. Adverse event reporting was inconsistent, with systematic collection methods described in only 34% of trials. Industry-sponsored studies showed significantly higher rates of positive primary outcomes compared to investigator-initiated trials (78% vs. 52%, p<0.01). These findings highlight critical areas requiring attention to enhance the reliability and clinical utility of psychopharmacological research. Standardized reporting guidelines, appropriate statistical consultation, and enhanced peer review processes are essential to address these systematic weaknesses in clinical trial methodology and interpretation.

title: Amino acid neurochemistry of the vertebrate retina.
abstract: The vertebrate retina contains a diverse array of amino acid neurotransmitters and neuromodulators that mediate synaptic transmission between photoreceptors, bipolar cells, horizontal cells, amacrine cells, and ganglion cells. While glutamate and GABA have been extensively characterized as the primary excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters respectively, the functional roles of glycine, taurine, aspartate, and other amino acids remain incompletely understood. We employed high-performance liquid chromatography, immunocytochemistry, and electrophysiological recordings to comprehensively map amino acid distribution and function across retinal layers in cat, rabbit, and goldfish preparations. Glutamate immunoreactivity was predominantly localized to photoreceptor terminals and ON-bipolar cells, with tissue concentrations reaching 8-12 mM in outer plexiform layer. GABA was concentrated in horizontal cells and a subset of amacrine cells, showing species-specific distribution patterns. Glycine demonstrated robust immunostaining in narrow-field amacrine cells and exhibited co-localization with GABA in 23% of GABAergic neurons. Taurine, present at unexpectedly high concentrations (15-20 mM), showed uniform distribution across all retinal layers with particular enrichment in Müller cells. Electrophysiological analysis revealed that glycine mediates crossover inhibition in OFF-pathway ganglion cells, while taurine modulates calcium homeostasis and exhibits neuroprotective properties under ischemic conditions. These findings demonstrate that amino acid neurotransmission in the vertebrate retina involves complex, overlapping systems that extend beyond classical glutamatergic and GABAergic pathways, suggesting novel therapeutic targets for retinal degenerative diseases.
journal: Progress in retinal and eye research

title: Analysis of neurotransmitter tissue content of Drosophila melanogaster in different life stages.
abstract: The neurotransmitter systems of Drosophila melanogaster undergo extensive remodeling during metamorphosis, yet comprehensive quantitative analysis across developmental stages remains limited. This study employed high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) and liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) to quantify dopamine, serotonin, octopamine, tyramine, and GABA content in whole-body extracts of D. melanogaster across four distinct life stages: third-instar larvae, pupae (48-hour), newly eclosed adults (0-24 hours), and mature adults (7-day). Twenty replicate samples of each stage were analyzed, with neurotransmitter content normalized to total protein concentration. Dopamine levels showed a biphasic pattern, decreasing 3.2-fold from larvae to pupae (p<0.001), then increasing 4.7-fold in newly eclosed adults before stabilizing in mature adults. Serotonin content remained relatively constant through larval and pupal stages but increased 2.8-fold in adults (p<0.001). Octopamine exhibited the most dramatic developmental regulation, with barely detectable levels in larvae and pupae, followed by a 15-fold increase in adults (p<0.001). Tyramine showed similar but less pronounced changes, increasing 6.2-fold from pupae to adults. GABA content gradually declined throughout development, decreasing by 40% from larvae to mature adults (p<0.05). These findings reveal stage-specific neurotransmitter profiles that likely reflect the distinct behavioral and physiological requirements of each developmental phase, providing a quantitative foundation for future studies of neurotransmitter function during Drosophila development and metamorphosis.

title: Effects of soman (pinacolyl methylphosphonofluoridate) on coronary blood flow and cardiac function in swine.
abstract: Organophosphorus compounds such as soman pose significant toxicological threats due to their irreversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase, leading to cholinergic crisis. While the neurological and respiratory effects of soman poisoning are well-documented, the direct cardiovascular consequences remain poorly characterized. This study examined the acute effects of soman exposure on coronary hemodynamics and myocardial function in an anesthetized swine model. Twelve Yorkshire swine (25-30 kg) were instrumented with coronary flow probes, left ventricular pressure catheters, and ECG electrodes under pentobarbital anesthesia. Animals received either soman (0.5 × LD50, n=8) or saline control (n=4) via intravenous injection. Coronary blood flow, left ventricular pressure, heart rate, and cardiac output were continuously monitored for 120 minutes post-exposure. Soman administration produced rapid onset of cholinergic signs including bradycardia, hypotension, and cardiac arrhythmias within 5-10 minutes. Coronary blood flow decreased significantly by 35 ± 8% (p<0.01) compared to baseline, while coronary vascular resistance increased 2.3-fold. Left ventricular contractility (dP/dt max) declined by 42 ± 12% (p<0.001), and cardiac output fell to 60% of control values. These hemodynamic changes preceded the onset of severe respiratory distress and were partially reversed by atropine pretreatment in additional animals. The results demonstrate that soman exerts direct cardiotoxic effects independent of respiratory compromise, suggesting that cardiovascular support should be considered as an essential component of organophosphate poisoning treatment protocols.
journal: Fundamental and applied toxicology : official journal of the Society of Toxicology

title: Mass spectrometry imaging, an emerging technology in neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry imaging (MALDI-MSI) represents a transformative analytical approach for investigating drug distribution and neurochemical changes in brain tissue with unprecedented spatial resolution. Traditional neuropsychopharmacological studies rely on tissue homogenization techniques that obscure regional drug localization and metabolite distribution patterns critical for understanding therapeutic mechanisms. This review synthesizes current applications of MALDI-MSI in neuropsychopharmacology research, examining methodological advances and their implications for drug discovery and development. We analyzed peer-reviewed publications from 2008-2014 utilizing MSI techniques for central nervous system drug studies, focusing on spatial resolution capabilities, quantitative accuracy, and biological relevance. MALDI-MSI enables direct detection and mapping of pharmaceuticals, neurotransmitters, and their metabolites at cellular-level resolution (10-50 μm) while preserving tissue architecture. Recent studies demonstrate successful imaging of antipsychotics, antidepressants, and anxiolytics across discrete brain regions, revealing heterogeneous distribution patterns not detectable through conventional methods. Quantitative improvements through internal standard incorporation and matrix optimization have enhanced analytical reliability, with detection limits reaching femtomolar concentrations for select compounds. Temporal pharmacokinetic studies show dynamic drug accumulation in target regions, providing mechanistic insights into therapeutic onset and duration. These findings establish MALDI-MSI as a powerful complement to traditional pharmacological approaches, offering unique capabilities for understanding drug action in complex neural networks. Integration of MSI technology with existing neuropsychopharmacological methodologies promises to accelerate drug development through improved target validation and biomarker identification, ultimately enhancing therapeutic strategies for psychiatric and neurological disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Two novel combined drug treatments for women with hypoactive sexual desire disorder.
abstract: Hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) affects approximately 10% of women and represents the most common form of female sexual dysfunction, yet effective pharmacological interventions remain limited. Current monotherapies targeting dopaminergic, noradrenergic, or melanocortin pathways have shown modest efficacy with significant side effects. This study investigated two novel combination therapies designed to enhance sexual motivation through synergistic neurotransmitter modulation. A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial was conducted in 180 premenopausal women diagnosed with HSDD. Participants were randomized to receive either bupropion (300mg/day) combined with buspirone (30mg/day), flibanserin (100mg/day) combined with low-dose testosterone gel (300μg/day), or placebo for 12 weeks. Primary outcomes included changes in sexual desire as measured by the Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI) desire domain and Sexual Desire Inventory-2 (SDI-2). Secondary measures assessed sexual satisfaction, distress levels, and adverse events. Both combination treatments demonstrated significantly greater improvements in sexual desire compared to placebo (p<0.001). The bupropion-buspirone combination increased mean FSFI desire scores by 2.4 points (95% CI: 1.8-3.0), while flibanserin-testosterone improved scores by 2.1 points (95% CI: 1.5-2.7). Sexual satisfaction improved in both active treatment groups with minimal adverse effects, primarily mild nausea and dizziness. These findings suggest that combination pharmacotherapies targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems may offer superior efficacy over monotherapy approaches for treating HSDD, warranting larger phase III trials to establish optimal dosing regimens and long-term safety profiles.

title: Chemogenetics drives paradigm change in the investigation of behavioral circuits and neural mechanisms underlying drug action.
abstract: The advent of chemogenetic tools has revolutionized the field of neuropharmacology by enabling precise temporal and spatial control over neuronal activity in behaving animals. Traditional approaches to studying drug action and behavioral circuits have relied heavily on pharmacological interventions and lesion studies, which often lack specificity and reversibility. Designer Receptors Exclusively Activated by Designer Drugs (DREADDs) represent a transformative methodology that allows researchers to selectively modulate genetically-defined neuronal populations with high temporal precision. This review examines how chemogenetic approaches have advanced our understanding of neural circuits underlying addiction, reward processing, and drug-seeking behaviors over the past decade. We analyze key studies demonstrating the application of both excitatory (hM3Dq) and inhibitory (hM4Di) DREADDs in dissecting the functional roles of specific brain regions and cell types in behavioral responses to drugs of abuse. Chemogenetic manipulation of dopaminergic neurons in the ventral tegmental area, GABAergic interneurons in the nucleus accumbens, and glutamatergic projections from the prefrontal cortex has provided unprecedented insights into the circuit-level mechanisms of addiction and relapse. Furthermore, the development of next-generation chemogenetic tools, including light-sensitive variants and improved selectivity profiles, continues to expand experimental possibilities. The integration of chemogenetics with advanced behavioral paradigms, electrophysiology, and neuroimaging techniques has established new standards for mechanistic studies in behavioral neuroscience. These technological advances promise to accelerate the translation of basic research findings into targeted therapeutic interventions for substance use disorders and other neuropsychiatric conditions.

title: Drugs used to treat Parkinson's disease, present status and future directions.
abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) affects approximately 1% of individuals over 65 years of age and represents one of the most prevalent neurodegenerative disorders worldwide. Current pharmacological interventions primarily target the dopaminergic deficit characteristic of PD, yet significant limitations remain in achieving optimal symptom control while minimizing adverse effects. This comprehensive review examines the current therapeutic armamentarium for PD management and evaluates emerging treatment paradigms. We systematically analyzed clinical efficacy data from randomized controlled trials published between 1990-2008 for established PD medications, including levodopa/carbidopa, dopamine agonists, monoamine oxidase-B inhibitors, and catechol-O-methyltransferase inhibitors. Additionally, we reviewed preclinical and early clinical data for novel therapeutic approaches targeting non-dopaminergic systems and neuroprotective strategies. Levodopa remains the gold standard for symptomatic treatment, providing superior motor benefit compared to dopamine agonists, though long-term use is associated with motor fluctuations and dyskinesias in 50-80% of patients within 5-10 years. Dopamine agonists demonstrate efficacy in early PD with reduced dyskinesia risk but increased psychiatric adverse effects. Emerging therapies show promise, including adenosine A2A receptor antagonists, glutamate modulators, and compounds targeting α-synuclein aggregation. Novel drug delivery systems, such as continuous dopaminergic stimulation via pumps and patches, may address motor fluctuations. Future PD therapeutics will likely emphasize combination approaches targeting multiple pathophysiological mechanisms, including neuroinflammation, mitochondrial dysfunction, and protein aggregation, potentially offering disease modification beyond current symptomatic treatments.

title: Experimental neuropsychopharmacology, yesterday, today and tomorrow. A conversation with Michel Hamon.
abstract: Experimental neuropsychopharmacology has undergone remarkable transformation over the past five decades, evolving from rudimentary behavioral observations to sophisticated molecular investigations. This retrospective analysis examines the field's historical trajectory, current methodological advances, and future directions through comprehensive review of seminal contributions and emerging paradigms. We conducted a systematic evaluation of neuropsychopharmacological research milestones spanning 1970-2020, analyzing publications, methodological innovations, and translational outcomes. Key developments were assessed across three temporal phases: classical pharmacological approaches (1970-1990), molecular revolution period (1990-2010), and contemporary integrative era (2010-present). Historical analysis revealed fundamental shifts from receptor binding studies and behavioral pharmacology toward multi-omics approaches, optogenetics, and precision medicine strategies. The integration of neuroimaging, electrophysiology, and genetic tools has enabled unprecedented mechanistic insights into drug action and neural circuit function. Contemporary methodologies including CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing, single-cell transcriptomics, and computational modeling have revolutionized target identification and validation processes. Current challenges include translational gaps between preclinical models and clinical efficacy, personalized treatment approaches, and ethical considerations in neuropharmacological research. Future directions emphasize systems-level understanding of brain-drug interactions, artificial intelligence-driven drug discovery, and biomarker-guided therapeutic development. The field's evolution demonstrates successful transition from empirical observations to mechanistically-informed drug development, positioning experimental neuropsychopharmacology as a cornerstone of modern psychiatric and neurological therapeutics. Continued integration of technological advances with rigorous experimental design will be essential for addressing unmet clinical needs in brain disorders.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of novel and selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists: WAY-181187 and WAY-208466.
abstract: The 5-HT6 receptor has emerged as a promising therapeutic target for cognitive enhancement and treatment of neuropsychiatric disorders. We characterized the neuropharmacological profiles of two novel selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists, WAY-181187 and WAY-208466, using in vitro binding assays, functional studies, and behavioral paradigms. Both compounds demonstrated high selectivity for 5-HT6 receptors with Ki values of 4.2 nM (WAY-181187) and 2.8 nM (WAY-208466) and >100-fold selectivity over other serotonin receptor subtypes. Functional assays using HEK293 cells stably expressing human 5-HT6 receptors confirmed full agonist activity, with EC50 values of 8.1 nM and 5.3 nM for WAY-181187 and WAY-208466, respectively. Both compounds stimulated cAMP accumulation and showed excellent brain penetration following systemic administration in rats. In behavioral studies, acute administration of WAY-181187 (3-30 mg/kg, p.o.) and WAY-208466 (1-10 mg/kg, p.o.) dose-dependently enhanced performance in the novel object recognition task and reversed scopolamine-induced deficits in spatial working memory. The cognitive-enhancing effects were blocked by the selective 5-HT6 antagonist SB-271046, confirming receptor-mediated mechanisms. Neither compound produced significant effects on locomotor activity, anxiety-related behaviors, or feeding at cognitively active doses. These findings demonstrate that WAY-181187 and WAY-208466 are potent, selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists with procognitive properties, supporting the therapeutic potential of 5-HT6 receptor activation for treating cognitive impairments associated with Alzheimer's disease and schizophrenia.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: The roles of dopamine and serotonin in decision making: evidence from pharmacological experiments in humans.
abstract: Decision-making processes involve complex interactions between multiple neurotransmitter systems, with dopamine and serotonin playing particularly crucial roles. While dopamine has been implicated in reward prediction and reinforcement learning, serotonin's contribution to decision-making remains less well characterized. We investigated the distinct and interactive effects of dopaminergic and serotonergic manipulations on human decision-making using a double-blind, placebo-controlled, within-subjects pharmacological approach. Healthy volunteers (n=24) completed a probabilistic reversal learning task and temporal discounting paradigm under four conditions: placebo, the dopamine D2 receptor antagonist haloperidol (2mg), the selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor citalopram (20mg), and combined haloperidol-citalopram administration. Haloperidol significantly impaired participants' ability to adapt to reward contingency reversals and increased preference for immediate over delayed rewards, consistent with dopamine's role in reward prediction error signaling and temporal discounting. Citalopram enhanced punishment sensitivity and reduced impulsive choice behavior, supporting serotonin's involvement in behavioral inhibition and impulse control. Notably, combined administration revealed opposing effects: citalopram partially rescued haloperidol-induced deficits in reversal learning while haloperidol attenuated citalopram's effects on punishment sensitivity. These findings demonstrate dissociable roles for dopamine and serotonin in human decision-making, with dopamine primarily mediating reward-based learning and temporal preferences, while serotonin modulates response to aversive outcomes and impulsivity. The antagonistic interactions between these systems suggest that balanced dopaminergic-serotonergic function is critical for optimal decision-making, with implications for understanding psychiatric disorders characterized by decision-making abnormalities.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Antidepressant-related sexual dysfunction - perspectives from neuroimaging.
abstract: Antidepressant-induced sexual dysfunction (AISD) affects 30-70% of patients treated with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), yet the underlying neural mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study employed functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to examine neural activation patterns associated with sexual arousal in healthy controls versus patients experiencing AISD. Twenty-four healthy volunteers and eighteen patients with major depressive disorder experiencing AISD following chronic SSRI treatment (mean duration 8.3 ± 3.2 months) underwent fMRI scanning while viewing erotic visual stimuli. Blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signal changes were measured in regions of interest including the anterior cingulate cortex, insula, amygdala, hypothalamus, and genital sensory cortex. Subjective arousal ratings and physiological measures of sexual response were also collected. Compared to controls, AISD patients showed significantly reduced activation in the hypothalamus (p < 0.01), anterior cingulate cortex (p < 0.05), and right insula (p < 0.05) during erotic stimulus presentation. These neural differences correlated with decreased subjective arousal ratings (r = 0.68, p < 0.001) and diminished physiological responses. Notably, hypothalamic hypoactivation was most strongly associated with reported sexual dysfunction severity (r = -0.72, p < 0.001). These findings suggest that SSRI-induced alterations in serotonergic neurotransmission may disrupt normal hypothalamic and limbic processing of sexual stimuli, providing neurobiological evidence for the mechanisms underlying AISD. Understanding these neural substrates may inform the development of targeted therapeutic interventions for this prevalent and distressing side effect.

title: Neurobiology and neuroimmunology of Tourette's syndrome: an update.
abstract: Tourette's syndrome (TS) is a complex neuropsychiatric disorder characterized by chronic motor and vocal tics, with emerging evidence implicating both neurodevelopmental abnormalities and immune dysfunction in its pathogenesis. Recent neurobiological investigations have expanded our understanding of the cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) circuits underlying tic generation, while neuroimmunological studies suggest autoimmune mechanisms may contribute to symptom onset and exacerbation in susceptible individuals. We conducted a comprehensive review of recent neuroimaging, post-mortem, and immunological studies published between 1995-2003, analyzing data from over 2,400 TS patients and 1,800 controls across multiple research centers. Structural and functional neuroimaging studies consistently demonstrate altered dopaminergic neurotransmission within the caudate nucleus and putamen, with increased D2 receptor binding density correlating with tic severity (r=0.67, p<0.001). Post-mortem analyses reveal decreased interneuron populations in the striatum, particularly parvalbumin-positive GABAergic cells, suggesting disinhibition of motor programs. Immunological investigations demonstrate elevated anti-neuronal antibodies in 34% of TS patients versus 8% of controls (p<0.0001), with molecular mimicry between Group A streptococcal antigens and basal ganglia proteins. Cytokine profiling reveals increased IL-12 and TNF-α levels during symptom exacerbations. These findings support a multifactorial model wherein genetic susceptibility, environmental triggers, and immune dysregulation converge to disrupt CSTC circuit function. The identification of specific neuroimmune biomarkers may facilitate early diagnosis and guide development of targeted immunomodulatory therapies for TS management.
journal: Cellular and molecular life sciences : CMLS

title: PET Neurochemical Imaging Modes.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as a powerful tool for investigating neurochemical processes in vivo, offering unprecedented insights into neurotransmitter systems and their alterations in neurological and psychiatric disorders. This comprehensive review examines current PET neurochemical imaging approaches and their clinical applications. We analyzed literature from 2010-2015 encompassing major neurotransmitter systems including dopaminergic, serotonergic, cholinergic, and GABAergic pathways, evaluating radiotracer development, imaging protocols, and quantitative methodologies. Key dopaminergic tracers such as [11C]raclopride and [18F]DOPA continue to provide robust measures of receptor availability and presynaptic function, while newer compounds like [11C]PE2I offer improved specificity for dopamine transporter imaging. Serotonergic imaging has advanced significantly with [11C]DASB for serotonin transporter quantification and [18F]altanserin for 5-HT2A receptor studies. Cholinergic system evaluation benefits from [18F]fluorobenzovesamicol for vesicular acetylcholine transporter imaging, particularly valuable in dementia research. GABAergic imaging remains challenging, though [11C]flumazenil provides reliable benzodiazepine receptor measurements. Quantitative analysis methods have evolved from simple standardized uptake values to sophisticated kinetic modeling approaches using arterial input functions and reference tissue methods. Recent developments in partial volume correction and motion compensation have enhanced accuracy. PET neurochemical imaging continues to expand our understanding of brain chemistry in health and disease, with emerging tracers for glutamatergic, histaminergic, and cannabinoid systems promising further advances in translational neuroscience research and drug development.

title: Diagnosis and management of chronic daily headache.
abstract: Chronic daily headache (CDH) affects approximately 4-5% of the general population and represents a significant neurological burden with substantial impact on quality of life and healthcare utilization. The classification encompasses transformed migraine, chronic tension-type headache, new daily persistent headache, and hemicrania continua, each requiring distinct therapeutic approaches. This comprehensive review analyzes current diagnostic criteria and evidence-based management strategies for CDH syndromes. We systematically reviewed published literature from 1990-2009, focusing on randomized controlled trials and large observational studies examining diagnostic methodologies and therapeutic interventions. Clinical assessment should emphasize headache frequency, medication overuse patterns, and associated neurological symptoms to differentiate CDH subtypes. Neuroimaging is indicated when red flag symptoms are present or in cases of new-onset daily headache. Medication overuse headache, present in 30-50% of CDH patients, requires structured detoxification protocols. First-line preventive treatments include topiramate (50-100mg daily) and amitriptyline (25-75mg daily), with response rates of 40-60% in controlled trials. Botulinum toxin A shows emerging efficacy for chronic migraine, with significant headache reduction in 70% of patients. Behavioral interventions, including cognitive-behavioral therapy and biofeedback, provide additive benefits when combined with pharmacotherapy. Early recognition and appropriate management of CDH can substantially reduce headache frequency and improve functional outcomes. A multidisciplinary approach incorporating pharmacological and non-pharmacological strategies, with emphasis on medication overuse prevention, represents the optimal management paradigm for these challenging headache disorders.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of an atypical antipsychotic, NRA0562.
abstract: The therapeutic limitations of conventional antipsychotics and the need for improved atypical agents with reduced side effect profiles have prompted continued investigation of novel dopaminergic compounds. NRA0562, a benzisoxazole derivative, was evaluated for its neuropharmacological properties as a potential atypical antipsychotic agent. Radioligand binding studies were performed using rat brain membranes to determine receptor affinity profiles. Behavioral assays included apomorphine-induced climbing, haloperidol-induced catalepsy, and prepulse inhibition tests in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Extracellular dopamine and serotonin levels were measured using in vivo microdialysis in the nucleus accumbens and medial prefrontal cortex. NRA0562 demonstrated high affinity for dopamine D2 (Ki = 2.1 nM) and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors (Ki = 0.8 nM), with a 5-HT2A/D2 ratio of 0.38. Moderate affinity was observed for α1-adrenergic (Ki = 15.3 nM) and histamine H1 receptors (Ki = 21.7 nM). The compound effectively blocked apomorphine-induced climbing (ED50 = 0.24 mg/kg, p.o.) while producing minimal catalepsy at antipsychotic doses. NRA0562 significantly improved prepulse inhibition deficits induced by apomorphine (p < 0.01). Microdialysis studies revealed preferential increases in cortical dopamine release compared to striatal regions, with concurrent elevation of prefrontal serotonin levels. These findings suggest that NRA0562 exhibits a favorable neuropharmacological profile characteristic of atypical antipsychotics, with potential advantages in treating both positive and negative symptoms of schizophrenia while minimizing extrapyramidal side effects. Further preclinical and clinical evaluation is warranted.

title: Neuropharmacology of cognition and memory: a unifying theory of neuromodulator imbalance in psychiatry and amnesia.
abstract: The neurobiological mechanisms underlying cognitive dysfunction in psychiatric disorders and amnesia remain poorly understood, despite growing evidence that multiple neurotransmitter systems are involved. We propose a unifying theoretical framework suggesting that cognitive impairments across diverse conditions result from imbalances in key neuromodulatory systems rather than discrete neurotransmitter deficits. This hypothesis integrates emerging data on acetylcholine, dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin interactions in hippocampal and prefrontal circuits critical for memory formation and executive function. We systematically reviewed neurochemical, pharmacological, and neuroimaging studies published between 1990-2005 examining cognitive dysfunction in major depression, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and various amnesias. Converging evidence suggests that optimal cognitive performance requires precise ratios between cholinergic enhancement and dopaminergic modulation in the hippocampus, while prefrontal executive functions depend on balanced noradrenergic and serotonergic tone. Disruption of these neuromodulatory balances, rather than absolute neurotransmitter levels, appears to underlie memory consolidation deficits and attentional impairments observed across psychiatric conditions. In amnesia syndromes, similar imbalances occur secondary to structural damage affecting neuromodulatory projections. This framework predicts that therapeutic interventions targeting neuromodulatory ratios rather than individual neurotransmitter systems may prove more effective for cognitive enhancement. The model suggests novel combination therapies and provides testable hypotheses for future pharmacological studies aimed at restoring optimal neuromodulatory balance in cognitive disorders.

title: S(-)Propranolol as a discriminative stimulus and its comparison to the stimulus effects of cocaine in rats.
abstract: The β-adrenergic antagonist propranolol exists as two enantiomers with distinct pharmacological profiles, with S(-)propranolol demonstrating membrane-stabilizing properties independent of β-blockade. Previous studies suggest that propranolol's discriminative stimulus properties may involve non-adrenergic mechanisms. The present study investigated whether S(-)propranolol could serve as a discriminative stimulus in rats and examined potential cross-generalization with cocaine, given both compounds' membrane-stabilizing effects. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were trained to discriminate S(-)propranolol (10 mg/kg, IP) from saline in a two-lever operant conditioning paradigm using a FR10 schedule reinforced with food pellets. Following acquisition, generalization tests were conducted with various doses of S(-)propranolol (1-20 mg/kg), racemic propranolol (2.5-15 mg/kg), R(+)propranolol (5-20 mg/kg), and cocaine (2.5-20 mg/kg). Rats successfully acquired the S(-)propranolol discrimination within 45±8 sessions. Dose-response analysis revealed full generalization to the training dose occurred at 7.5-10 mg/kg S(-)propranolol (ED50 = 4.2 mg/kg). Racemic propranolol produced partial generalization (maximum 65% drug-lever responding), while R(+)propranolol showed minimal generalization (<25%). Notably, cocaine demonstrated significant cross-generalization with S(-)propranolol, producing dose-dependent increases in drug-lever responding (ED50 = 8.7 mg/kg) with maximum generalization of 78% at 15 mg/kg. These findings suggest that S(-)propranolol's discriminative stimulus effects are mediated by mechanisms distinct from β-adrenergic blockade and share common interoceptive properties with cocaine, likely involving sodium channel blockade or other membrane-stabilizing actions.

title: Targeted disruption of cocaine-activated nucleus accumbens neurons prevents context-specific sensitization.
abstract: The nucleus accumbens (NAc) plays a critical role in drug addiction, yet the specific neuronal populations mediating different aspects of cocaine-induced plasticity remain poorly defined. Context-specific sensitization, in which repeated cocaine exposure in a particular environment leads to enhanced locomotor responses upon re-exposure to that context, represents a key model of addiction-related learning. Here we used activity-dependent genetic targeting to selectively ablate cocaine-activated NAc neurons and examine their contribution to sensitization. We employed transgenic mice expressing diphtheria toxin receptor under control of the immediate early gene c-fos promoter, allowing targeted elimination of neurons activated during initial cocaine exposure. Mice received cocaine (15 mg/kg, i.p.) or saline in distinct environmental contexts for five consecutive days, followed by diphtheria toxin administration to ablate c-fos-expressing NAc neurons. After a 10-day withdrawal period, animals were challenged with cocaine in both the drug-paired and unpaired contexts. Control mice exhibited robust context-specific sensitization, displaying significantly enhanced locomotor activity when cocaine was administered in the previously drug-paired environment compared to the unpaired context (2.8-fold vs 1.4-fold increase over saline baseline, p<0.001). In contrast, mice with targeted ablation of cocaine-activated NAc neurons showed equivalent, non-sensitized responses in both contexts, with locomotor activity comparable to drug-naive animals. Importantly, acute cocaine responses remained intact, indicating preserved basic drug sensitivity. These findings demonstrate that NAc neurons activated during initial cocaine exposure form a stable cellular engram essential for context-dependent sensitization, providing direct evidence for ensemble-specific encoding of addiction-related memories.

title: [New directions in GABAergic pharmacology of anxiety and depression].
abstract: GABAergic dysfunction has been increasingly implicated in the pathophysiology of anxiety and depressive disorders, yet traditional benzodiazepine therapies present significant limitations including tolerance, dependence, and cognitive impairment. This review examines emerging therapeutic approaches targeting the GABAergic system through novel mechanisms. We analyzed preclinical and clinical data from 1995-2003 investigating selective GABA receptor subunit modulators, neurosteroid analogues, and GABA transporter inhibitors. Particular attention was given to compounds demonstrating anxiolytic or antidepressant properties without classical benzodiazepine side effects. Selective α2/α3 subunit-preferring compounds showed promising anxiolytic efficacy in animal models while producing minimal sedation and reduced tolerance liability compared to non-selective agents. Synthetic neurosteroids, particularly 3α-hydroxy ring A-reduced pregnane derivatives, demonstrated rapid-onset antidepressant effects in forced swim and learned helplessness paradigms, potentially mediated through positive allosteric modulation of GABAA receptors and HPA axis regulation. GABA transporter-1 selective inhibitors exhibited both anxiolytic and antidepressant properties in multiple preclinical models, suggesting therapeutic potential through enhanced GABAergic tone. Clinical pilot studies with several compounds confirmed preliminary efficacy signals, though larger controlled trials remain necessary. These findings indicate that subunit-selective GABAA receptor modulators, neurosteroid-based therapies, and GABA reuptake inhibitors represent promising new directions for treating anxiety and depression. Such approaches may overcome limitations of current GABAergic medications while exploiting the therapeutic potential of this crucial inhibitory neurotransmitter system.

title: Neuroglialpharmacology: white matter pathophysiologies and psychiatric treatments.
abstract: Emerging evidence suggests that white matter abnormalities and glial dysfunction play critical roles in the pathophysiology of major psychiatric disorders, yet current therapeutic approaches primarily target neuronal mechanisms. Recent neuroimaging studies have revealed widespread white matter microstructural alterations in schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and major depression, while post-mortem analyses demonstrate significant oligodendrocyte pathology and altered myelin gene expression. This review synthesizes current understanding of neuroglial pharmacology and its therapeutic implications for psychiatric treatment. We examined preclinical and clinical literature from 1995-2011 focusing on white matter pathophysiology, glial cell biology, and emerging therapeutic targets. Database searches identified 247 relevant studies examining oligodendrocyte function, myelin dynamics, and glial-neuronal interactions in psychiatric contexts. Converging evidence indicates that psychiatric medications exert significant effects on glial cells beyond their primary neuronal targets. Lithium promotes oligodendrocyte survival and myelin repair through GSK-3β inhibition, while quetiapine enhances oligodendrocyte progenitor cell differentiation. Antidepressants modulate microglial activation and promote remyelination via BDNF-dependent mechanisms. Novel therapeutic approaches targeting white matter pathology show promise, including clemastine for myelin repair and minocycline for microglial modulation. Importantly, genetic variants affecting oligodendrocyte function predict treatment response to conventional psychiatric medications. These findings support a paradigm shift toward "neuroglialpharmacology" that considers both neuronal and glial targets. Future psychiatric drug development should incorporate white matter endpoints and glial biomarkers to optimize therapeutic efficacy. Understanding neuroglial mechanisms may explain individual treatment responses and guide personalized psychiatric medicine approaches.

title: Love and attachment: the psychobiology of social bonding.
abstract: Social bonding represents a fundamental aspect of human behavior with significant implications for mental health and neuropsychiatric disorders. While the neurobiological mechanisms underlying attachment have been extensively studied in animal models, translational research examining the psychobiology of human social bonding remains limited. This review synthesizes current understanding of the neurochemical and neuroanatomical substrates of love and attachment, integrating findings from preclinical studies with emerging human neuroimaging data. We examined peer-reviewed literature from 1990-2008 focusing on the roles of oxytocin, vasopressin, dopamine, and endogenous opioids in pair bonding and attachment behaviors. Prairie vole studies demonstrate that oxytocin and vasopressin receptor expression in the nucleus accumbens and ventral pallidum critically regulate pair bond formation, while dopaminergic signaling in mesolimbic reward circuits mediates the reinforcing aspects of social contact. Human neuroimaging studies reveal activation of similar reward pathways during romantic attachment, including the ventral tegmental area, caudate nucleus, and anterior cingulate cortex. Oxytocin administration enhances trust, empathy, and social recognition in healthy volunteers, supporting its role in human bonding. Dysregulation of these systems may contribute to attachment disorders, depression, and autism spectrum disorders. The neurochemistry of love involves complex interactions between neuropeptide, monoamine, and opioid systems that evolved to promote selective social bonds essential for reproductive success and offspring care. Understanding these mechanisms provides insight into the biological basis of human relationships and offers potential therapeutic targets for disorders characterized by impaired social functioning.

title: Neurogenic bladder model for spinal cord injury: spinal cord microdialysis and chronic urodynamics.
abstract: Neurogenic bladder dysfunction represents a major clinical complication following spinal cord injury (SCI), yet the underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly understood. We developed a combined approach utilizing spinal cord microdialysis with chronic urodynamic monitoring to investigate neurotransmitter dynamics in a rat model of SCI-induced neurogenic bladder. Adult female Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) underwent complete T10 spinal cord transection, with sham-operated controls (n=12). Four weeks post-injury, microdialysis probes were implanted in the L6-S1 spinal cord segments, and chronic bladder catheters were placed for simultaneous urodynamic recordings. Extracellular levels of acetylcholine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and GABA were measured during spontaneous voiding cycles over 72-hour periods. SCI animals demonstrated characteristic neurogenic bladder patterns including detrusor hyperreflexia, reduced bladder capacity (2.1±0.3 mL vs 4.8±0.4 mL in controls, p<0.001), and increased voiding frequency. Microdialysis revealed significantly elevated acetylcholine release during detrusor contractions in SCI rats (284±41% above baseline) compared to controls (156±23%, p<0.01). Norepinephrine levels were markedly reduced in SCI animals (38±12% of control values, p<0.001), while GABA concentrations showed compensatory increases (167±28% of controls, p<0.05). Serotonin levels remained unchanged between groups. These findings demonstrate that SCI-induced neurogenic bladder involves complex alterations in spinal neurotransmitter balance, with enhanced cholinergic activity and impaired noradrenergic modulation. This experimental model provides a valuable platform for investigating pharmacological interventions targeting specific neurotransmitter systems in neurogenic bladder dysfunction.

title: Clinical practice with anti-dementia drugs: A revised (third) consensus statement from the British Association for Psychopharmacology.
abstract: The British Association for Psychopharmacology convened an expert panel to update evidence-based recommendations for anti-dementia drug prescribing, following significant developments in clinical practice since the previous 2011 consensus. A multidisciplinary group of clinicians and researchers systematically reviewed recent literature on cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) and memantine, alongside emerging evidence on combination therapies and long-term treatment strategies. The panel evaluated randomized controlled trials, real-world effectiveness studies, and safety data published between 2011-2017, with particular attention to patient selection criteria, treatment optimization, and discontinuation practices. Updated recommendations emphasize earlier initiation of cholinesterase inhibitors in mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease, with donepezil remaining first-line treatment due to superior tolerability and adherence. Memantine should be considered as add-on therapy in moderate disease or as monotherapy when cholinesterase inhibitors are contraindicated. New evidence supports continuing treatment into severe dementia stages, challenging previous recommendations for discontinuation. The panel identified improved cognitive and functional outcomes with optimized dosing regimens and highlighted the importance of regular monitoring for therapeutic response and adverse effects. Combination therapy with cholinesterase inhibitors plus memantine showed modest additional benefits in moderate-to-severe disease. Despite limited disease-modifying effects, current anti-dementia drugs provide clinically meaningful symptomatic benefits when appropriately prescribed. These revised guidelines emphasize individualized treatment approaches, incorporating patient preferences and comorbidities, while acknowledging the continued need for novel therapeutic approaches targeting underlying disease mechanisms in dementia.

title: Rats can learn a temporal task in a single session.
abstract: Temporal processing is fundamental to adaptive behavior, yet the neural mechanisms underlying rapid acquisition of timing tasks remain poorly understood. Previous studies have shown that rodents can learn to time intervals over multiple training sessions, but whether rats can acquire temporal discrimination within a single session and the neurochemical basis of such rapid learning has not been systematically investigated. Here, we examined the ability of male Wistar rats (n=24) to learn a peak-interval timing task within a single 90-minute session using a novel fixed-interval 20-second schedule with unpredictable probe trials. Additionally, we investigated the role of dopaminergic signaling by administering either saline or haloperidol (0.1 mg/kg, i.p.) 30 minutes prior to training. Behavioral performance was assessed using peak time, peak rate, and coefficient of variation measures. Results demonstrated that control rats rapidly acquired temporal control, showing significant peak timing behavior within the first 30 minutes of training (p<0.001). Peak times averaged 19.8±2.1 seconds by session end, with coefficient of variation decreasing from 0.45 to 0.23 across the session. Haloperidol-treated animals showed significantly impaired acquisition, with delayed emergence of temporal control and higher variability throughout training (p<0.01). These findings reveal that rats possess remarkable capacity for rapid temporal learning within a single session and highlight the critical role of dopaminergic neurotransmission in the acquisition of timing behavior. This rapid learning paradigm provides a valuable tool for investigating the neurobiological mechanisms of interval timing.

title: High temporal resolution for in vivo monitoring of neurotransmitters in awake epileptic rats using brain microdialysis and capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence detection.
abstract: Epilepsy is characterized by complex alterations in neurotransmitter dynamics during seizure events, yet conventional analytical methods lack the temporal resolution necessary to capture rapid neurochemical changes in vivo. We developed an integrated system combining brain microdialysis with capillary electrophoresis and laser-induced fluorescence detection (CE-LIF) to monitor glutamate, GABA, and glycine concentrations with unprecedented temporal resolution in awake, freely-moving epileptic rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with chronic hippocampal electrodes and microdialysis probes following pilocarpine-induced status epilepticus. Microdialysate samples were collected at 30-second intervals and analyzed using a custom-built CE-LIF system with 488-nm argon laser excitation and fluorescein isothiocyanate derivatization. The analytical system achieved detection limits of 0.8 nM for glutamate, 1.2 nM for GABA, and 0.6 nM for glycine, with separation completed within 2 minutes. During spontaneous seizures, glutamate concentrations increased 340±45% above baseline within 60 seconds of electrographic seizure onset, while GABA levels showed a delayed increase of 180±30% at 2-3 minutes post-ictal. Glycine remained relatively stable throughout seizure events. The glutamate/GABA ratio peaked at 4.2±0.8 during ictal periods compared to baseline values of 1.8±0.2. This high-resolution monitoring system provides critical insights into the temporal sequence of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitter release during epileptic seizures and represents a significant advancement in real-time neurochemical analysis for epilepsy research.

title: Three-dimensional patterning in biomedicine: Importance and applications in neuropharmacology.
abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) patterning technologies have emerged as transformative tools in neuropharmacology research, enabling precise spatial control over drug delivery and cellular microenvironments. Traditional two-dimensional culture systems inadequately recapitulate the complex architecture of neural tissues, limiting the translation of pharmacological findings to clinical applications. This review examines recent advances in 3D biomaterial patterning techniques and their applications in neuropharmacological studies. We analyzed peer-reviewed literature from 2010-2018 focusing on 3D printing, microfluidics, hydrogel patterning, and electrospinning approaches for neural drug delivery systems. Key applications include gradient-based drug screening platforms, spatially-controlled release systems for neuroprotective agents, and biomimetic blood-brain barrier models. 3D-printed polylactic acid scaffolds demonstrated sustained release of brain-derived neurotrophic factor over 28 days, while microfluidic devices enabled real-time monitoring of drug penetration across engineered neural barriers. Hydrogel-based systems showed superior cell viability (>85%) compared to conventional cultures (65%) when evaluating neuroprotective compounds. Electrospun nanofiber matrices facilitated directional axonal growth and localized delivery of anti-inflammatory drugs in spinal cord injury models. These technologies significantly improved drug screening accuracy, reduced animal testing requirements, and enhanced our understanding of neuropharmacological mechanisms. Future developments in 4D printing and smart biomaterials promise even greater precision in temporal drug release patterns. 3D patterning represents a paradigm shift in neuropharmacology, offering unprecedented control over drug-tissue interactions and accelerating the development of targeted neurotherapeutics.
journal: Journal of biomedical materials research. Part B, Applied biomaterials

title: Effect of short-term intake of high- and low-concentrations of sucrose solution on the neurochemistry of male and female mice.
abstract: Chronic high sucrose consumption has been extensively studied for its neurochemical effects, yet the acute impact of varying sucrose concentrations on brain neurotransmitter systems remains poorly characterized. This study investigated sex-specific neurochemical responses following short-term exposure to different sucrose concentrations in adult C57BL/6J mice. Male and female mice (n=12 per group) were randomly assigned to receive either high-concentration sucrose (20% w/v), low-concentration sucrose (5% w/v), or water for 7 days ad libitum. Brain tissue samples from the prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, and striatum were analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography to quantify dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, and their metabolites. Behavioral assessments included sucrose preference tests and locomotor activity measurements. High-concentration sucrose significantly increased dopamine levels in the striatum of both sexes (p<0.01), with males showing greater elevation than females (2.3-fold vs. 1.7-fold increase). Serotonin turnover in the prefrontal cortex was enhanced in female mice receiving high-concentration sucrose (p<0.05) but remained unchanged in males. Low-concentration sucrose produced minimal neurochemical alterations compared to controls. Locomotor activity increased dose-dependently with sucrose concentration, particularly in female subjects. These findings demonstrate that short-term sucrose exposure induces rapid, concentration-dependent neurochemical changes with notable sex differences in neurotransmitter responses. The differential sensitivity between males and females suggests distinct neural mechanisms underlying sugar reward processing, with implications for understanding sex-specific vulnerabilities to dietary sugar overconsumption and related metabolic disorders.

title: In-vivo brain dialysis of neurotransmitters.
abstract: Recent advances in microdialysis technology have provided unprecedented opportunities to monitor neurotransmitter release in the living brain with high temporal resolution.
