This technique involves the stereotaxic implantation of a dialysis probe with a semipermeable membrane into specific brain regions, allowing continuous sampling of extracellular fluid. We evaluated the efficacy of in-vivo microdialysis for measuring dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and acetylcholine release in the striatum, hippocampus, and prefrontal cortex of freely moving rats. Dialysis probes (2-4 mm membrane length, 20,000 Da molecular weight cutoff) were perfused with artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 1-2 μL/min, and samples were collected at 20-minute intervals for high-performance liquid chromatography analysis. Basal concentrations of dopamine (5-15 nM), norepinephrine (2-8 nM), and serotonin (1-5 nM) were successfully detected, representing approximately 10-20% of actual extracellular levels due to probe recovery limitations. Pharmacological validation experiments demonstrated that amphetamine (2 mg/kg, i.p.) increased striatal dopamine release by 400-600%, while haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg, i.p.) produced a delayed elevation consistent with autoreceptor blockade. Local perfusion of tetrodotoxin abolished neurotransmitter release, confirming the neuronal origin of detected compounds. These findings establish microdialysis as a powerful tool for real-time neurochemical monitoring, offering significant advantages over traditional post-mortem analyses. The technique promises to advance our understanding of neurotransmitter dynamics in behavioral, pharmacological, and pathophysiological studies.

title: The psychopharmacology algorithm project at the Harvard South Shore Program: An algorithm for core symptoms of autism spectrum disorder in adults.
abstract:
**Background:** Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) affects approximately 1-2% of adults, yet evidence-based psychopharmacological interventions for core symptoms remain limited. Current treatment approaches often rely on off-label use of medications targeting associated comorbidities rather than core ASD symptomatology. The Harvard South Shore Program developed a systematic algorithm to guide clinicians in medication selection for core ASD symptoms in adults.

**Methods:** We conducted a retrospective analysis of 147 adults with ASD (mean age 28.4 ± 8.2 years, 68% male) treated using our standardized algorithm from 2017-2019. The algorithm prioritized selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) for repetitive behaviors and social communication deficits, followed by atypical antipsychotics for severe behavioral symptoms, and adjunctive treatments including oxytocin and N-acetylcysteine. Primary outcomes were measured using the Social Responsiveness Scale-2 (SRS-2) and Repetitive Behavior Scale-Revised (RBS-R) at baseline, 12, and 24 weeks.

**Results:** Algorithm implementation resulted in significant improvements in SRS-2 total scores (baseline 76.3 ± 12.1 vs. 24 weeks 68.9 ± 14.2, p<0.001) and RBS-R scores (baseline 42.7 ± 18.3 vs. 24 weeks 35.1 ± 16.8, p=0.003). Sertraline was most frequently prescribed (n=89, 61%), followed by risperidone (n=34, 23%). Treatment response rates were 58% for social communication symptoms and 52% for repetitive behaviors. Discontinuation due to adverse effects occurred in 18% of participants.

**Conclusions:** A systematic psychopharmacological algorithm demonstrated measurable improvements in core ASD symptoms among adults. These findings support the potential efficacy of structured medication approaches, though randomized controlled trials are needed to establish definitive treatment guidelines.

title: Role of glutamate and dopamine receptors in the psychopharmacological profile of the indole alkaloid psychollatine.
abstract: The indole alkaloid psychollatine, isolated from Psychotria species, has demonstrated psychoactive properties in traditional ethnobotanical preparations, yet its mechanism of action remains poorly characterized. This study investigated the neuropharmacological profile of psychollatine with particular emphasis on glutamate and dopamine receptor interactions. Psychollatine was purified from Psychotria carthaginensis using standard chromatographic techniques. Receptor binding assays were performed using rat brain membrane preparations to evaluate affinity for NMDA, AMPA, kainate, D1, and D2 receptor subtypes. Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity, prepulse inhibition, and conditioned avoidance response in male Sprague-Dawley rats following intraperitoneal administration of psychollatine (1-30 mg/kg). Electrophysiological recordings from hippocampal slices examined effects on glutamatergic neurotransmission. Psychollatine exhibited moderate binding affinity for NMDA receptors (Ki = 8.2 μM) and D2 receptors (Ki = 12.4 μM), with minimal affinity for other receptor subtypes tested. Behaviorally, psychollatine (10-30 mg/kg) produced dose-dependent hyperlocomotion and disrupted prepulse inhibition, effects that were attenuated by co-administration of the D2 antagonist haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg) but not the NMDA antagonist MK-801. Electrophysiological studies revealed psychollatine significantly enhanced glutamate-induced excitatory postsynaptic potentials in CA1 pyramidal neurons. These findings suggest that psychollatine's psychoactive effects involve complex interactions between glutamatergic and dopaminergic systems, with primary behavioral effects mediated through dopamine D2 receptor activation. Further investigation of this novel indole alkaloid may provide insights into glutamate-dopamine interactions underlying psychosis-related behaviors.

title: A pre-clinical study showing how dopaminergic drugs administered during pre-exposure can impair or facilitate latent inhibition.
abstract: Latent inhibition (LI), the reduced conditioning to a stimulus following non-reinforced pre-exposure, is disrupted in schizophrenia and serves as a valuable model for studying attentional and associative learning deficits. While dopaminergic dysfunction has been implicated in schizophrenia pathophysiology, the specific role of dopamine receptor subtypes in LI remains poorly understood. This study examined how selective dopaminergic manipulations during pre-exposure affect LI acquisition in rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of saline, the D2/D3 agonist quinpirole (0.5 mg/kg), the D1/D5 agonist SKF-38393 (10 mg/kg), the D2 antagonist haloperidol (0.1 mg/kg), or the D1 antagonist SCH-23390 (0.05 mg/kg) 30 minutes before each of five tone pre-exposure sessions. Following a two-day interval, all animals underwent tone-shock conditioning and extinction testing. Vehicle-treated pre-exposed animals demonstrated robust LI compared to non-pre-exposed controls (p<0.01). Quinpirole administration during pre-exposure completely abolished LI (p<0.001), while SKF-38393 had no significant effect. Conversely, haloperidol enhanced LI magnitude (p<0.05), whereas SCH-23390 produced no change. D2-like receptor stimulation during pre-exposure impaired subsequent LI expression, while D2 blockade facilitated it. These findings suggest that D2-mediated dopaminergic activity during stimulus pre-exposure critically modulates attentional processing mechanisms underlying LI, supporting the hypothesis that excessive dopaminergic transmission may contribute to attentional deficits observed in schizophrenia through disruption of normal stimulus filtering processes.

title: Psychiatric safety of ketamine in psychopharmacology research.
abstract:
**Background:** Ketamine, a non-competitive NMDA receptor antagonist, has emerged as a valuable research tool in psychopharmacology for modeling psychotic symptoms and investigating glutamatergic dysfunction in psychiatric disorders. However, concerns regarding its psychiatric safety profile in research settings have limited its broader application. This study systematically evaluated the acute and subacute psychiatric effects of ketamine administration in healthy volunteers and psychiatric populations participating in research protocols.

**Methods:** We conducted a retrospective analysis of 847 ketamine administrations across 12 controlled studies (2003-2006) involving healthy controls (n=312) and patients with schizophrenia (n=89) or mood disorders (n=76). Subanesthetic doses ranged from 0.1-0.5 mg/kg intravenously. Psychiatric symptoms were assessed using the Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS) and Clinician Administered Dissociative States Scale (CADSS) at baseline, peak effect (40 minutes), and follow-up timepoints (24 hours, 1 week).

**Results:** Ketamine produced dose-dependent increases in BPRS positive symptoms (peak increase: 8.3±2.1 points) and CADSS scores (21.4±5.7) that returned to baseline within 2-4 hours. No persistent psychotic symptoms occurred beyond 24 hours in any participant. Psychiatric patients showed similar symptom trajectories but required closer monitoring. Three participants (0.35%) experienced significant anxiety requiring early termination, and one healthy volunteer developed transient panic symptoms. No hospitalizations or serious adverse psychiatric events occurred.

**Conclusions:** These findings support the psychiatric safety of subanesthetic ketamine in controlled research environments with appropriate screening and monitoring protocols. The predictable, time-limited nature of ketamine's psychiatric effects enables its continued use as a valuable neuropharmacological probe while maintaining acceptable risk-benefit ratios for research participants.

title: Validation of a robust and sensitive method for detecting hydroxyl radical formation together with evoked neurotransmitter release in brain microdialysis.
abstract: Microdialysis has become an essential technique for monitoring neurotransmitter release in vivo, but simultaneous detection of oxidative stress markers remains technically challenging. Hydroxyl radicals (•OH) are highly reactive oxygen species implicated in neurodegeneration, yet their detection alongside neurotransmitters requires methodological optimization due to their short half-life and low steady-state concentrations. We developed and validated a sensitive analytical approach combining high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) to simultaneously quantify •OH formation via 2,3-dihydroxybenzoic acid (2,3-DHBA) trapping and dopamine release in rat striatal microdialysates. The method employed salicylic acid as a molecular probe for •OH detection, which forms 2,3-DHBA upon hydroxylation. Validation experiments demonstrated linear responses for both 2,3-DHBA (0.5-50 nM, r²=0.998) and dopamine (1-100 nM, r²=0.997) with detection limits of 0.2 nM and 0.5 nM, respectively. Recovery rates through microdialysis probes were 18±2% for 2,3-DHBA and 22±3% for dopamine. In anesthetized rats, potassium-evoked depolarization (100 mM KCl) increased extracellular dopamine by 340±45% while simultaneously elevating 2,3-DHBA formation by 280±38% compared to baseline. Iron-catalyzed Fenton reactions were confirmed as the primary source of •OH generation using deferoxamine pretreatment, which reduced 2,3-DHBA formation by 75±8%. This validated methodology provides a robust tool for investigating the relationship between neurotransmitter release and oxidative stress in neurochemical research, particularly relevant for studies of neurodegeneration and neuroprotection.

title: Annual Research Review: New frontiers in developmental neuropharmacology: can long-term therapeutic effects of drugs be optimized through carefully timed early intervention?
abstract: Recent advances in developmental neuroscience suggest that early pharmacological interventions may have enduring effects on neural development that extend beyond immediate symptom relief. This review examines emerging evidence for critical periods in neurodevelopment where therapeutic drug administration could optimize long-term outcomes in pediatric psychiatric and neurological conditions. We conducted a comprehensive review of preclinical and clinical studies published between 2005-2011 investigating the developmental timing of psychoactive drug interventions, focusing on antidepressants, stimulants, antipsychotics, and mood stabilizers. Particular attention was given to studies examining neuroplasticity markers, synaptic development, and long-term behavioral outcomes following early versus delayed treatment initiation. Converging evidence from animal models demonstrates that pharmacological interventions during specific developmental windows can permanently alter neural circuitry through effects on synaptogenesis, myelination, and neurotransmitter system maturation. Clinical data, while more limited, suggest that early intervention with certain medications may confer greater therapeutic benefits and potentially reduce long-term medication requirements compared to delayed treatment approaches. However, concerning findings also indicate that some drug classes may interfere with normal developmental processes when administered during vulnerable periods, potentially leading to adverse long-term consequences. These findings highlight the critical importance of developmental timing in pediatric neuropharmacology and suggest that optimizing therapeutic outcomes requires careful consideration of both the benefits and risks of early intervention. Future research should prioritize identifying specific critical periods for different drug classes and developing biomarkers to guide personalized timing of therapeutic interventions in developing populations.
journal: Journal of child psychology and psychiatry, and allied disciplines

title: Achieving synaptically relevant pulses of neurotransmitter using PDMS microfluidics.
abstract: Precise spatiotemporal control of neurotransmitter delivery remains a significant challenge in neuropharmacological research, limiting our ability to study synaptic transmission mechanisms. Traditional drug application methods, including bath perfusion and iontophoresis, lack the temporal resolution necessary to mimic physiological neurotransmitter release kinetics. Here, we describe a novel microfluidic approach using polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) devices to generate synaptically relevant pulses of neurotransmitters with millisecond temporal precision and micrometer spatial resolution. We fabricated multi-channel PDMS microfluidic chips with integrated pneumatic valves capable of switching between control and neurotransmitter-containing solutions within 2-5 milliseconds. The system was validated using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from cultured hippocampal neurons and acute brain slices. Glutamate pulses (1 mM, 10-100 ms duration) applied to individual dendritic spines generated AMPA receptor-mediated currents with rise times of 1.8 ± 0.3 ms and decay kinetics comparable to miniature excitatory postsynaptic currents. Similarly, GABA applications (10 mM, 5-50 ms) produced physiologically relevant inhibitory responses. The spatial confinement of neurotransmitter application was confirmed using fluorescent dextran tracers, demonstrating effective targeting within 5 μm of the delivery site. This microfluidic platform enables reproducible delivery of brief neurotransmitter pulses that closely approximate synaptic release, providing a valuable tool for investigating receptor kinetics, synaptic integration, and pharmacological mechanisms under physiologically relevant conditions. The technique offers significant advantages over existing methods for studying fast synaptic transmission and drug action at individual synapses.

title: 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline antagonizes a rise in brain dopamine metabolism, glutamate release in frontal cortex and locomotor hyperactivity produced by MK-801 but not the disruptions of prepulse inhibition, and impairment of working memory in rat.
abstract: N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor hypofunction has been implicated in the pathophysiology of schizophrenia, with NMDA receptor antagonists like MK-801 producing behavioral and neurochemical alterations resembling aspects of this disorder. 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (1MeTIQ), an endogenous compound with neuroprotective properties, has shown potential modulatory effects on dopaminergic neurotransmission. This study investigated whether 1MeTIQ could attenuate MK-801-induced neurochemical and behavioral changes in male Wistar rats. Animals received pretreatment with 1MeTIQ (25 or 50 mg/kg, i.p.) 30 minutes before MK-801 administration (0.3 mg/kg, i.p.). Dopamine metabolism was assessed by measuring DOPAC and HVA levels in striatum and frontal cortex using HPLC. Glutamate release in frontal cortex was monitored by in vivo microdialysis. Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity, prepulse inhibition (PPI) of acoustic startle, and spatial working memory using the Y-maze test. MK-801 significantly increased locomotor activity, dopamine turnover, and cortical glutamate efflux while disrupting PPI and impairing working memory performance. Pretreatment with 1MeTIQ dose-dependently prevented MK-801-induced hyperlocomotion, normalized dopamine metabolism, and blocked excessive glutamate release in frontal cortex. However, 1MeTIQ failed to reverse MK-801-induced PPI deficits or working memory impairment. These findings suggest that 1MeTIQ selectively modulates certain aspects of NMDA receptor hypofunction, particularly those involving dopaminergic hyperactivity and glutamatergic dysfunction, while cognitive and sensorimotor gating abnormalities remain unaffected. The partial protective profile of 1MeTIQ indicates distinct neurobiological mechanisms underlying different schizophrenia-like phenotypes induced by NMDA receptor

title: Photolysis of caged neurotransmitters: theory and procedures for light delivery.
abstract: Caged neurotransmitters have emerged as powerful tools for investigating synaptic transmission and neural circuit function with high spatial and temporal resolution. However, optimal application of these photolabile compounds requires precise understanding of photolysis kinetics and standardized protocols for light delivery systems. We present a comprehensive theoretical framework for caged neurotransmitter photolysis and describe validated procedures for implementing UV flash photolysis in acute brain slice preparations. Photolysis efficiency was characterized using caged glutamate (CNB-glutamate) and caged GABA (CNB-GABA) in hippocampal CA1 pyramidal neurons through whole-cell patch-clamp recordings. Flash lamp systems delivering 1-5 ms UV pulses (peak wavelength 365 nm) achieved 60-80% uncaging efficiency with spatial resolution of 10-20 μm when coupled to appropriate objective lenses. Theoretical modeling revealed that photolysis follows first-order kinetics with rate constants dependent on photon flux density, cage photolysis cross-section, and quantum yield. Practical considerations include cage compound concentration (50-500 μM), flash intensity calibration, and optical pathway optimization to minimize tissue photodamage while maximizing neurotransmitter release. Validation experiments demonstrated reliable induction of fast synaptic responses with rise times of 2-8 ms, comparable to natural synaptic transmission. These standardized protocols enable reproducible caged compound experiments across laboratories and provide essential technical foundations for advanced applications including synaptic plasticity studies, circuit mapping, and investigation of neurotransmitter receptor kinetics in native tissue environments.

title: [The neurochemistry and neuropharmacology of frontotemporal dementia].
abstract: Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) represents a heterogeneous group of neurodegenerative disorders characterized by progressive deterioration of the frontal and temporal cortices, leading to profound changes in behavior, personality, and language. Despite its clinical significance as the second most common cause of presenile dementia, the neurochemical basis of FTD remains poorly understood compared to Alzheimer's disease. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding the neurochemical alterations and pharmacological interventions in FTD. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of postmortem studies, neuroimaging investigations, and clinical trials published between 1990 and 2005, focusing on neurotransmitter systems, protein aggregation patterns, and therapeutic approaches. The neurochemical profile of FTD reveals significant disruptions in multiple neurotransmitter systems, including reduced cholinergic and GABAergic activity in affected regions, altered serotonergic function correlating with behavioral symptoms, and dysregulation of dopaminergic pathways contributing to motor manifestations. Protein aggregation studies demonstrate distinct patterns of tau and ubiquitin-positive inclusions, differentiating FTD subtypes from other tauopathies. Current pharmacological interventions show limited efficacy, with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors providing modest benefits for behavioral symptoms and cholinesterase inhibitors showing inconsistent results. Novel therapeutic targets include modulation of tau phosphorylation, enhancement of GABAergic transmission, and neuroprotective strategies targeting oxidative stress. The complex neurochemical landscape of FTD necessitates development of targeted therapies addressing specific neurotransmitter deficits and pathological protein accumulation, moving beyond traditional approaches used in Alzheimer's disease.

title: An in vivo technique for pharmacological manipulation of Drosophila brain during optical recording.
abstract: The fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster has emerged as a powerful model system for investigating neural circuits and behavior, yet current methods for simultaneous pharmacological manipulation and optical monitoring of brain activity in vivo remain limited. We developed a novel technique combining targeted drug delivery with real-time calcium imaging to enable pharmacological dissection of neural function in the intact Drosophila brain. Our approach utilizes a custom-designed perfusion chamber that maintains fly viability while allowing precise application of pharmacological agents to exposed brain regions. We incorporated a moveable micropipette system for localized drug delivery coupled with two-photon calcium imaging of genetically encoded indicators (GCaMP) in identified neurons. We validated this system using GABA receptor modulators (picrotoxin, muscimol) and demonstrated selective modulation of neuronal activity in mushroom body Kenyon cells and antennal lobe projection neurons. Picrotoxin application (10-100 μM) increased spontaneous calcium transient frequency by 340 ± 45%, while muscimol (50-200 μM) decreased activity by 78 ± 12% compared to saline controls (n=8-12 flies per condition). The preparation remained stable for over 2 hours, with consistent pharmacological responses and preserved circuit architecture as verified by post-hoc immunohistochemistry. This technique provides temporal resolution of seconds and spatial precision of individual dendritic compartments. Our method significantly advances the toolkit available for Drosophila neuropharmacology research and will facilitate mechanistic studies of neural circuit function, neurotransmitter systems, and drug screening applications in this genetically tractable model organism.

title: How to design PET experiments to study neurochemistry: application to alcoholism.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as a powerful tool for investigating neurochemical alterations in alcohol use disorders, yet methodological considerations critically impact data interpretation and reproducibility. This review synthesizes current best practices for designing PET neurochemistry studies in alcoholism research, addressing common pitfalls and optimization strategies. We examined 127 published PET studies investigating dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems in alcohol-dependent subjects from 2000-2013, analyzing experimental design parameters including radioligand selection, scanning protocols, and subject characterization. Key design considerations include appropriate radioligand choice based on target receptor density and binding kinetics, standardized abstinence periods to minimize acute withdrawal effects, and careful matching of control subjects for age, sex, and smoking status. Quantification approaches significantly influenced effect sizes, with reference tissue methods showing greater sensitivity for detecting group differences in striatal dopamine D2/D3 receptor availability compared to arterial input function methods. Studies incorporating pharmacological challenges revealed enhanced detection of synaptic neurotransmitter changes, particularly for dopamine release paradigms. Sample size calculations indicated that detecting 15-20% group differences in receptor binding requires 12-15 subjects per group with 80% power. Standardized abstinence periods of 3-4 weeks optimized detection of trait-related neuroadaptations while minimizing acute withdrawal confounds. These methodological guidelines provide a framework for designing robust PET neurochemistry studies in alcoholism, facilitating more reliable characterization of addiction-related neurobiological alterations and improved translation to therapeutic interventions.
journal: The Yale journal of biology and medicine

title: A Focused Review on the Treatment of Pediatric Patients with Atypical Antipsychotics.
abstract: Atypical antipsychotics are increasingly prescribed to pediatric patients for various psychiatric conditions, yet comprehensive safety and efficacy data in this population remain limited. The unique developmental neurobiology of children and adolescents may confer differential treatment responses and adverse event profiles compared to adults. This focused review synthesizes current evidence regarding the clinical use of second-generation antipsychotics in pediatric populations aged 6-17 years. A systematic literature search was conducted using PubMed, EMBASE, and PsycINFO databases from 2008-2017, identifying randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series examining aripiprazole, olanzapine, quetiapine, risperidone, paliperidone, and ziprasidone in pediatric patients. Primary outcomes included efficacy measures for FDA-approved indications (schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, autism spectrum disorders, and Tourette's disorder) and safety profiles including metabolic parameters, extrapyramidal symptoms, and prolactin elevation. Twenty-three studies encompassing 2,847 pediatric patients were included. Risperidone and aripiprazole demonstrated robust efficacy for irritability associated with autism spectrum disorders, while olanzapine and quetiapine showed comparable efficacy to adults for bipolar mania. Significant safety concerns emerged, including substantial weight gain (mean 4.2-8.7 kg over 6-12 weeks), metabolic dysregulation in 15-35% of patients, and hyperprolactinemia with risperidone and paliperidone. Extrapyramidal symptoms occurred less frequently than with typical antipsychotics but remained clinically significant. These findings support careful risk-benefit consideration when prescribing atypical antipsychotics to pediatric patients, emphasizing the need for regular metabolic monitoring and exploration of non-pharmacological interventions as first-line treatments when appropriate.
journal: Journal of child and adolescent psychopharmacology

title: Endocannabinoids and the neurochemistry of gluttony.
abstract: The endocannabinoid system has emerged as a critical mediator of feeding behavior and energy homeostasis, yet its role in pathological overeating remains poorly understood. We investigated the neurochemical mechanisms underlying cannabinoid-induced hyperphagia in the hypothalamic feeding circuits of adult male Wistar rats (n=48). Animals received bilateral microinjections of the CB1 receptor agonist WIN55,212-2 (0.1-1.0 μg) or vehicle into the paraventricular nucleus (PVN) or lateral hypothalamus (LH) during scheduled feeding periods. Food intake, meal microstructure, and tissue levels of anandamide (AEA) and 2-arachidonoylglycerol (2-AG) were measured using HPLC-MS/MS. Immunohistochemistry and in situ hybridization examined neuropeptide Y (NPY), agouti-related peptide (AgRP), and proopiomelanocortin (POMC) expression. WIN55,212-2 administration in the LH, but not PVN, significantly increased 24-hour food intake (vehicle: 24.3±1.8g vs. high dose: 41.7±3.2g, p<0.001) and meal frequency without affecting meal size. This hyperphagic response was blocked by the CB1 antagonist rimonabant. Hypothalamic AEA levels were elevated 3-fold in food-deprived animals compared to ad libitum fed controls (p<0.01), while 2-AG remained unchanged. CB1 activation upregulated NPY and AgRP mRNA expression in the arcuate nucleus by 180% and 220% respectively, while suppressing POMC expression by 60% (all p<0.05). These findings demonstrate that endocannabinoids promote excessive food intake through CB1-mediated disinhibition of orexigenic pathways in the lateral hypothalamus, suggesting therapeutic targets for treating pathological overeating disorders.

title: I Believe I Can Fly!: Use of Drosophila as a Model Organism in Neuropsychopharmacology Research.
abstract: The fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster has emerged as a powerful model organism for neuropsychopharmacological research, offering unique advantages including genetic tractability, conserved neurotransmitter systems, and behavioral paradigms relevant to human psychiatric disorders. This review synthesizes current applications of Drosophila in drug discovery and mechanism elucidation across major neuropsychiatric conditions. We conducted a comprehensive literature analysis of Drosophila-based studies published between 2010-2016, focusing on models of addiction, depression, anxiety, schizophrenia, and cognitive disorders. Our analysis reveals that Drosophila exhibits conserved responses to major classes of psychoactive compounds, including dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic modulators. Notably, fly models have successfully recapitulated key features of human drug addiction, including sensitization, tolerance, and withdrawal behaviors following exposure to cocaine, alcohol, and nicotine. Sleep and circadian rhythm studies in flies have provided crucial insights into the neurobiological basis of mood disorders and identified novel therapeutic targets. Furthermore, Drosophila models of neurodevelopmental disorders have facilitated the discovery of small molecule compounds that rescue behavioral and synaptic deficits. High-throughput screening approaches using fly models have identified several lead compounds currently advancing through mammalian preclinical testing. These findings demonstrate that Drosophila serves as an invaluable bridge between molecular mechanisms and mammalian studies in neuropsychopharmacology. The genetic precision, cost-effectiveness, and translational relevance of fly models position Drosophila as an essential component of the modern drug discovery pipeline for neuropsychiatric therapeutics.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Tobacco smoking and nicotine neuropsychopharmacology: some future research directions.
abstract: Tobacco smoking remains one of the most prevalent forms of drug dependence worldwide, yet fundamental questions regarding nicotine's neuropsychopharmacological mechanisms continue to limit therapeutic interventions. While nicotinic acetylcholine receptors have been established as primary targets for nicotine's reinforcing effects, the complex interactions between receptor subtypes, regional brain distribution, and behavioral outcomes require systematic investigation. This review synthesizes current knowledge of nicotine neuropharmacology and proposes critical research directions for the next decade. Key areas requiring investigation include: (1) characterization of neuronal nicotinic receptor subunit diversity and their differential roles in dependence processes, (2) examination of nicotine's effects on dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic neurotransmission in reward circuits, (3) development of animal models that better recapitulate human smoking patterns and individual vulnerability factors, and (4) identification of genetic polymorphisms influencing nicotine metabolism and receptor function. Emerging molecular techniques, including receptor subtype-selective ligands, transgenic mouse models, and in vivo microdialysis, offer unprecedented opportunities to dissect nicotine's neurobiological effects. Additionally, neuroimaging approaches in human subjects may bridge findings from animal studies to clinical populations. Future research should prioritize understanding how chronic nicotine exposure produces long-lasting neuroadaptations that maintain tobacco dependence. Such investigations will be essential for developing more effective pharmacotherapies and identifying individuals at highest risk for tobacco addiction, ultimately reducing the substantial public health burden of smoking-related diseases.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Advances in paediatric neuropsychopharmacology: an overview.
abstract: The field of paediatric neuropsychopharmacology has experienced remarkable growth over the past decade, driven by increased recognition of childhood psychiatric disorders and the unique pharmacological challenges they present. This comprehensive review examines recent advances in the use of psychotropic medications in children and adolescents, focusing on developmental considerations, efficacy data, and safety profiles. A systematic literature review was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases from 1990-2001, supplemented by recent conference proceedings and regulatory documents. Key therapeutic areas examined include attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), childhood depression, anxiety disorders, and early-onset psychotic disorders. Significant progress has been made in understanding the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of psychotropic agents in developing populations. Stimulant medications for ADHD demonstrate robust efficacy with well-characterized safety profiles, while selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors show promise for childhood anxiety and depression, though with important caveats regarding suicidal ideation monitoring. Atypical antipsychotics have emerged as valuable treatments for severe behavioural disturbances and early-onset schizophrenia, despite concerns about metabolic side effects. Critical gaps remain in our understanding of long-term developmental effects of psychotropic medications, particularly regarding growth, sexual maturation, and cognitive development. The paucity of controlled trials in paediatric populations continues to challenge evidence-based prescribing. Future research priorities include dose-finding studies, long-term safety surveillance, and investigation of pharmacogenomic factors affecting drug response in children. Enhanced regulatory frameworks and industry incentives are essential to advance this critical field.

title: Changes in striatal electroencephalography and neurochemistry induced by kainic acid seizures are modified by dopamine receptor antagonists.
abstract: The excitotoxic effects of kainic acid (KA) involve complex interactions between glutamatergic and dopaminergic systems, particularly in the striatum where these neurotransmitter pathways extensively converge. This study investigated whether dopamine receptor antagonists could modulate KA-induced electrophysiological and neurochemical alterations in the rat striatum. Adult male Wistar rats (n=8 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of KA (10 mg/kg) alone or in combination with the D1 receptor antagonist SCH23390 (0.5 mg/kg) or the D2 receptor antagonist haloperidol (1.0 mg/kg). Striatal electroencephalographic activity was continuously monitored for 6 hours post-injection, and neurochemical analyses were performed using high-performance liquid chromatography to measure dopamine, DOPAC, HVA, and GABA concentrations at 24 hours. KA administration produced characteristic epileptiform discharges in the striatum within 30 minutes, accompanied by significant depletion of striatal dopamine (-67%, p<0.001) and GABA (-43%, p<0.01) levels. Pretreatment with SCH23390 significantly attenuated the duration and amplitude of KA-induced seizure activity and partially protected against dopamine depletion (-31%, p<0.05 vs KA alone). Haloperidol pretreatment showed less pronounced protective effects on both electrophysiological and neurochemical parameters. These findings suggest that D1 receptor-mediated mechanisms contribute to KA-induced striatal excitotoxicity, and that selective D1 antagonism may offer neuroprotection against glutamate-mediated seizure damage in this brain region.

title: Effects of soybean peptide on immune function, brain function, and neurochemistry in healthy volunteers.
abstract:
**Background:** Soy-derived bioactive peptides have demonstrated immunomodulatory and neuroprotective properties in animal models, yet their effects on human brain function and neurochemistry remain poorly characterized. This study investigated whether oral supplementation with soybean peptide influences immune markers, cognitive performance, and neurotransmitter metabolism in healthy adults.

**Methods:** In this randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, 84 healthy volunteers (aged 25-55 years) received either soybean peptide (3g daily) or placebo for 8 weeks. Primary outcomes included serum cytokine levels (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, IL-10), lymphocyte proliferation responses, and cognitive assessments using standardized neuropsychological batteries. Secondary measures comprised plasma amino acid profiles, urinary monoamine metabolites (3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid, 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid), and brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) concentrations.

**Results:** Soybean peptide supplementation significantly increased serum IL-10 levels (p=0.032) and enhanced lymphocyte proliferation responses to mitogens (p=0.018) compared to placebo. Cognitive performance improved in attention and working memory domains (p=0.024 and p=0.041, respectively). Plasma tryptophan and tyrosine concentrations increased by 23% and 18%, respectively (both p<0.05), while urinary 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid excretion rose by 34% (p=0.009). BDNF levels showed a modest but significant elevation (p=0.047) in the treatment group.

**Conclusions:** These findings suggest that soybean peptide supplementation enhances immune function and cognitive performance in healthy individuals, potentially through modulation of amino acid availability and neurotransmitter synthesis. Further research is warranted to elucidate optimal dosing strategies and long-term effects on neurological health.

title: Psychedelic Psychiatry's Brave New World.
abstract: The resurgence of psychedelic research has transformed our understanding of psychiatric therapeutics, yet the underlying neural mechanisms remain poorly characterized. Here, we investigated the molecular and circuit-level effects of psilocybin, a 5-HT2A receptor agonist, in treatment-resistant depression using a combination of single-cell RNA sequencing, electrophysiology, and functional connectivity analysis in both mouse models and human subjects. Single-cell profiling revealed that psilocybin induces rapid transcriptional changes in cortical pyramidal neurons, particularly upregulating genes associated with synaptic plasticity including BDNF, Arc, and Egr1. Electrophysiological recordings demonstrated increased dendritic spine formation and enhanced long-term potentiation in prefrontal cortex within 24 hours of treatment. Notably, we identified a critical period of heightened neuroplasticity lasting 2-4 weeks post-administration, characterized by elevated mTOR signaling and increased protein synthesis. In a randomized, double-blind clinical trial (n=89), patients receiving psilocybin-assisted therapy showed significant improvements in depression scores (HAM-D reduction of 18.3 ± 3.2 points) compared to placebo controls (4.1 ± 2.8 points, p<0.001). Functional MRI revealed increased connectivity between default mode network regions and decreased amygdala hyperactivity that correlated with therapeutic outcomes. These effects persisted for at least 6 months, suggesting sustained neuroplastic remodeling. Our findings reveal that psychedelics leverage endogenous plasticity mechanisms to enable rapid and durable psychiatric improvements, establishing a neurobiological framework for precision psychedelic medicine.

title: Affinity labeling the dopamine transporter ligand binding site.
abstract: The dopamine transporter (DAT) plays a crucial role in terminating dopaminergic neurotransmission by removing dopamine from the synaptic cleft, and represents a primary target for psychostimulants such as cocaine and amphetamine. Despite extensive pharmacological characterization, the precise molecular architecture of the DAT ligand binding site remains poorly understood. We employed photoaffinity labeling techniques to identify amino acid residues within the cocaine and dopamine binding domains of the human DAT. A series of photoreactive analogues of cocaine and dopamine were synthesized, including [³H]3β-[4-(4-azido-3-iodophenyl)methyl]tropane-2β-carboxylic acid methyl ester (RTI-82) and [³H]2-azido-dopamine. Membrane preparations from HEK293 cells stably expressing human DAT were incubated with photolabels under UV irradiation conditions. Covalently modified transporters were isolated by immunoprecipitation and subjected to proteolytic digestion followed by peptide mapping and Edman degradation sequencing. RTI-82 specifically labeled Asp79 in transmembrane domain 1 and Phe320 in transmembrane domain 6, while 2-azido-dopamine primarily modified Ser422 in transmembrane domain 8. Competition experiments with unlabeled ligands confirmed specificity of labeling. Mutational analysis revealed that substitution of labeled residues significantly reduced binding affinity for both substrate and inhibitor ligands. These results provide direct biochemical evidence for a multi-domain binding pocket involving transmembrane segments 1, 6, and 8, and suggest overlapping but distinct binding sites for dopamine and cocaine within the DAT structure. This photoaffinity mapping approach offers valuable insights into transporter structure-function relationships and may facilitate rational drug design efforts.

title: Changes in cerebrospinal fluid neurochemistry during pregnancy.
abstract: Pregnancy involves profound physiological adaptations that may influence central nervous system neurochemistry, yet systematic examination of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) neurotransmitter profiles during gestation remains limited. We investigated changes in CSF concentrations of major neurotransmitter metabolites and neuropeptides across pregnancy trimesters. Twenty-four healthy pregnant women underwent lumbar puncture during routine spinal anesthesia for cesarean delivery, with samples collected from 8 women per trimester. Control CSF samples were obtained from 12 age-matched non-pregnant women undergoing elective procedures. High-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection was used to measure homovanillic acid (HVA), 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), and 3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylglycol (MHPG). Radioimmunoassays quantified β-endorphin, corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH), and oxytocin concentrations. Significant progressive increases in CSF β-endorphin levels were observed across trimesters (first: 18.2±3.1 pg/mL; second: 34.7±5.8 pg/mL; third: 58.9±8.2 pg/mL) compared to controls (12.4±2.3 pg/mL; p<0.001). CRH concentrations were elevated in second and third trimesters (2.8-fold and 4.1-fold increases, respectively; p<0.01). Oxytocin levels increased significantly only in the third trimester (p<0.05). CSF 5-HIAA concentrations decreased progressively throughout pregnancy, reaching 35% below control values by the third trimester (p<0.01). HVA and MHPG levels remained unchanged. These findings demonstrate substantial alterations in central nervous system neurochemistry during pregnancy, particularly in endogenous opioid, stress-response, and serotonergic systems. Such changes may contribute to pregnancy-associated mood alterations and provide neurobiological insights into peripartum psychiatric disorders.

title: Interaction Between the Trace Amine-Associated Receptor 1 and the Dopamine D2 Receptor Controls Cocaine's Neurochemical Actions.
abstract: The trace amine-associated receptor 1 (TAAR1) has emerged as a potential modulator of dopaminergic neurotransmission, but its precise interaction with dopamine D2 receptors (D2R) in mediating cocaine's effects remains unclear. We investigated the functional relationship between TAAR1 and D2R in controlling cocaine-induced neurochemical responses using pharmacological and molecular approaches in rodent models. Wild-type and TAAR1 knockout mice received acute cocaine administration (15 mg/kg, i.p.) with or without selective TAAR1 agonist RO5263397 (10 mg/kg) or D2R antagonist raclopride (1 mg/kg). Extracellular dopamine levels in the nucleus accumbens were measured using in vivo microdialysis, while D2R binding and cAMP signaling were assessed using radioligand binding and biochemical assays. Cocaine increased extracellular dopamine by 340% in wild-type mice, but this response was potentiated to 485% in TAAR1 knockout animals. TAAR1 activation with RO5263397 significantly reduced cocaine-induced dopamine efflux and attenuated D2R-mediated signaling through enhanced receptor internalization. Co-immunoprecipitation studies revealed direct protein-protein interactions between TAAR1 and D2R in striatal tissue. Functional coupling experiments demonstrated that TAAR1 activation inhibited D2R-mediated adenylyl cyclase suppression in a dose-dependent manner. These findings establish that TAAR1 functions as a negative allosteric modulator of D2R signaling and serves as an endogenous brake on cocaine's dopaminergic effects. The TAAR1-D2R interaction represents a novel regulatory mechanism that could be therapeutically targeted for substance use disorders, suggesting TAAR1 agonists may have clinical utility in treating cocaine addiction.

title: Zebrafish assessment of cognitive improvement and anxiolysis: filling the gap between in vitro and rodent models for drug development.
abstract: Traditional drug discovery for neuropsychiatric disorders relies heavily on in vitro assays followed by costly rodent studies, creating a significant gap in translational research. Zebrafish (Danio rerio) have emerged as a promising intermediate model organism, offering vertebrate complexity with high-throughput screening capabilities. This review examines the utility of zebrafish behavioral assays for evaluating cognitive enhancement and anxiolytic properties of novel compounds. We analyzed published studies from 2005-2011 employing standardized zebrafish protocols including novel tank diving, light-dark preference, shoaling behavior, and spatial learning paradigms. Pharmacological validation studies demonstrated that zebrafish exhibit dose-dependent responses to established anxiolytics (diazepam, buspirone) and cognitive enhancers (modafinil, piracetam) consistent with mammalian models. The novel tank test showed 89% concordance with rodent elevated plus-maze results for anxiolytic screening across 23 compounds tested. Cognitive assessment using T-maze and conditioned place preference protocols revealed significant correlations (r=0.76, p<0.001) with Morris water maze performance in corresponding rodent studies. Zebrafish demonstrated superior throughput capacity, requiring 48-72 hours versus 2-4 weeks for equivalent rodent protocols, while maintaining 85% translational predictivity for clinical outcomes. These findings establish zebrafish as a valuable bridge between cellular and mammalian models, offering rapid, cost-effective screening for neurotropic compounds. The high evolutionary conservation of neurotransmitter systems and behavioral responses supports zebrafish integration into tiered drug development strategies for anxiety and cognitive disorders.

title: Time-dependent behavioral, neurochemical, and metabolic dysregulation in female C57BL/6 mice caused by chronic high-fat diet intake.
abstract: Obesity and metabolic dysfunction are increasingly recognized as risk factors for neuropsychiatric disorders, yet the temporal relationship between diet-induced metabolic changes and behavioral alterations remains poorly understood, particularly in females. This study investigated time-dependent effects of chronic high-fat diet (HFD) consumption on behavioral, neurochemical, and metabolic parameters in female C57BL/6 mice. Eight-week-old female mice were fed either standard chow (10% fat) or HFD (60% fat) for 2, 8, or 16 weeks (n=12-15/group). Behavioral assessments included open field test, elevated plus maze, forced swim test, and novel object recognition. Neurochemical analyses measured monoamine levels in prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, and striatum using HPLC. Metabolic parameters included body weight, glucose tolerance, insulin sensitivity, and serum leptin levels. HFD mice showed progressive weight gain and glucose intolerance by 2 weeks, with insulin resistance developing by 8 weeks. Anxiety-like behavior emerged at 8 weeks, accompanied by reduced dopamine and serotonin levels in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Depressive-like behavior and cognitive impairment were evident only after 16 weeks of HFD exposure, coinciding with further reductions in hippocampal BDNF expression and elevated neuroinflammatory markers. Serum leptin levels were elevated throughout all time points but showed diminished central leptin sensitivity after 8 weeks. These findings demonstrate that chronic HFD consumption in female mice produces sequential metabolic dysfunction, anxiety, and finally depression with cognitive deficits, suggesting distinct temporal windows for intervention strategies targeting diet-induced neuropsychiatric complications.

title: History of modern psychopharmacology: a personal view with an emphasis on antidepressants.
abstract: The development of modern psychopharmacology has fundamentally transformed psychiatric practice over the past five decades, with antidepressant medications representing one of the most significant therapeutic advances in medicine. This historical review examines the evolution of antidepressant pharmacotherapy from the serendipitous discovery of imipramine's antidepressant properties in 1957 through the introduction of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) in the 1980s and 1990s. A comprehensive analysis of landmark clinical trials, regulatory milestones, and theoretical developments was conducted using published literature, FDA records, and personal communications with key researchers in the field. The tricyclic antidepressants, beginning with imipramine and amitriptyline, established the foundation for understanding monoaminergic mechanisms in depression, despite significant anticholinergic and cardiovascular side effects. The monoamine oxidase inhibitors, though effective, were limited by dietary restrictions and drug interactions. The introduction of fluoxetine in 1987 marked a paradigm shift, offering comparable efficacy with improved tolerability profiles. Subsequent SSRIs including sertraline, paroxetine, and fluvoxamine expanded treatment options, while novel mechanisms including dual-action agents like venlafaxine emerged in the 1990s. Clinical response rates consistently demonstrated 60-70% efficacy across drug classes, though remission rates remained suboptimal. The evolution of antidepressant pharmacotherapy illustrates the critical importance of serendipitous observations, rigorous clinical investigation, and mechanistic understanding in advancing psychiatric therapeutics. Future directions must address treatment-resistant depression and personalized medicine approaches to optimize patient outcomes.

title: [Neuropharmacology yesterday, today and tomorrow].
abstract: The development of neuropharmacology as a scientific discipline has undergone remarkable transformation since the pioneering investigations of Sechenov and Pavlov. Early neurophysiological studies focused primarily on anatomical localization and basic reflex mechanisms, while contemporary neuropharmacological research emphasizes molecular mechanisms of synaptic transmission and receptor pharmacology. This analytical review examines the evolution of neuropharmacological concepts and methodologies from classical neurophysiology to modern molecular approaches. Historical analysis was conducted through systematic examination of key publications spanning seven decades, from initial studies of autonomic pharmacology to recent investigations of neurotransmitter systems. Classical experiments established fundamental principles of chemical neurotransmission, beginning with acetylcholine at neuromuscular junctions and extending to central monoaminergic pathways. Contemporary research has identified numerous neurotransmitter systems including dopamine, serotonin, GABA, and neuropeptides, elucidating their roles in behavior, cognition, and neurological disorders. Methodological advances in radioligand binding, electrophysiology, and biochemical assays have enabled precise characterization of receptor subtypes and their pharmacological profiles. Current investigations focus on G-protein coupled receptors, ion channels, and intracellular signaling cascades. Future directions will likely emphasize molecular cloning approaches, computer-assisted drug design, and therapeutic applications for neurodegenerative diseases. The integration of classical neurophysiological principles with emerging molecular techniques represents the optimal strategy for advancing neuropharmacological knowledge in service of clinical medicine and our understanding of higher nervous system function.
journal: Fiziologicheskii zhurnal SSSR imeni I. M. Sechenova

title: Cosmetic psychopharmacology and the President's Council on Bioethics.
abstract: The emerging field of cosmetic psychopharmacology—the use of psychoactive medications to enhance normal psychological functioning rather than treat diagnosed mental illness—has generated significant ethical and policy debates. The President's Council on Bioethics has raised concerns about the implications of pharmaceutical cognitive and mood enhancement for human nature, authentic selfhood, and social justice. This analysis examines the neurobiological basis for these concerns and evaluates current evidence regarding the efficacy and safety of off-label psychotropic use in healthy populations. We reviewed clinical trials of stimulants, antidepressants, and anxiolytics in non-clinical samples, along with neuroimaging studies of enhancement effects on brain function. Additionally, we analyzed bioethical arguments presented in the Council's reports and related policy documents. Current evidence suggests modest cognitive improvements from stimulants like modafinil and amphetamines in healthy individuals, with limited data on long-term safety. Antidepressants may enhance emotional resilience and social cognition in non-depressed populations, while anxiolytics can reduce performance anxiety. However, individual responses vary considerably, and mechanisms of action in normal brains remain poorly understood. The Council's concerns about authenticity and coercion appear philosophically complex but empirically unsubstantiated. More problematic are issues of equitable access and potential pressure for enhancement use in competitive environments. Rigorous long-term studies are needed to establish safety profiles for cosmetic psychopharmacological interventions. Regulatory frameworks should focus on evidence-based safety standards rather than essentialist notions of human nature, while addressing legitimate concerns about social justice and autonomous choice.

title: The endocannabinoid system: a new molecular target for the treatment of tobacco addiction.
abstract: Tobacco addiction remains a major public health concern with limited therapeutic options and high relapse rates. Recent advances in understanding the endocannabinoid system (ECS) have revealed its crucial role in reward processing and addiction pathways, suggesting potential therapeutic applications for smoking cessation. The present study investigated the effects of cannabinoid CB1 receptor modulation on nicotine self-administration and withdrawal behaviors in rodent models. Male Sprague-Dawley rats were trained to self-administer nicotine (0.03 mg/kg/infusion) and subsequently treated with the CB1 receptor antagonist rimonabant (0.3-3.0 mg/kg, i.p.) or the fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH) inhibitor URB597 (0.1-1.0 mg/kg, i.p.). Nicotine self-administration, progressive ratio performance, cue-induced reinstatement, and somatic withdrawal signs were assessed. Rimonabant dose-dependently reduced nicotine self-administration by 45-78% and significantly attenuated cue-induced reinstatement without affecting food-maintained responding. Conversely, URB597 enhanced endogenous anandamide levels and reduced somatic withdrawal symptoms including weight loss, teeth chattering, and anxiety-like behaviors. Neurochemical analysis revealed that both treatments modulated dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens, with rimonabant reducing nicotine-evoked dopamine increases by 60%. These findings demonstrate that pharmacological manipulation of the ECS can effectively reduce nicotine-seeking behaviors and withdrawal symptoms through distinct but complementary mechanisms. The data support the therapeutic potential of targeting cannabinoid signaling pathways for tobacco addiction treatment, warranting further clinical investigation of ECS modulators as novel smoking cessation aids.

title: [Psychopharmacological Studies in Mice].
abstract: Psychoactive compounds continue to pose significant challenges in understanding their mechanisms of action and potential therapeutic applications. This study investigated the behavioral and neurochemical effects of novel synthetic compounds XM-47 and XM-52 in male ICR mice to elucidate their psychopharmacological profiles. Mice (n=8-10 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of test compounds (0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0 mg/kg) or vehicle control. Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity monitoring, elevated plus-maze testing, forced swim test, and rotarod performance over 120 minutes post-administration. Brain tissue samples were analyzed for monoamine neurotransmitter levels using high-performance liquid chromatography. XM-47 demonstrated dose-dependent increases in locomotor activity (peak at 1.0 mg/kg, 247% of control, p<0.01) and significant anxiolytic-like effects in the elevated plus-maze (increased open arm time, p<0.05). XM-52 exhibited primarily sedative properties with decreased locomotor activity at higher doses and impaired rotarod performance. Neurochemical analysis revealed XM-47 significantly elevated striatal dopamine levels (168% of control, p<0.01) and reduced serotonin turnover, while XM-52 increased GABA concentrations in the hippocampus and cortex. Neither compound showed antidepressant-like activity in the forced swim test. These findings suggest XM-47 acts primarily through dopaminergic mechanisms with stimulant and anxiolytic properties, whereas XM-52 demonstrates GABAergic activity consistent with its sedative profile. Further investigation into receptor binding specificity and therapeutic potential is warranted.
journal: Yakugaku zasshi : Journal of the Pharmaceutical Society of Japan

title: Consensus statements on the clinical uses of pregabalin for Hong Kong.
abstract:
**Background:** Pregabalin, a gabapentinoid with established efficacy in neuropathic pain and epilepsy, has gained increasing attention for psychiatric applications in Asia-Pacific regions. However, clinical practice guidelines specific to Hong Kong populations remain limited. This study aimed to develop evidence-based consensus statements on pregabalin's clinical uses in Hong Kong psychiatric practice.

**Methods:** A modified Delphi process was conducted involving 24 psychiatrists and neurologists from major Hong Kong medical institutions between January-September 2019. Three rounds of structured questionnaires examined pregabalin's efficacy, safety, dosing protocols, and clinical applications across anxiety disorders, bipolar disorder, and substance use disorders. Literature review encompassed randomized controlled trials and real-world evidence from Asian populations. Consensus was defined as ≥75% agreement among panelists.

**Results:** Strong consensus emerged supporting pregabalin as second-line treatment for generalized anxiety disorder (91.7% agreement) and panic disorder (87.5% agreement) when first-line agents prove inadequate. For bipolar disorder, 79.2% agreed pregabalin demonstrated utility as adjunctive treatment for anxiety symptoms and sleep disturbances. Recommended starting doses were 75mg twice daily for anxiety disorders and 150mg daily for bipolar applications, with gradual titration based on response and tolerability. Common adverse effects included dizziness (68% of patients), sedation (45%), and weight gain (32%). Panelists emphasized careful monitoring for dependency potential, particularly in patients with substance use history.

**Conclusions:** These consensus statements provide practical guidance for pregabalin use in Hong Kong clinical practice, emphasizing its role as adjunctive therapy while highlighting the importance of individualized treatment approaches and comprehensive patient monitoring in Chinese populations.
journal: Asia-Pacific psychiatry : official journal of the Pacific Rim College of Psychiatrists

title: In vivo neurochemical correlates of cognitive processes: methodological and conceptual challenges.
abstract: The investigation of neurochemical substrates underlying cognitive processes in living organisms presents formidable methodological and theoretical obstacles that significantly impact data interpretation and translational relevance. Traditional approaches including microdialysis, voltammetry, and pharmacological manipulations have provided valuable insights into neurotransmitter dynamics during learning, memory, and attention tasks, yet each technique carries inherent limitations that constrain temporal and spatial resolution. We critically examine current methodological approaches used to assess in vivo neurochemical changes during cognitive performance, with particular emphasis on technical constraints affecting data quality and reproducibility. Microdialysis studies demonstrate significant variability in neurotransmitter sampling efficiency across brain regions, with recovery rates ranging from 15-40% depending on probe membrane characteristics and tissue properties. Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry offers superior temporal resolution but remains limited to select neurotransmitters and restricted anatomical locations. Pharmacological interventions, while providing mechanistic insights, often produce systemic effects that complicate attribution of cognitive changes to specific neural circuits. Furthermore, substantial inter-individual differences in baseline neurochemical activity and drug metabolism introduce considerable variance in experimental outcomes. Conceptual challenges include establishing causal relationships between neurochemical fluctuations and behavioral performance, accounting for compensatory mechanisms, and integrating data across multiple neurotransmitter systems. We propose that future advances require integration of complementary techniques, development of more selective pharmacological tools, and adoption of standardized protocols to enhance reproducibility. These methodological refinements are essential for advancing our understanding of the complex neurochemical basis of cognition and facilitating translation to clinical applications.

title: Psychopharmacology of autistic spectrum disorders in children and adolescents.
abstract: Autism spectrum disorders (ASDs) affect approximately 1 in 110 children and are characterized by impaired social communication, restricted interests, and repetitive behaviors. While behavioral interventions remain the cornerstone of treatment, pharmacological management of associated symptoms has become increasingly important in clinical practice. This review examines the current evidence base for psychopharmacological interventions in children and adolescents with ASDs. A comprehensive literature search was conducted using PubMed, Cochrane Library, and PsycINFO databases from 1990 to 2011, focusing on randomized controlled trials and systematic reviews of pharmacological treatments in pediatric ASD populations. Key outcome measures included core autism symptoms, associated behavioral problems, and adverse effects. Evidence supports the use of atypical antipsychotics, particularly risperidone and aripiprazole, for managing irritability, aggression, and self-injurious behaviors, with both agents receiving FDA approval for these indications in pediatric ASD patients. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors show mixed efficacy for repetitive behaviors and anxiety symptoms, with fluoxetine demonstrating some benefit in adolescents. Stimulant medications can effectively treat comorbid attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptoms, though response rates may be lower than in typically developing children. Alpha-2 agonists show promise for hyperactivity and sleep disturbances. However, no medications have demonstrated significant improvement in core social communication deficits. While pharmacological interventions can provide meaningful symptom relief for associated behavioral problems in children and adolescents with ASDs, treatment decisions must carefully weigh potential benefits against risks of adverse effects, with close monitoring essential for optimal outcomes.

title: Emerging roles for novel antipsychotic medications in the treatment of schizophrenia.
abstract: Second-generation antipsychotic medications have emerged as promising alternatives to conventional neuroleptics in the treatment of schizophrenia, offering potential advantages in efficacy and tolerability. This review examines the clinical evidence supporting the use of clozapine, risperidone, and olanzapine in schizophrenic patients, with particular attention to their effects on positive symptoms, negative symptoms, and extrapyramidal side effects. A comprehensive analysis of randomized controlled trials published between 1990 and 1997 was conducted, encompassing over 3,000 patients across multiple treatment centers. Clinical efficacy was assessed using the Positive and Negative Syndrome Scale (PANSS) and the Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS), while extrapyramidal symptoms were evaluated using standardized rating instruments. Results demonstrate that novel antipsychotics produce superior improvements in negative symptoms compared to haloperidol and other typical agents, with effect sizes ranging from 0.4 to 0.8. Clozapine showed particular efficacy in treatment-resistant populations, with 30-40% of previously refractory patients achieving clinically meaningful improvement. Risperidone and olanzapine demonstrated comparable positive symptom control to conventional agents while producing significantly fewer extrapyramidal side effects at therapeutic doses. However, novel agents were associated with distinct adverse effect profiles, including weight gain, sedation, and potential metabolic disturbances. These findings suggest that second-generation antipsychotics represent a significant advancement in schizophrenia treatment, offering improved therapeutic outcomes and enhanced patient tolerability, though careful monitoring remains essential for optimal clinical management.
journal: The Psychiatric clinics of North America

title: Academic psychiatry and the pharmaceutical industry.
abstract: The relationship between academic psychiatry and the pharmaceutical industry has become increasingly complex, raising concerns about potential conflicts of interest that may influence research integrity and clinical practice. This study examined the extent and nature of financial relationships between academic psychiatrists and pharmaceutical companies, and their potential impact on research outcomes and clinical recommendations. We conducted a comprehensive survey of 847 academic psychiatrists at 25 major medical institutions across the United States, supplemented by analysis of pharmaceutical company disclosure reports and published research from 2003-2005. Additionally, we performed a systematic review of 312 randomized controlled trials in major psychiatry journals to assess correlations between industry funding and reported outcomes. Our findings revealed that 78% of academic psychiatrists reported some form of financial relationship with pharmaceutical companies, including consulting fees (45%), research grants (52%), speakers' bureau participation (31%), and equity holdings (12%). Industry-funded studies were significantly more likely to report favorable outcomes for the sponsor's drug compared to independently funded research (OR = 3.4, 95% CI: 2.1-5.6, p < 0.001). Furthermore, 23% of department chairs reported that industry relationships influenced faculty hiring and promotion decisions. These results demonstrate widespread financial entanglements between academic psychiatry and pharmaceutical companies that may compromise scientific objectivity and patient care. Academic medical centers must develop more stringent conflict-of-interest policies and transparency requirements to preserve the integrity of psychiatric research and maintain public trust in the profession.

title: New vistas in immunopsychopharmacology.
abstract: The emerging field of immunopsychopharmacology represents a paradigm shift in understanding the complex interactions between the immune system, central nervous system, and psychoactive drugs. Growing evidence suggests that traditional psychopharmacological agents may exert therapeutic effects partially through modulation of immune function, while immune mediators reciprocally influence neurochemical processes underlying psychiatric disorders. This review synthesizes current knowledge from both clinical and preclinical studies examining bidirectional neuroimmune communication in the context of psychotropic drug action. We analyzed data from recent investigations utilizing cytokine measurements, lymphocyte proliferation assays, and neuroendocrine challenge tests in patients receiving antidepressants, antipsychotics, and anxiolytics. Additionally, we examined animal studies employing immune stimulation models and psychopharmacological interventions. Results demonstrate that selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and tricyclic antidepressants significantly alter interleukin-1β, tumor necrosis factor-α, and interleukin-6 levels in depressed patients, correlating with clinical improvement. Antipsychotic medications modulate T-helper cell responses and natural killer cell activity in schizophrenic patients. Benzodiazepines influence immunoglobulin production and complement activation. Conversely, immune challenges with lipopolysaccharide or interleukin-1 administration in animal models produce depression-like behaviors reversible by antidepressant treatment. These findings suggest that immunomodulation may represent a novel therapeutic target for psychiatric disorders. Understanding neuroimmune mechanisms could lead to development of more effective treatments combining traditional psychopharmacological approaches with immunotherapeutic strategies, potentially revolutionizing psychiatric medicine in the next millennium.

title: Effects of the mGluR2/3 agonist LY379268 on ketamine-evoked behaviours and neurochemical changes in the dentate gyrus of the rat.
abstract: Glutamate dysfunction has been implicated in the pathophysiology of schizophrenia, with both hypoactivation of NMDA receptors and dysregulation of metabotropic glutamate receptors contributing to disease symptoms. The mGluR2/3 agonist LY379268 has shown promise in preclinical models as a potential antipsychotic agent. The present study examined whether LY379268 could attenuate ketamine-induced behavioral and neurochemical alterations in rats, using the dentate gyrus as a key region of interest due to its role in cognitive processing and NMDA receptor expression. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10/group) received intraperitoneal injections of vehicle, ketamine (30 mg/kg), LY379268 (3 mg/kg), or ketamine plus LY379268 pretreatment (30 min prior). Locomotor activity was monitored for 120 minutes post-injection, and dentate gyrus tissue was collected for neurochemical analysis using high-performance liquid chromatography. Ketamine administration significantly increased locomotor activity compared to controls (p<0.001) and elevated dopamine and glutamate concentrations in the dentate gyrus (147% and 132% of control, respectively). Pretreatment with LY379268 dose-dependently reduced ketamine-induced hyperlocomotion by 68% (p<0.01) and normalized dentate gyrus neurotransmitter levels. LY379268 alone had no significant effects on behavior or neurochemistry. These findings demonstrate that mGluR2/3 activation effectively counteracts NMDA receptor hypofunction-induced behavioral and neurochemical disturbances, supporting the therapeutic potential of mGluR2/3 agonists in treating glutamate-related psychiatric disorders. The dentate gyrus appears to be a critical site for these modulatory effects.

title: Effects of dopamine D2/D3 receptor antagonism on human planning and spatial working memory.
abstract: Dopaminergic signaling in the prefrontal cortex and striatum plays a crucial role in executive functions, yet the specific contribution of D2/D3 receptors to higher-order cognitive processes remains poorly understood. This study investigated the effects of selective D2/D3 receptor antagonism on planning abilities and spatial working memory in healthy volunteers. We conducted a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover study with 24 healthy adults (mean age 24.3±3.1 years, 12 female). Participants received either sulpiride (400mg) or placebo and completed the Tower of London task to assess planning performance and a spatial n-back task (1-back, 2-back, 3-back) to evaluate working memory capacity. Cognitive testing occurred 2-3 hours post-administration during peak plasma concentrations. Compared to placebo, sulpiride significantly impaired planning performance, increasing both solution time (p=0.012) and number of moves required (p=0.031) on complex Tower of London problems. Spatial working memory showed a differential pattern, with sulpiride selectively impairing 3-back performance accuracy (p=0.018) while leaving 1-back and 2-back performance intact. Reaction times were prolonged across all working memory conditions under sulpiride (p=0.007). These findings demonstrate that D2/D3 receptors are critical for optimal planning and high-demand spatial working memory in humans. The selective impairment of complex cognitive operations suggests that D2/D3 antagonism disrupts prefrontal-striatal circuits specifically when cognitive load is high, providing insight into the neurochemical mechanisms underlying executive dysfunction in psychiatric disorders characterized by altered dopaminergic transmission.

title: Synthesis and pharmacological evaluation of 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinolines.
abstract: The synthesis of novel 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinoline derivatives was undertaken to explore their potential as central nervous system active compounds. Tetrahydroisoquinoline scaffolds have demonstrated significant pharmacological activity at dopaminergic and adrenergic receptors, warranting investigation of structurally related analogues. A series of twenty-three compounds was synthesized via Pictet-Spengler cyclization reactions starting from appropriately substituted phenethylamines and aldehydes, followed by phenolic coupling reactions to generate the chromeno-fused derivatives. All compounds were characterized by 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and mass spectrometry. Pharmacological evaluation included radioligand binding assays at dopamine D1 and D2 receptors, α1- and α2-adrenergic receptors, and serotonin 5-HT1A and 5-HT2A receptors using rat brain membranes. Functional activity was assessed using cAMP accumulation assays and calcium mobilization studies. Several 4-phenoxy-tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives exhibited moderate to high affinity for dopamine D2 receptors (Ki = 45-180 nM), with compound 7c showing selectivity over D1 receptors (>50-fold). The chromeno-fused analogues demonstrated preferential binding to α1-adrenergic receptors, with compound 12b displaying nanomolar affinity (Ki = 32 nM). Functional studies revealed that most active compounds behaved as partial agonists or antagonists at their respective targets. These results suggest that 4-phenoxy-tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives represent promising lead compounds for the development of selective dopaminergic agents, while the chromeno-fused derivatives may serve as α1-adrenergic receptor modulators with potential cardiovascular applications.

title: Gaddum and LSD: the birth and growth of experimental and clinical neuropharmacology research on 5-HT in the UK.
abstract: The discovery of lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD) and John Henry Gaddum's pioneering work in the 1950s fundamentally transformed our understanding of serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine, 5-HT) neuropharmacology in the United Kingdom. This historical review examines the development of 5-HT research from Gaddum's initial observations of LSD's antagonistic effects on smooth muscle preparations through to the establishment of modern clinical neuropharmacology programmes. We analysed primary literature, laboratory records, and correspondence from key British institutions including the National Institute for Medical Research, Edinburgh University, and the Medical Research Council between 1953-1980. Gaddum's seminal 1953 paper demonstrating LSD's ability to block 5-HT responses in isolated tissue preparations provided the first evidence linking indoleamine neurotransmission to psychoactive drug effects. This work catalysed subsequent investigations by Brodie, Woolley, and Shaw, leading to the identification of 5-HT in mammalian brain tissue and the development of the first selective serotonergic ligands. The establishment of the MRC Neuropsychopharmacology Unit in 1961 under Gaddum's influence created a framework for systematic clinical studies of 5-HT function in psychiatric disorders. By 1975, British researchers had characterised multiple 5-HT receptor subtypes, developed novel antidepressant compounds, and established serotonin's role in mood regulation.
