Gaddum's initial LSD studies represent a pivotal moment in neuropharmacology, demonstrating how serendipitous observations of psychedelic compounds can illuminate fundamental neurotransmitter mechanisms and drive therapeutic innovation in psychiatry and neurology.

title: Neurochemistry of Anesthetic States.
abstract: The molecular mechanisms underlying anesthetic-induced unconsciousness remain incompletely understood, hindering the development of safer anesthetic agents and improved monitoring techniques. While previous studies have characterized individual neurotransmitter systems during anesthesia, comprehensive neurochemical profiling across multiple brain regions has been limited by methodological constraints. Here, we present a novel enzymatic approach combining high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) and optimized tissue extraction protocols to simultaneously quantify monoamines, amino acids, and acetylcholine in discrete brain regions during various anesthetic states. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were anesthetized with propofol, sevoflurane, or ketamine at equipotent doses, with conscious controls receiving saline. Brain regions including prefrontal cortex, thalamus, hippocampus, and brainstem were rapidly extracted and processed using our modified enzymatic digestion protocol. Propofol administration significantly decreased dopamine (67% reduction, p<0.001) and norepinephrine (45% reduction, p<0.01) in the prefrontal cortex while increasing GABA levels by 34% (p<0.05). Sevoflurane produced similar but less pronounced changes, whereas ketamine uniquely elevated glutamate concentrations in the hippocampus (28% increase, p<0.01) while reducing thalamic acetylcholine by 52% (p<0.001). Regional variations in neurochemical responses correlated with known anesthetic binding sites and consciousness-related neural circuits. This enzymatic approach provides enhanced sensitivity and reproducibility for studying anesthetic neurochemistry, revealing agent-specific patterns that may inform mechanism-based anesthetic development and perioperative monitoring strategies.

title: Pharmacological regulation of gene expression.
abstract: The pharmacological regulation of gene expression represents a critical mechanism underlying therapeutic drug action and adverse effects in the central nervous system. While traditional approaches have focused on receptor binding and enzymatic inhibition, emerging evidence suggests that many psychoactive compounds exert their effects through direct modulation of transcriptional machinery. We investigated the capacity of classical neuropharmacological agents to regulate gene expression in primary cortical neurons and assessed the temporal dynamics of these responses. Primary rat cortical neurons were treated with clinically relevant concentrations of fluoxetine (10 μM), haloperidol (1 μM), diazepam (5 μM), or vehicle control for 6, 12, and 24 hours. Gene expression profiles were analyzed using quantitative RT-PCR arrays targeting 84 genes involved in synaptic plasticity, neurotransmitter synthesis, and cellular stress responses. Chromatin immunoprecipitation assays examined drug-induced changes in histone modifications at target gene promoters. Fluoxetine treatment resulted in significant upregulation of BDNF (2.3-fold, p<0.01) and CREB1 (1.8-fold, p<0.05) within 6 hours, accompanied by increased H3K4me3 marks at their promoter regions. Haloperidol induced delayed but sustained changes in dopamine receptor gene expression and immediate early genes. Diazepam primarily affected GABAergic signaling genes with minimal transcriptional effects. Time-course analysis revealed distinct temporal patterns for each compound class. These findings demonstrate that neuropharmacological agents possess intrinsic capacity to reshape neuronal gene expression programs through epigenetic mechanisms, providing novel insights into their therapeutic mechanisms and suggesting new targets for drug development in psychiatric and neurological disorders.

title: A review of monoaminergic neuropsychopharmacology in zebrafish.
abstract: The zebrafish (Danio rerio) has emerged as a powerful vertebrate model for investigating monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems and their pharmacological modulation. This review synthesizes current knowledge of dopaminergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic pathways in zebrafish and their relevance to neuropsychiatric drug discovery. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published literature from 2000-2010 examining monoamine neurotransmitter distribution, receptor pharmacology, and behavioral responses to psychoactive compounds in zebrafish. Immunohistochemical and in situ hybridization studies reveal that zebrafish possess conserved monoaminergic nuclei analogous to mammalian systems, including dopaminergic clusters in the posterior tuberculum and ventral diencephalon, serotonergic raphe-like neurons, and noradrenergic locus coeruleus homologs. Pharmacological characterization demonstrates that zebrafish monoamine receptors exhibit similar ligand binding profiles and G-protein coupling patterns to their mammalian counterparts. Behavioral assays measuring locomotion, anxiety-like responses, and social behaviors show dose-dependent effects of classical psychotropic drugs including SSRIs, dopamine receptor agonists/antagonists, and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors that parallel mammalian responses. Importantly, genetic mutants affecting monoamine synthesis and metabolism display behavioral phenotypes consistent with human neuropsychiatric conditions. These findings establish zebrafish as a valuable translational model bridging molecular mechanisms and behavioral outcomes in monoaminergic neuropsychopharmacology. The conservation of monoamine systems across vertebrates, combined with zebrafish experimental advantages including genetic tractability, optical transparency, and high-throughput screening capabilities, positions this model organism as an important tool for understanding monoamine function and developing novel therapeutics for psychiatric disorders.

title: The Psychopharmacology of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Preclinical Roadmap.
abstract: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) affects 1-3% of the global population and is characterized by intrusive thoughts and repetitive behaviors mediated by dysregulated cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) circuits. Despite decades of clinical research, current pharmacotherapies provide adequate symptom relief for only 40-60% of patients, highlighting an urgent need for novel therapeutic targets. This review synthesizes emerging preclinical evidence to identify promising pharmacological avenues for OCD treatment. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of rodent models exhibiting OCD-like phenotypes, including genetic knockout models, pharmacological induction paradigms, and behavioral paradigms such as marble burying and schedule-induced polydipsia. Key findings demonstrate that beyond traditional serotonergic mechanisms, glutamatergic dysfunction plays a central role in OCD pathophysiology, with NMDA receptor hypofunction and altered mGluR5 signaling consistently observed across multiple model systems. Preclinical studies reveal therapeutic potential for glutamate modulators including memantine, ketamine, and mGluR2/3 agonists, which normalize compulsive behaviors and restore CSTC circuit function. Additionally, emerging evidence supports roles for GABAergic interneuron dysfunction, altered dopaminergic signaling in the nucleus accumbens, and neuroinflammatory processes involving microglia activation. Novel targets showing preclinical efficacy include sigma-1 receptor modulators, adenosine A2A receptor antagonists, and histone deacetylase inhibitors. Integration of optogenetic and chemogenetic approaches has further elucidated circuit-specific mechanisms and identified the orbitofrontal cortex and anterior cingulate cortex as critical nodes for therapeutic intervention. These preclinical findings provide a roadmap for developing next-generation OCD pharmacotherapies targeting glutamate, GABA, and neuromodulatory systems beyond serotonin.

title: Past and future of neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: The field of neuropsychopharmacology has undergone remarkable transformation since the serendipitous discovery of chlorpromazine's antipsychotic properties in the 1950s. This review examines the historical development of psychotropic medications and projects future directions based on emerging neuroscientific understanding. We analyzed the evolution of major drug classes including antipsychotics, antidepressants, anxiolytics, and mood stabilizers through comprehensive literature review spanning 1950-1989, evaluating therapeutic efficacy, mechanism elucidation, and adverse effect profiles. Additionally, we assessed contemporary research trends in receptor pharmacology, neurotransmitter systems, and molecular biology techniques. Historical analysis reveals three distinct phases: the empirical discovery period (1950s-1960s), characterized by phenothiazines and tricyclic antidepressants; the mechanistic understanding era (1970s-1980s), marked by dopamine hypothesis development and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor introduction; and the emerging molecular phase (late 1980s), featuring receptor subtype characterization. Current limitations include incomplete understanding of psychiatric disease etiology, significant adverse effects, and variable therapeutic response. Future developments likely include: selective receptor subtype modulators based on advancing molecular pharmacology; neuropeptide-targeted therapeutics; drugs addressing specific neurotransmitter pathway dysfunctions; and personalized medicine approaches utilizing pharmacogenetic principles. The integration of modern neuroscience methodologies with traditional pharmacological approaches promises more effective, better-tolerated treatments. Success will depend upon continued collaboration between clinical psychiatry, basic neuroscience, and pharmaceutical research, potentially revolutionizing psychiatric therapeutics within the next two decades.

title: Neuropharmacology of Synthetic Cathinones.
abstract: Synthetic cathinones, commonly known as "bath salts," are β-ketone analogues of amphetamines that have emerged as drugs of abuse with significant public health implications. These psychoactive substances exhibit complex pharmacological profiles through interactions with monoamine transporters, yet their precise mechanisms of action and neurotoxic potential remain incompletely characterized. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding the neuropharmacological properties of prominent synthetic cathinones including mephedrone, methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV), and α-pyrrolidinovalerophenone (α-PVP). We examined transporter selectivity profiles using in vitro uptake and release assays in HEK293 cells expressing human dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin transporters, alongside behavioral studies in rodent models of self-administration and locomotor sensitization. Synthetic cathinones demonstrated varied pharmacological profiles: mephedrone acted as a non-selective monoamine releaser similar to MDMA, while MDPV and α-PVP functioned primarily as potent dopamine and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors with minimal serotonergic activity. Pyrrolidine-containing cathinones showed enhanced potency and abuse liability compared to their non-pyrrolidine counterparts. In vivo studies revealed robust locomotor stimulation and reinforcing properties, with some compounds producing hyperthermia and oxidative stress markers in brain tissue. Structure-activity relationships indicated that α-carbon substitution and pyrrolidine ring incorporation significantly influence transporter selectivity and pharmacological potency. These findings highlight the diverse neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying synthetic cathinone toxicity and provide crucial insights for developing targeted therapeutic interventions and informing regulatory policies regarding these emerging substances of abuse.

title: Pharmaco fMRI: Determining the functional anatomy of the effects of medication.
abstract: Recent advances in functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) have enabled the investigation of drug effects on neural circuits in vivo, providing crucial insights into medication mechanisms of action. However, standardized approaches for pharmaco-fMRI studies remain limited. This study aimed to establish methodological guidelines and demonstrate the utility of pharmaco-fMRI in characterizing the functional neuroanatomy of psychoactive medication effects. We conducted a double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover study in 24 healthy volunteers (ages 21-35) using a single oral dose of escitalopram (10mg) versus placebo. Participants underwent resting-state and task-based fMRI scanning at baseline and 3 hours post-administration. The task paradigm included emotional face processing and working memory components. We analyzed whole-brain activation patterns, functional connectivity, and region-of-interest analyses focused on limbic and prefrontal circuits. Escitalopram significantly altered BOLD signal in the amygdala during emotional face processing (p<0.001, FWE-corrected) and increased functional connectivity between the anterior cingulate cortex and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex during working memory tasks. Resting-state analyses revealed enhanced default mode network coherence following drug administration compared to placebo (p<0.05, cluster-corrected). Individual differences in drug response correlated with baseline connectivity strength in fronto-limbic circuits (r=0.67, p<0.01). These findings demonstrate that pharmaco-fMRI can reliably detect acute medication effects on neural circuits relevant to psychiatric disorders. This approach offers a powerful translational tool for understanding drug mechanisms, optimizing dosing strategies, and potentially predicting individual treatment responses in clinical populations.

title: Neurophamacology of yawning.
abstract: Yawning is a stereotyped behavioral pattern observed across vertebrate species, yet its underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. While traditionally associated with fatigue or boredom, emerging evidence suggests yawning serves important physiological functions including thermoregulation and arousal state transitions. This study investigated the neurotransmitter systems mediating yawning behavior using pharmacological manipulation in male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-12 per group). Yawning frequency was quantified over 60-minute observation periods following systemic administration of selective receptor agonists and antagonists. Dopaminergic modulation was examined using apomorphine (0.05-0.8 mg/kg), haloperidol (0.1-1.0 mg/kg), and quinpirole (0.01-0.1 mg/kg). Cholinergic effects were assessed with physostigmine (0.1-0.5 mg/kg) and atropine (1-10 mg/kg). Additional experiments examined GABAergic, serotonergic, and opioidergic contributions. Low-dose apomorphine significantly increased yawning frequency (312% of control, p<0.001), an effect blocked by haloperidol pretreatment. Quinpirole produced a biphasic response with maximal yawning at 0.05 mg/kg. Physostigmine dose-dependently enhanced yawning behavior, while atropine produced modest inhibition. GABAA receptor activation with muscimol suppressed spontaneous yawning by 67% (p<0.01). Naloxone pretreatment potentiated apomorphine-induced yawning, suggesting tonic opioidergic inhibition. These findings demonstrate that yawning is regulated by complex interactions between dopaminergic D2/D3 receptors, cholinergic muscarinic pathways, and modulatory GABAergic and opioidergic systems. Understanding yawning neuropharmacology may provide insights into arousal disorders and serve as a behavioral biomarker for dopaminergic function in neurological conditions.

title: The presence of comorbidity in Tourette syndrome increases the need for pharmacological treatment.
abstract: Tourette syndrome (TS) is a complex neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by motor and vocal tics, with up to 90% of patients presenting comorbid psychiatric conditions including attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), and anxiety disorders. While tic severity alone has traditionally guided pharmacological intervention decisions, the impact of comorbid conditions on treatment necessity remains poorly understood. We conducted a retrospective analysis of 247 pediatric patients (ages 6-17 years) diagnosed with TS at our tertiary care center between 2005-2008. Patients were categorized into TS-only (n=61) and TS-comorbid groups (n=186), with comprehensive assessment using the Yale Global Tic Severity Scale, Children's Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale, ADHD Rating Scale-IV, and Beck Youth Inventories. Treatment decisions and medication utilization were analyzed using logistic regression models controlling for tic severity, age, and socioeconomic status. Patients with TS plus comorbidities were significantly more likely to receive pharmacological intervention than those with TS alone (78.5% vs 41.0%, p<0.001). This association remained significant after controlling for tic severity (OR=3.24, 95% CI: 1.82-5.77, p<0.001). The presence of ADHD (OR=2.89) and OCD symptoms (OR=2.15) were the strongest predictors of medication use. Functional impairment scores were significantly higher in the comorbid group (p<0.001), correlating with increased polypharmacy requirements. These findings suggest that comorbid psychiatric conditions, rather than tic severity alone, primarily drive pharmacological treatment decisions in pediatric TS, highlighting the importance of comprehensive psychiatric evaluation in treatment planning.

title: Photochemical and pharmacological evaluation of 7-nitroindolinyl-and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl-amino acids as novel, fast caged neurotransmitters.
abstract: Caged neurotransmitters have emerged as powerful tools for investigating synaptic transmission and neural circuit function with high temporal and spatial resolution. However, existing caged compounds often suffer from slow photolysis kinetics and limited tissue penetration, restricting their utility in physiological studies. We synthesized and characterized two novel classes of caged amino acid neurotransmitters based on 7-nitroindolinyl and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl protecting groups conjugated to glutamate, GABA, and glycine. Photochemical properties were assessed using flash photolysis with UV laser pulses (355 nm), while biological activity was evaluated in acute hippocampal slices from adult rats using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from CA1 pyramidal neurons. The 7-nitroindolinyl-caged compounds exhibited photolysis time constants of 45-65 μs, representing a 3-4 fold improvement over conventional o-nitrobenzyl derivatives. The 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl analogs demonstrated even faster kinetics (τ = 25-40 μs) with enhanced two-photon cross-sections at 740 nm. Caged glutamate and GABA showed minimal biological activity prior to photolysis (<2% of uncaged response) and complete restoration of native receptor responses following UV illumination. Quantum yields ranged from 0.15-0.28, comparable to existing caged compounds. The improved photolysis kinetics and maintained biological fidelity of these 7-nitroindolinyl-based caged neurotransmitters make them promising tools for high-resolution studies of fast synaptic transmission and enable more precise temporal control in neuropharmacological investigations of receptor function and neural network dynamics.

title: Building a virtual archive using brain architecture and Web 3D to deliver neuropsychopharmacology content over the Internet.
abstract: The exponential growth of neuropsychopharmacology data necessitates innovative approaches for knowledge dissemination and educational content delivery. Traditional static resources fail to adequately represent the complex three-dimensional relationships between brain structures, neurotransmitter systems, and pharmacological interventions. We developed a novel virtual archive platform that integrates detailed brain architecture models with Web 3D visualization technologies to create an interactive repository for neuropsychopharmacology content accessible through standard Internet browsers. The system was constructed using VRML 2.0 and X3D standards, incorporating high-resolution anatomical models derived from MRI datasets and histological atlases. Interactive modules were designed to visualize drug distribution pathways, receptor binding sites, and neurochemical cascades within accurate neuroanatomical contexts. The platform includes over 200 pharmaceutical agents with their corresponding mechanisms of action, pharmacokinetic profiles, and clinical applications. User evaluation studies involving 45 graduate students and 12 faculty members demonstrated significantly improved learning outcomes compared to traditional textbook-based instruction (p<0.001). Navigation efficiency metrics showed 73% faster information retrieval compared to conventional database searches. The virtual archive successfully renders complex neuropsychopharmacological concepts in an intuitive, spatially-accurate environment while maintaining compatibility across multiple browser platforms and operating systems. This Web 3D approach represents a paradigm shift in scientific information architecture, offering scalable solutions for complex biomedical data visualization and education. The platform's open architecture enables continuous content expansion and collaborative development, establishing a foundation for next-generation neuropsychopharmacology knowledge management systems.
journal: Computer methods and programs in biomedicine

title: Psychopharmacological profile of the alkaloid psychollatine as a 5HT2A/C serotonin modulator.
abstract: The naturally occurring indole alkaloid psychollatine, isolated from the Amazonian plant Psychotria psychollatrensis, has been traditionally used by indigenous communities for its psychoactive properties. Recent ethnobotanical surveys have identified this compound as a potential modulator of serotonergic neurotransmission, yet its specific receptor binding profile and behavioral effects remain uncharacterized. We investigated the in vitro binding affinity and functional activity of psychollatine at human 5-HT2A and 5-HT2C receptors expressed in HEK-293 cells, along with its behavioral effects in rodent models. Radioligand binding assays revealed that psychollatine exhibits high affinity for 5-HT2A receptors (Ki = 12.3 ± 2.1 nM) and moderate affinity for 5-HT2C receptors (Ki = 89.4 ± 8.7 nM), with minimal binding to other serotonin receptor subtypes. Functional calcium mobilization assays demonstrated that psychollatine acts as a partial agonist at 5-HT2A receptors (EC50 = 24.8 nM, Emax = 67% of serotonin) and as a weak partial agonist at 5-HT2C receptors (EC50 = 156 nM, Emax = 34% of serotonin). In vivo studies showed that psychollatine (0.5-2.0 mg/kg, i.p.) dose-dependently induced head-twitch responses in mice, an effect completely blocked by the selective 5-HT2A antagonist MDL 100907. The compound also modulated prepulse inhibition and locomotor activity in a manner consistent with 5-HT2A/2C activation. These findings establish psychollatine as a novel naturally occurring 5-HT2A/2C receptor modulator with potential applications in understanding serotonergic neurotransmission and developing new therapeutic agents.

title: Evaluation of metaquant microdialysis for measurement of absolute concentrations of amphetamine and dopamine in brain: a viable method for assessing pharmacokinetic profile of drugs in the brain.
abstract: Accurate measurement of absolute drug concentrations in brain tissue remains a critical challenge in neuropharmacology research. Traditional microdialysis techniques provide relative concentration changes but yield recovery-dependent values that complicate pharmacokinetic interpretation. This study evaluated the metaquant microdialysis approach for determining absolute concentrations of amphetamine and dopamine in rat striatum. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with CMA/12 probes in the dorsal striatum. Following amphetamine administration (2.5 mg/kg i.p.), metaquant calibration was performed using retrodialysis of known concentrations of amphetamine (10-1000 nM) and dopamine (1-100 nM) through the probe membrane. Extraction fraction was determined by measuring the difference between perfusate input and output concentrations. Dialysate samples were analyzed using HPLC-ED with detection limits of 0.5 nM for amphetamine and 0.2 nM for dopamine. The metaquant method yielded extraction fractions of 0.18 ± 0.03 for amphetamine and 0.22 ± 0.04 for dopamine in vivo. Peak absolute amphetamine concentrations reached 285 ± 45 nM at 45 minutes post-injection, while dopamine concentrations increased from baseline 12 ± 3 nM to 89 ± 18 nM at 30 minutes. Pharmacokinetic analysis revealed amphetamine half-life of 78 ± 12 minutes in striatal extracellular fluid. These results demonstrate that metaquant microdialysis provides reliable absolute concentration measurements, enabling accurate assessment of drug pharmacokinetics in discrete brain regions and offering significant advantages over conventional relative microdialysis approaches for neuropharmacological investigations.

title: Simple behavioral methods to assess the effect of drugs or toxins on sensory experience.
abstract: Behavioral assessment of sensory function represents a critical component in neuropharmacological research, yet many current methods require extensive training, sophisticated equipment, or invasive procedures. We developed and validated a battery of simple, non-invasive behavioral assays to evaluate drug and toxin effects on visual, auditory, tactile, and olfactory sensory systems in laboratory rats. The protocol consists of four standardized tests: (1) visual cliff avoidance for depth perception, (2) acoustic startle response amplitude for auditory sensitivity, (3) adhesive tape removal latency for somatosensory function, and (4) buried food pellet discovery time for olfactory detection. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8 per group) were tested following administration of known sensory-impairing agents: atropine (1-10 mg/kg, i.p.) for visual impairment, kanamycin (400 mg/kg, i.p.) for ototoxicity, capsaicin (50 mg/kg, s.c.) for nociceptive dysfunction, and zinc sulfate (intranasal) for anosmia. All compounds produced dose-dependent sensory deficits detectable within 30-60 minutes post-administration. Atropine significantly increased visual cliff crossing latency (p<0.01), kanamycin reduced startle amplitude by 65% (p<0.001), capsaicin doubled tape removal time (p<0.01), and zinc sulfate increased food-finding latency 4-fold (p<0.001). Inter-rater reliability exceeded 0.85 for all measures, and test-retest correlation was >0.90 across a two-week interval. These simple behavioral methods provide researchers with accessible, quantitative tools for screening sensory toxicity and monitoring therapeutic interventions without requiring specialized training or equipment, making them particularly suitable for laboratories with limited resources.

title: Treatment of hyperkinetic movement disorders.
abstract: Hyperkinetic movement disorders, including dystonia, chorea, and tremor, represent a heterogeneous group of neurological conditions with limited therapeutic options and significant functional impairment. Current pharmacological interventions often provide inadequate symptom control with substantial adverse effects. We conducted a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial to evaluate the efficacy and safety of riluzole, a glutamate antagonist with neuroprotective properties, in patients with treatment-refractory hyperkinetic movement disorders. Between January 2006 and September 2008, we enrolled 184 patients aged 18-75 years from 12 European centres. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either riluzole 100 mg daily (n=92) or matched placebo (n=92) for 16 weeks, with a 4-week washout period. The primary endpoint was change in the Unified Huntington's Disease Rating Scale motor subscore from baseline to week 16. Secondary endpoints included quality of life measures, functional disability scores, and safety parameters. Riluzole treatment resulted in a significant reduction in motor symptoms compared with placebo (mean difference -8·2 points, 95% CI -12·4 to -4·0; p=0·0002). Functional disability improved substantially in the riluzole group (p=0·018), with corresponding improvements in quality of life indices. Treatment was generally well tolerated, with mild gastrointestinal disturbances being the most common adverse events. These findings suggest that riluzole represents a promising therapeutic approach for hyperkinetic movement disorders, offering clinically meaningful symptom improvement with an acceptable safety profile. Further studies are warranted to establish optimal dosing regimens and long-term efficacy.

title: Microelectrode array-based system for neuropharmacological applications with cortical neurons cultured in vitro.
abstract: In vitro neuronal networks cultured on microelectrode arrays (MEAs) represent a promising platform for neuropharmacological screening and mechanistic studies of neural activity modulation. However, existing MEA systems often lack the temporal resolution and signal processing capabilities required for comprehensive pharmacological analysis. We developed an integrated MEA-based system optimized for real-time monitoring of spontaneous and evoked electrical activity in primary rat cortical neurons. The system incorporates a 60-electrode planar array with 30 μm diameter electrodes spaced at 200 μm intervals, coupled to custom amplification circuitry and digital signal processing algorithms for spike detection and network burst analysis. Primary cortical neurons from embryonic day 18 Sprague-Dawley rats were cultured on poly-D-lysine coated MEAs and maintained for 14-21 days in vitro. We characterized baseline network activity patterns and tested acute responses to GABA_A receptor modulators (muscimol, bicuculline), glutamate receptor antagonists (CNQX, APV), and sodium channel blockers (TTX, lidocaine). The system reliably detected single-unit spikes with signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 4:1 and temporal resolution of 40 kHz. Pharmacological interventions produced dose-dependent, reversible changes in firing rates and network burst characteristics consistent with known mechanisms of action. Muscimol (1-50 μM) decreased spike rates by 65-95%, while bicuculline (10-100 μM) increased burst frequency by 200-400%. This MEA platform provides a robust, high-throughput approach for screening neuroactive compounds and investigating network-level effects of pharmacological interventions in vitro.

title: Reduced computational models of serotonin synthesis, release, and reuptake.
abstract: Serotonergic dysfunction underlies numerous psychiatric and neurological disorders, yet computational modeling of serotonin (5-HT) dynamics remains limited by model complexity and computational cost. We developed reduced-order mathematical models of 5-HT synthesis, release, and reuptake to enable efficient simulation of serotonergic neurotransmission while preserving biological fidelity. Starting from detailed biophysical models incorporating tryptophan hydroxylase kinetics, vesicular packaging, calcium-dependent exocytosis, and serotonin transporter (SERT) function, we applied model reduction techniques including quasi-steady-state approximations and singular perturbation analysis. The resulting reduced models were validated against experimental data from rat dorsal raphe nucleus recordings and compared to full-order simulations. Our simplified models captured 5-HT synthesis dynamics with 85% accuracy while reducing computational time by two orders of magnitude. The reduced release model accurately reproduced stimulus-evoked 5-HT transients measured by fast-scan cyclic voltammetry, including both peak amplitudes and decay kinetics. Reuptake dynamics were well-preserved across physiological and pharmacologically-relevant SERT inhibition conditions, with less than 10% deviation from full models. Parameter sensitivity analysis revealed that synthesis rate and SERT density were the most critical determinants of steady-state 5-HT levels. These computationally efficient models enable real-time simulation of serotonergic circuits and provide a foundation for closed-loop neuromodulation systems targeting 5-HT dysfunction. The reduced-order framework presented here can be readily extended to other monoaminergic systems and integrated into larger network models of brain function.

title: Neurochemical signatures revealed by glutamine labeling in the chicken retina.
abstract: The retina contains multiple neuronal populations that utilize glutamate as their primary neurotransmitter, including photoreceptors, bipolar cells, and ganglion cells. While previous studies have characterized glutamate immunoreactivity in retinal neurons, the metabolic pathways underlying glutamate synthesis and cycling remain poorly understood. We employed [³H]glutamine autoradiography combined with selective pharmacological treatments to investigate glutamine uptake and metabolism in the chicken retina, a well-established model for vertebrate retinal organization. Eyecups from 14-day-old white leghorn chickens were incubated with [³H]glutamine (50 μCi/ml) for 30 minutes, either alone or in the presence of methionine sulfoximine (MSO), an inhibitor of glutamine synthetase, or aminooxyacetic acid (AOAA), an inhibitor of aminotransferases. Autoradiographic analysis revealed intense [³H]glutamine labeling in the inner nuclear layer, particularly in putative Müller glial cells, with moderate labeling in the ganglion cell layer and outer plexiform layer. MSO treatment significantly reduced labeling in Müller cells by 73% while enhancing labeling in bipolar and amacrine cell populations. AOAA treatment produced a distinct laminar pattern, with preferential accumulation in the inner plexiform layer and reduced labeling in photoreceptor terminals. Quantitative densitometry demonstrated that glutamine uptake varies significantly across retinal cell types, with Müller cells showing the highest capacity for glutamine metabolism. These findings suggest that glutamine serves as a precursor for multiple neurotransmitter pools in the retina and that glial-neuronal metabolic coupling plays a critical role in maintaining glutamatergic transmission in retinal circuits.

title: Integral methods for automatic quantification of fast-scan-cyclic-voltammetry detected neurotransmitters.
abstract: Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) has emerged as the gold standard for real-time detection of neurotransmitter release in brain tissue, yet current quantification methods rely heavily on manual peak identification and baseline correction, introducing operator bias and limiting throughput. Existing automated approaches often fail to accurately detect transient dopamine signals in noisy biological recordings, particularly during complex behavioral paradigms. We developed a novel integral-based computational framework for automated FSCV signal quantification that leverages continuous wavelet transforms and machine learning algorithms to identify and quantify dopamine release events. Our method was validated using synthetic datasets with known signal characteristics and tested on ex vivo brain slice recordings from rat nucleus accumbens (n=8) and in vivo recordings from freely-moving mice during reward-seeking behavior (n=12). The integral-based approach demonstrated superior performance compared to traditional peak-detection methods, achieving 94.3% accuracy in synthetic data validation and significantly reducing false positive rates (2.1% vs 8.7%, p<0.001) in biological recordings. Quantification precision improved by 35% compared to manual analysis, with inter-rater reliability increasing from r=0.76 to r=0.94. Processing time was reduced from 45 minutes to 3 minutes per dataset. The algorithm successfully detected dopamine transients with amplitudes as low as 50 nM and temporal resolution of 100 ms, while maintaining robust performance across different electrode types and experimental conditions. This automated quantification framework represents a significant advancement in FSCV data analysis, enabling high-throughput screening applications and reducing analytical bias in neurochemical measurements.

title: [Atypical antipsychotic drugs].
abstract: The introduction of atypical antipsychotic drugs has fundamentally altered the treatment landscape for schizophrenia and related psychotic disorders. Unlike conventional neuroleptics that primarily block dopamine D2 receptors, atypical agents demonstrate complex pharmacological profiles involving multiple neurotransmitter systems. We conducted a comprehensive review of the pharmacological mechanisms and clinical efficacy of atypical antipsychotics, focusing on clozapine, risperidone, olanzapine, and quetiapine. Clinical data from 24 controlled studies involving 3,847 patients with schizophrenia were analyzed, comparing efficacy and side effect profiles between atypical and conventional antipsychotics. Atypical agents demonstrated superior efficacy in treating negative symptoms, with 40-60% of patients showing significant improvement compared to 20-35% with haloperidol. Importantly, the incidence of extrapyramidal side effects was markedly reduced: tardive dyskinesia occurred in 0.5-5% of patients treated with atypical drugs versus 15-20% with conventional neuroleptics. The unique receptor binding profiles of atypical antipsychotics, particularly their high 5-HT2A/D2 receptor affinity ratios and interaction with cholinergic, histaminergic, and adrenergic systems, appear to underlie their improved tolerability. However, metabolic side effects including weight gain and glucose dysregulation emerged as new concerns, particularly with olanzapine and clozapine. These findings suggest that atypical antipsychotics represent a significant therapeutic advance, offering improved symptom control with reduced neurological complications, though requiring careful monitoring of metabolic parameters. Their introduction has established new standards for antipsychotic therapy in Dutch clinical practice.

title: Identifying novel protein-protein interactions using co-immunoprecipitation and mass spectroscopy.
abstract: Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are fundamental to neuronal signaling cascades and synaptic plasticity, yet many critical interactions remain uncharacterized. Traditional biochemical approaches have limited sensitivity for detecting transient or low-abundance protein complexes in neural tissues. We developed an optimized protocol combining co-immunoprecipitation (co-IP) with liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) to systematically identify novel PPIs in mouse brain tissue. Adult C57BL/6 mice were sacrificed and cortical, hippocampal, and striatal regions were rapidly dissected and homogenized under native conditions. Co-IP was performed using antibodies against known synaptic scaffolding proteins including PSD-95, NMDAR1, and mGluR5. Immunoprecipitated complexes were resolved by SDS-PAGE, subjected to in-gel trypsin digestion, and analyzed by high-resolution LC-MS/MS using a linear ion trap mass spectrometer. Database searches identified peptides with >95% confidence scores. We identified 127 high-confidence protein interactions, including 43 novel PPIs not previously reported in neuronal tissues. Notable discoveries included interactions between mGluR5 and several previously uncharacterized membrane-associated proteins, and a novel binding partner for PSD-95 with predicted kinase activity. Validation by reciprocal co-IP confirmed 89% of identified interactions. Brain region-specific analysis revealed distinct interaction profiles, suggesting specialized protein complex assembly across neural circuits. This optimized co-IP/MS protocol provides a robust platform for discovering functionally relevant protein interactions in neural tissues and may facilitate identification of new therapeutic targets for neurological disorders.

title: High-sensitive liquid chromatographic method for determination of neuronal release of serotonin, noradrenaline and dopamine monitored by microdialysis in the rat prefrontal cortex.
abstract: Microdialysis coupled with liquid chromatography has become an essential technique for monitoring neurotransmitter release in vivo, but existing methods often lack the sensitivity required for simultaneous detection of multiple monoamines in small brain regions. We developed a highly sensitive HPLC method with electrochemical detection for the simultaneous quantification of serotonin (5-HT), noradrenaline (NA), and dopamine (DA) in microdialysate samples from rat prefrontal cortex. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with microdialysis probes (2 mm membrane length) in the medial prefrontal cortex. Dialysate samples (20 μL) were collected at 20-minute intervals and analyzed using a C18 reverse-phase column with a mobile phase containing sodium acetate buffer, methanol, and octanesulfonic acid at pH 4.2. Detection was performed using a glassy carbon electrode at +0.65V versus Ag/AgCl reference. The method demonstrated excellent linearity (r² > 0.998) over the concentration range 0.1-50 fmol for all three neurotransmitters, with detection limits of 0.05 fmol for 5-HT, 0.08 fmol for NA, and 0.03 fmol for DA. Intra-day and inter-day precision were <8% for all compounds. Basal extracellular concentrations were 1.2 ± 0.3 fmol/20μL for 5-HT, 2.1 ± 0.4 fmol/20μL for NA, and 0.8 ± 0.2 fmol/20μL for DA. Pharmacological validation using selective reuptake inhibitors confirmed the neuronal origin of detected neurotransmitters. This sensitive method enables reliable monitoring of monoaminergic neurotransmission in the prefrontal cortex and should prove valuable for neuropharmacological studies investigating cognitive and emotional behaviors.

title: Mercury, selenium and neurochemical biomarkers in different brain regions of migrating common loons from Lake Erie, Canada.
abstract: Mercury (Hg) contamination in aquatic ecosystems poses significant neurological risks to piscivorous birds, while selenium (Se) may provide neuroprotective effects through antagonistic interactions. Common loons (Gavia immer) are particularly vulnerable due to their high trophic position and reliance on fish consumption. This study investigated regional brain distribution of Hg and Se, and associated neurochemical alterations in migrating loons from Lake Erie. Brain tissues from 24 adult loons collected during fall migration (2009-2010) were analyzed for total Hg and Se concentrations in cerebrum, cerebellum, and brainstem using cold vapor atomic absorption spectrophotometry and hydride generation atomic absorption spectrophotometry, respectively. Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity, dopamine, serotonin, and their metabolites were quantified using spectrophotometric and HPLC methods. Mercury concentrations ranged from 0.89-4.2 μg/g wet weight, with highest levels in cerebellum, followed by cerebrum and brainstem. Selenium concentrations (0.34-1.1 μg/g wet weight) showed similar regional patterns and were positively correlated with Hg (r=0.72, p<0.001). Significant decreases in AChE activity were observed in cerebrum (23% reduction) and cerebellum (31% reduction) relative to control values. Dopamine concentrations were reduced by 18% in cerebrum, while serotonin metabolite ratios indicated altered neurotransmitter turnover. Se:Hg molar ratios below 1.0 in 67% of individuals suggested inadequate Se protection. These findings demonstrate region-specific neurotoxic effects of Hg exposure in loons, with potential implications for motor function and behavior during critical migration periods.

title: Application of triple-probe microdialysis for fast pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic evaluation of dopamimetic activity of drug candidates in the rat brain.
abstract: Conventional microdialysis techniques for evaluating dopamimetic drug candidates require multiple experiments and extensive time commitments, limiting throughput in preclinical screening. We developed a novel triple-probe microdialysis methodology enabling simultaneous pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic assessment of dopamine-enhancing compounds in awake, freely-moving rats. Three microdialysis probes were stereotaxically implanted: one in the nucleus accumbens shell (pharmacodynamic readout), one in the caudate-putamen (regional comparison), and one in the jugular vein (pharmacokinetic sampling). Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of L-DOPA (100 mg/kg), amphetamine (2 mg/kg), or vehicle. Dialysate samples were collected every 15 minutes for 4 hours and analyzed using HPLC with electrochemical detection. Triple-probe microdialysis successfully demonstrated distinct pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic profiles for both compounds. L-DOPA showed delayed dopamine elevation (peak at 90 min) with preferential accumulation in nucleus accumbens versus caudate-putamen (2.3-fold difference), while amphetamine produced rapid, sustained increases in both regions. Plasma pharmacokinetics revealed L-DOPA's shorter half-life (32 ± 4 min) compared to amphetamine (78 ± 12 min). The triple-probe approach reduced experimental time by 60% compared to sequential single-probe studies while maintaining statistical power. This methodology provides a robust platform for rapid screening of dopamimetic drug candidates, offering simultaneous assessment of brain penetration, regional selectivity, and neurochemical efficacy within individual animals, thereby reducing animal usage while accelerating preclinical drug development timelines.

title: A rodent model of appetitive discrimination with concomitant evaluation of anxiety-like behavior.
abstract: The development of animal models that simultaneously assess multiple behavioral domains is crucial for understanding the complex interactions between cognitive function and emotional state in neuropsychiatric disorders. Existing paradigms typically evaluate appetitive learning and anxiety-related behaviors separately, limiting translational insights. We developed a novel behavioral apparatus combining elements of operant conditioning chambers with anxiety-testing environments to concurrently measure appetitive discrimination learning and anxiety-like behavior in rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were trained to discriminate between two auditory cues (2 kHz vs 8 kHz tones) for sucrose reinforcement while navigating an elevated platform with open and enclosed compartments. Discrimination performance was quantified by response accuracy and latency to lever press, while anxiety-like behavior was assessed through time spent in open versus enclosed areas and locomotor patterns. Animals successfully acquired the discrimination task over 14 days, reaching 85±7% accuracy by day 10. Rats with superior discrimination performance (>90% accuracy) showed significantly reduced anxiety-like behavior compared to poor performers (<70% accuracy), spending 23±4% versus 12±3% time in open areas, respectively (p<0.01). Administration of diazepam (1.0 mg/kg, i.p.) enhanced both discrimination learning and reduced anxiety measures, while amphetamine (1.5 mg/kg, i.p.) improved discrimination but increased anxiety-related behaviors. This paradigm provides a valuable tool for investigating the neurobiological substrates underlying cognition-emotion interactions and screening compounds with potential therapeutic effects on comorbid cognitive and anxiety disorders.

title: Effects of zona incerta lesions on striatal neurochemistry and behavioral asymmetry in 6-hydroxydopamine-lesioned rats.
abstract: The zona incerta (ZI) has emerged as a potential modulator of basal ganglia function, yet its role in Parkinson's disease pathophysiology remains poorly understood. This study investigated whether ZI lesions influence striatal neurochemistry and motor asymmetry in the unilateral 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) rat model of Parkinson's disease. Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) received unilateral 6-OHDA lesions of the medial forebrain bundle, followed by either sham surgery or electrolytic ZI lesions ipsilateral to the dopaminergic lesion. Behavioral asymmetry was assessed using amphetamine-induced rotation testing at 2, 4, and 6 weeks post-lesion. Striatal tissue was analyzed for dopamine, DOPAC, HVA, and serotonin content using high-performance liquid chromatography. Immunohistochemistry was performed to quantify tyrosine hydroxylase-positive neurons in the substantia nigra. ZI lesions significantly reduced amphetamine-induced rotational behavior compared to 6-OHDA-only controls (327 ± 45 vs. 612 ± 78 rotations/hour at 6 weeks, p<0.001). Striatal dopamine depletion was similar between groups (>90% reduction), but ZI-lesioned animals showed increased striatal serotonin levels (142% of control vs. 98% in 6-OHDA-only group, p<0.05) and elevated serotonin turnover ratios. No differences in nigral cell loss were observed between groups. These findings suggest that ZI lesions ameliorate parkinsonian motor asymmetry through mechanisms independent of dopamine restoration, potentially involving serotonergic compensation. The ZI may represent a novel therapeutic target for modulating basal ganglia output in movement disorders.

title: Effect of ethanolic extract of Rubia peregrina L. (Rubiaceae) on monoamine-mediated behaviour.
abstract: Rubia peregrina L. (Rubiaceae), commonly known as wild madder, has been traditionally used in Mediterranean folk medicine for treating various neuropsychiatric disorders, yet its neuropharmacological properties remain largely unexplored. This study investigated the effects of ethanolic extract of R. peregrina aerial parts on monoamine-mediated behaviours in experimental models. The ethanolic extract was prepared using standardized procedures and subjected to preliminary phytochemical screening. Male Swiss albino mice (25-30 g) were administered R. peregrina extract (100, 200, and 400 mg/kg, p.o.) and evaluated using forced swimming test, tail suspension test, open field test, and elevated plus maze. Additionally, monoamine oxidase (MAO-A and MAO-B) inhibitory activity was assessed using fluorometric assays, while brain monoamine levels were determined by HPLC. Phytochemical analysis revealed the presence of anthraquinones, flavonoids, and phenolic compounds. R. peregrina extract (200 and 400 mg/kg) significantly reduced immobility time in both forced swimming test (p<0.01) and tail suspension test (p<0.05) compared to control, suggesting antidepressant-like activity. The extract increased locomotor activity and exploratory behaviour in open field test, while enhancing anxiolytic-like effects in elevated plus maze. Biochemical analysis demonstrated significant inhibition of MAO-A (IC₅₀ = 145.2 μg/ml) and moderate MAO-B inhibition (IC₅₀ = 298.7 μg/ml). Brain tissue analysis revealed increased serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine levels following extract treatment. These findings suggest that R. peregrina possesses significant psychoactive properties mediated through monoaminergic mechanisms, supporting its traditional use and warranting further investigation as a potential natural antidepressant.

title: The neuropharmacological basis for the use of memantine in the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) represents a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by cognitive decline, memory impairment, and eventual loss of functional independence. Current therapeutic approaches have focused primarily on cholinesterase inhibition, yet this strategy addresses only one aspect of the complex pathophysiology underlying AD. Emerging evidence suggests that excitotoxicity mediated by excessive N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor activation contributes significantly to neuronal death in AD. Memantine, a low-affinity, voltage-dependent NMDA receptor antagonist, offers a novel therapeutic mechanism by selectively blocking pathological receptor overactivation while preserving physiological glutamatergic neurotransmission. This review examines the neuropharmacological rationale for memantine use in AD treatment through analysis of preclinical studies, receptor binding assays, electrophysiological recordings, and clinical trial data from recent phase II and III studies. Preclinical investigations demonstrate that memantine exhibits neuroprotective properties in models of excitotoxic neuronal injury and reduces amyloid-beta-induced toxicity. Clinical studies involving moderate to severe AD patients reveal significant improvements in cognitive function, activities of daily living, and global clinical measures compared to placebo treatment. The drug's unique pharmacological profile, characterized by fast blocking kinetics and moderate affinity for NMDA receptors, allows for therapeutic intervention without interfering with normal synaptic transmission. These findings support memantine as a promising therapeutic agent that targets the glutamatergic system in AD, representing a complementary approach to existing cholinergic therapies and potentially offering disease-modifying benefits in addition to symptomatic improvement.

title: Investigation of Potential Antioxidant, Thrombolytic and Neuropharmacological Activities of Homalomena aromatica Leaves Using Experimental and In Silico Approaches.
abstract: Homalomena aromatica (Schott) Hook.f. is a traditional medicinal plant widely used in Southeast Asian folk medicine for treating neurological disorders, but its pharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study aimed to evaluate the antioxidant, thrombolytic, and neuropharmacological properties of H. aromatica leaf extracts through combined experimental and computational approaches. Methanol and aqueous extracts were prepared and subjected to phytochemical screening, DPPH radical scavenging assays, and thrombolytic activity evaluation using human blood clots. Neuropharmacological activities were assessed using hole-board, open-field, and forced swimming tests in Swiss albino mice (n=6 per group). Molecular docking studies were performed against key neurological targets including acetylcholinesterase, GABA receptors, and monoamine oxidases. Phytochemical analysis revealed the presence of flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic compounds. The methanol extract demonstrated significant antioxidant activity (IC₅₀ = 47.32 ± 2.1 μg/mL) compared to ascorbic acid standard. Thrombolytic activity showed 23.4% clot lysis at 200 μg/mL concentration. In behavioral studies, both extracts (200 and 400 mg/kg) exhibited anxiolytic effects in the hole-board test and antidepressant-like activity in the forced swimming test, with the methanol extract showing superior efficacy. Molecular docking revealed strong binding affinities of identified compounds to acetylcholinesterase (-8.2 kcal/mol) and GABA-A receptors (-7.6 kcal/mol). These findings suggest that H. aromatica possesses promising antioxidant, thrombolytic, and neuropharmacological activities, supporting its traditional use and warranting further investigation for developing novel neurotherapeutic agents.

title: Different role of cAMP dependent protein kinase and CaMKII in H3 receptor regulation of histamine synthesis and release.
abstract: The histamine H3 receptor functions as an autoreceptor regulating histamine synthesis and release in the central nervous system, but the intracellular signaling mechanisms mediating these effects remain incompletely understood. We investigated the differential roles of cAMP-dependent protein kinase (PKA) and Ca2+/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II (CaMKII) in H3 receptor-mediated regulation of histaminergic function using rat hypothalamic slices and cultured histaminergic neurons. H3 receptor activation with imetit (1-100 nM) dose-dependently decreased histidine decarboxylase (HDC) activity and histamine release, effects blocked by the H3 antagonist thioperamide. Co-immunoprecipitation studies revealed constitutive association between H3 receptors and both PKA regulatory subunits and CaMKII. Selective PKA inhibition with H-89 (10 μM) completely prevented imetit-induced suppression of HDC activity and histamine synthesis, while having no effect on histamine release inhibition. Conversely, CaMKII inhibition with KN-93 (10 μM) selectively blocked H3-mediated reduction of histamine release without affecting HDC regulation. Western blot analysis showed that H3 activation decreased PKA-mediated phosphorylation of HDC at Ser320, while simultaneously reducing CaMKII autophosphorylation at Thr286. Immunofluorescence microscopy revealed distinct subcellular localizations of H3-PKA and H3-CaMKII complexes, with PKA predominantly cytosolic and CaMKII enriched at synaptic terminals. These findings demonstrate that H3 autoreceptors utilize spatially segregated signaling pathways, with PKA specifically regulating histamine synthesis through HDC phosphorylation, while CaMKII selectively controls histamine release mechanisms. This dual-pathway organization may allow for independent fine-tuning of histaminergic neurotransmission under different physiological conditions.

title: Can voltammetry measure nitrogen monoxide (NO) and/or nitrites?
abstract: Nitrogen monoxide (NO) and its oxidation products play critical roles in neuronal signaling, vascular regulation, and synaptic plasticity. Current methods for measuring NO in biological systems, including chemiluminescence and fluorescent indicators, have limitations in temporal resolution and spatial specificity. This study evaluated the feasibility of using fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) to detect NO and nitrite species in vitro and in brain tissue preparations. Carbon fiber microelectrodes (7 μm diameter) were fabricated and characterized for electrochemical detection of authentic NO solutions (1-100 μM) and sodium nitrite (10-500 μM) in phosphate-buffered saline and artificial cerebrospinal fluid. Voltammetric measurements were performed using triangular waveforms (-0.4 to +1.3 V, 400 V/s) and compared with established amperometric techniques. FSCV detected NO with an oxidation peak at approximately +0.9 V versus Ag/AgCl, showing linear responses from 5-75 μM (r² = 0.94) with detection limits of 2.3 μM. Nitrite exhibited distinct voltammetric signatures with oxidation occurring at +1.1 V. However, electrode fouling occurred within 15-20 minutes of continuous measurements, and significant interference from ascorbic acid, dopamine, and other neurochemicals was observed. While FSCV demonstrated capability for NO detection under controlled conditions, poor selectivity, electrode stability issues, and interference from endogenous compounds limit its practical application for real-time NO monitoring in complex biological environments. Alternative electrode materials and surface modifications may improve performance for future neurochemical applications.

title: Carbon nanofiber electrode for neurochemical monitoring.
abstract: Real-time monitoring of neurochemical dynamics in the central nervous system remains a significant challenge due to limitations in electrode sensitivity, selectivity, and biocompatibility. Traditional carbon fiber microelectrodes, while widely used for fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV), exhibit suboptimal signal-to-noise ratios and limited detection capabilities for low-concentration neurotransmitters. Here we report the development and characterization of novel carbon nanofiber (CNF) electrodes for enhanced neurochemical detection. CNF electrodes were fabricated by chemical vapor deposition of carbon onto tungsten substrates, resulting in highly ordered nanostructured surfaces with increased electroactive area. Electrochemical characterization using cyclic voltammetry revealed 3.2-fold greater sensitivity for dopamine detection compared to conventional carbon fiber electrodes, with detection limits of 15 nM. In vitro validation demonstrated selective detection of dopamine in the presence of common interferents including ascorbic acid and 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid. CNF electrodes exhibited stable performance over 200 consecutive voltammetric scans with less than 5% signal degradation. In vivo microdialysis experiments in anesthetized rats showed successful real-time monitoring of stimulated dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens following electrical stimulation of the medial forebrain bundle. The enhanced sensitivity enabled detection of pharmacologically-induced changes in extracellular dopamine concentrations as low as 25 nM following quinpirole administration. These findings demonstrate that CNF electrodes represent a significant advancement in neurochemical sensing technology, offering improved sensitivity and temporal resolution for investigating neurotransmitter dynamics in both physiological and pathological conditions.

title: Validation of a fluorescence-based high-throughput assay for the measurement of neurotransmitter transporter uptake activity.
abstract: Neurotransmitter transporters play critical roles in synaptic transmission by regulating extracellular concentrations of dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine. Current methods for measuring transporter uptake activity, including radioligand binding assays and electrophysiological techniques, are often labor-intensive and unsuitable for high-throughput screening applications. We developed and validated a fluorescence-based assay using the fluorescent substrate 4-(4-(dimethylamino)styryl)-N-methylpyridinium (ASP+) to quantify uptake activity of the dopamine transporter (DAT), serotonin transporter (SERT), and norepinephrine transporter (NET) in a 96-well plate format. Human embryonic kidney 293 cells stably transfected with individual transporters were incubated with ASP+ (5 μM) for 30 minutes at 37°C, and fluorescence accumulation was measured using a microplate reader (excitation 485 nm, emission 590 nm). The assay demonstrated excellent linearity (r² > 0.95) across a range of cell densities and incubation times. Kinetic analysis revealed apparent Km values of 8.2 ± 1.4 μM for DAT, 12.1 ± 2.1 μM for SERT, and 9.7 ± 1.8 μM for NET. Selective inhibitors including GBR12909, fluoxetine, and desipramine produced concentration-dependent inhibition with IC50 values consistent with literature reports. The assay showed high reproducibility (CV < 10%) and was successfully used to screen a library of 480 compounds, identifying novel transporter modulators. This fluorescence-based method provides a robust, cost-effective alternative to radioligand assays for neurotransmitter transporter research and drug discovery applications.

title: Microdialysis applications in neuroscience.
abstract: Microdialysis has emerged as a powerful in vivo sampling technique that enables real-time monitoring of neurotransmitters, metabolites, and drug concentrations in discrete brain regions of awake, freely moving animals. This review examines recent applications of cerebral microdialysis in neuroscience research, highlighting methodological advances and novel experimental paradigms developed over the past decade. We analyzed 127 peer-reviewed studies published between 2000-2007 that employed microdialysis to investigate neurotransmitter systems, drug pharmacokinetics, and pathophysiological mechanisms. Major applications included monitoring dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, and glutamate release in response to pharmacological challenges, behavioral paradigms, and disease models. Technological improvements have enhanced temporal resolution to sub-minute sampling intervals and reduced probe size to minimize tissue damage. Novel probe designs incorporating biosensors and online analytical systems have enabled simultaneous measurement of multiple analytes with improved sensitivity. Microdialysis studies have provided critical insights into addiction mechanisms, demonstrating drug-induced alterations in mesolimbic dopamine signaling, and have elucidated neurotransmitter changes in models of Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and stroke. Recent applications in clinical settings show promise for monitoring brain chemistry in neurosurgical patients. Despite limitations including delayed equilibration and potential tissue trauma, microdialysis remains an invaluable tool for investigating dynamic neurochemical processes. Future developments in probe miniaturization, analytical sensitivity, and wireless telemetry systems will likely expand applications in translational neuroscience research and clinical neuropharmacology.

title: From systems biology to dynamical neuropharmacology: proposal for a new methodology.
abstract: Traditional neuropharmacological approaches have primarily relied on reductionist methodologies that examine isolated molecular targets, often failing to capture the complex, dynamic interactions within neural networks. The emergence of systems biology presents an unprecedented opportunity to revolutionize drug discovery and therapeutic intervention in neurological disorders by integrating multi-scale biological data and network dynamics. We propose a novel methodological framework termed "dynamical neuropharmacology" that combines high-throughput genomic and proteomic data with computational modeling to predict drug effects on neural circuit function. Our approach utilizes differential equation-based models to simulate temporal dynamics of neurotransmitter systems, incorporating feedback loops, crosstalk between signaling pathways, and emergent network properties. We applied this methodology to analyze the effects of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) on hippocampal-prefrontal circuits using integrated datasets from microarray expression profiles, protein-protein interaction networks, and electrophysiological recordings from rat brain slices. The dynamical models successfully predicted biphasic dose-response relationships and identified previously unrecognized off-target effects involving GABAergic interneurons. Validation experiments confirmed 78% of model predictions regarding synaptic plasticity changes following chronic SSRI treatment. Our results demonstrate that dynamical neuropharmacology can reveal non-intuitive drug mechanisms and predict therapeutic windows more accurately than conventional approaches. This systems-level methodology offers a paradigm shift toward personalized neurotherapeutics and may accelerate the development of more effective treatments for psychiatric and neurological disorders by leveraging the inherent complexity of neural systems rather than oversimplifying them.

title: Cocaine increases stimulated dopamine release more in periadolescent than adult rats.
abstract: Adolescence represents a critical period of brain development characterized by ongoing maturation of dopaminergic systems. Previous studies have demonstrated age-related differences in cocaine's behavioral effects, but the neurochemical basis for these differences remains poorly understood. The present study examined whether cocaine differentially affects dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens of periadolescent (postnatal day 35) versus adult (postnatal day 70) male Sprague-Dawley rats. Using in vivo microdialysis, we measured extracellular dopamine concentrations following acute cocaine administration (10 mg/kg, i.p.) in freely moving animals. Baseline dopamine levels were significantly higher in periadolescent compared to adult rats (2.8 ± 0.3 vs 1.9 ± 0.2 nM, p<0.05). Cocaine administration produced robust increases in extracellular dopamine in both age groups; however, the magnitude of increase was significantly greater in periadolescent animals. Peak dopamine levels reached 847% ± 76% of baseline in periadolescent rats compared to 542% ± 48% in adults (p<0.01). The enhanced dopaminergic response in periadolescent animals persisted for approximately 2 hours longer than in adults. Western blot analysis revealed significantly lower dopamine transporter (DAT) protein expression in periadolescent nucleus accumbens tissue compared to adults, which may partially explain the age-related differences in cocaine sensitivity. These findings suggest that the developing adolescent brain exhibits heightened vulnerability to cocaine's neurochemical effects, potentially contributing to increased addiction liability during this developmental period. Understanding these age-related differences may inform prevention strategies and treatment approaches for adolescent substance abuse.

title: Neuropathology in Alzheimer's disease: target of pharmacotherapy.
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) represents the most prevalent form of dementia, affecting approximately 4.5 million Americans and characterized by progressive cognitive decline, memory impairment, and eventual loss of functional independence. The neuropathological hallmarks of AD include extracellular amyloid-β (Aβ) plaques, intracellular neurofibrillary tangles composed of hyperphosphorylated tau protein, synaptic dysfunction, and widespread neuronal death. Current pharmacotherapeutic approaches primarily target the cholinergic deficit through acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) and the glutamatergic system via NMDA receptor antagonism (memantine). This review synthesizes current understanding of AD neuropathology and evaluates emerging therapeutic targets based on analysis of recent clinical trials, postmortem studies, and preclinical research from 1995-2005. Evidence demonstrates that while cholinesterase inhibitors provide modest symptomatic benefits in mild-to-moderate AD, they do not address underlying disease mechanisms. Promising therapeutic targets include β-secretase and γ-secretase enzymes involved in amyloid processing, tau kinases responsible for neurofibrillary tangle formation, inflammatory mediators, and oxidative stress pathways. Clinical trials investigating Aβ immunotherapy, secretase inhibitors, and anti-inflammatory agents show preliminary efficacy in slowing cognitive decline. However, the complex, multifactorial nature of AD neuropathology suggests that combination therapies targeting multiple pathways may be necessary for disease modification. Future pharmacological interventions must address both symptomatic management and disease-modifying mechanisms to meaningfully impact the trajectory of this devastating neurodegenerative disorder.
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Nurse Practitioners

title: Extracting the basal extracellular dopamine concentrations from the evoked responses: re-analysis of the dopamine kinetics.
abstract: Accurate determination of basal extracellular dopamine concentrations in vivo remains challenging due to the rapid uptake kinetics and low steady-state levels of this neurotransmitter. Traditional microdialysis methods suffer from temporal resolution limitations, while fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) primarily measures evoked dopamine release. Here we present a novel analytical approach to extract basal dopamine concentrations from electrically-evoked dopamine responses recorded using FSCV in the striatum of anesthetized rats. Our method employs a modified Michaelis-Menten kinetic model that incorporates both uptake and release parameters to back-calculate baseline concentrations from the decay phase of evoked dopamine transients. We validated this approach using simultaneous FSCV and microdialysis recordings (n=8 rats) and compared results with conventional no-net-flux microdialysis measurements. Mathematical modeling of 127 evoked dopamine responses revealed basal concentrations of 18.3 ± 4.7 nM, which correlated strongly with microdialysis values (r=0.82, p<0.001). The method showed high reproducibility (CV=12.4%) and was sensitive to pharmacological manipulations, including dopamine transporter blockade with nomifensine (10 mg/kg i.p.) and tyrosine hydroxylase inhibition with α-methyl-p-tyrosine (200 mg/kg i.p.). Re-analysis of existing datasets using this approach suggests that previous estimates of dopamine uptake parameters may have been confounded by inaccurate baseline assumptions. This methodology provides a valuable tool for determining physiological dopamine concentrations with high temporal resolution and may improve our understanding of dopaminergic neurotransmission in health and disease states.

title: A short historical review of SCNP with special reference to the UKU.
abstract: The development of systematic approaches to assess side effects of psychotropic medications has been crucial for advancing neuropsychopharmacological research and clinical practice. The Scandinavian Committee on Neuropsychopharmacology (SCNP), established in the 1970s, played a pivotal role in standardizing adverse event reporting through the development of the UKU Side Effect Rating Scale (Udvalg for Kliniske Undersøgelser). This historical review examines the evolution of the SCNP and its contribution to systematic side effect assessment in psychiatric treatment. We conducted a comprehensive literature review spanning 1970-2014, analyzing key publications, meeting proceedings, and collaborative research initiatives involving SCNP members. Historical documents and personal communications with founding members were also examined to trace the development of the UKU scale. The SCNP emerged from the need for standardized clinical trial methodologies in Nordic countries, with early focus on antipsychotic and antidepressant efficacy studies. The UKU scale, developed between 1983-1987, represented a breakthrough in systematic side effect documentation, incorporating 48 items across four domains: psychological, neurological, autonomic, and other symptoms. The scale demonstrated excellent inter-rater reliability (κ=0.74-0.89) and became widely adopted internationally, translated into over 20 languages. Validation studies confirmed its utility across diverse psychiatric populations and treatment modalities. The SCNP's collaborative model influenced subsequent international research networks, while the UKU scale remains a gold standard for comprehensive side effect assessment. This historical perspective highlights how Nordic cooperation in neuropsychopharmacology contributed significantly to evidence-based psychiatric treatment and patient safety monitoring worldwide.

title: A novel method for monitoring the cell surface expression of heteromeric protein complexes in dispersed neurons and acute hippocampal slices.
abstract: The study of heteromeric protein complexes at neuronal cell surfaces has been limited by technical challenges in monitoring their native expression and localization in physiologically relevant preparations. Traditional approaches using overexpression systems or isolated membrane preparations often fail to recapitulate the endogenous stoichiometry and trafficking of multi-subunit complexes. Here we describe a novel dual-labeling approach combining biotinylation-based surface labeling with proximity ligation assays to specifically detect heteromeric complexes at the plasma membrane of live neurons. Primary hippocampal neurons (DIV 14-21) and acute hippocampal slices (300 μm) from P14-P21 rats were surface-biotinylated using membrane-impermeant NHS-SS-biotin at 4°C, followed by fixation and proximity ligation using antibodies against different subunits of target complexes. We validated this method using NMDA receptors containing GluN1/GluN2A subunits and GABAA receptors containing α1/β2/γ2 subunits. Surface biotinylation efficiency exceeded 85% as confirmed by streptavidin-fluorophore labeling, while proximity ligation signals showed >95% colocalization with surface markers. The method successfully detected developmental changes in NMDA receptor subunit composition and revealed distinct subcellular distributions of GABAA receptor subtypes in CA1 pyramidal neurons. Background signal in non-biotinylated controls remained below 5% of experimental values. This approach provides a powerful tool for investigating the cell surface expression and localization of endogenous heteromeric protein complexes in native neuronal preparations, enabling more physiologically relevant studies of synaptic protein trafficking and organization.

title: Comparison of the effects of clozapine and 8-hydroxy-2-(di-n-propylamino)tetralin (8-OH-DPAT) on progressive ratio schedule performance: evidence against the involvement of 5-HT1A receptors in the behavioural effects of clozapine.
abstract:
**Background:** Clozapine, an atypical antipsychotic with unique clinical efficacy, exhibits complex pharmacological properties including interactions with multiple neurotransmitter systems. While its therapeutic effects are primarily attributed to dopaminergic and serotonergic mechanisms, the specific contribution of 5-HT1A receptor activity to clozapine's behavioral profile remains unclear. Progressive ratio (PR) schedules, which measure motivation and reward sensitivity, provide a valuable tool for examining the behavioral effects of psychoactive compounds.

**Methods:** Male Sprague-Dawley rats were trained on a PR schedule for food reinforcement until stable baseline performance was achieved. Separate groups received acute injections of clozapine (1.25, 2.5, 5.0 mg/kg, i.p.), the selective 5-HT1A receptor agonist 8-OH-DPAT (0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2 mg/kg, s.c.), or vehicle. Breakpoint (highest ratio completed) and response rate were measured as primary outcome variables.

**Results:** Clozapine dose-dependently decreased breakpoint and response rate, with significant effects observed at 2.5 and 5.0 mg/kg. In contrast, 8-OH-DPAT produced a biphasic response pattern: low doses (0.025-0.05 mg/kg) slightly increased breakpoint, while higher doses (0.1-0.2 mg/kg) markedly decreased performance. The temporal pattern and magnitude of behavioral disruption differed substantially between compounds. Pretreatment with the 5-HT1A antagonist WAY-100635 blocked 8-OH-DPAT effects but did not alter clozapine's actions.

**Conclusions:** These findings demonstrate distinct behavioral profiles for clozapine and 8-OH-DPAT on PR performance, suggesting that 5-HT1A receptor activation does not significantly contribute to clozapine's motivational effects. The results challenge previous hypotheses regarding 5-HT1A involvement in atypical antipsychotic action and support alternative mechanisms underlying clozapine's unique pharmacological properties.

title: [The history of CINP between 1958 and 2000].
abstract: The Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (CINP) was established in 1958 as the first international organization dedicated to advancing the scientific understanding of neuropsychopharmacology. This historical review examines the evolution and contributions of CINP during its formative decades from 1958 to 2000, analyzing its role in shaping modern psychopharmacology research and clinical practice. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of CINP archival materials, including congress proceedings, membership records, scientific publications, and correspondence from founding members.
