Historical documents were obtained from the CINP archives and supplemented with interviews of key figures who participated in the organization's early development. Between 1958 and 2000, CINP organized 22 biennial world congresses across four continents, facilitating international collaboration among 3,847 researchers from 67 countries. The organization played a pivotal role in establishing standardized methodologies for psychopharmacological research, promoting the development of rating scales for psychiatric disorders, and fostering the emergence of biological psychiatry as a distinct discipline. Notable achievements included the creation of task forces on depression, schizophrenia, and anxiety disorders, which produced influential consensus statements that guided clinical practice. CINP also contributed significantly to the training of young investigators through fellowship programs and mentorship initiatives. The period from 1958 to 2000 represents a foundational era for CINP, during which the organization established itself as the premier international forum for neuropsychopharmacology research. CINP's contributions during this period were instrumental in transforming psychopharmacology from an emerging field into a mature scientific discipline with global impact.
journal: Nihon shinkei seishin yakurigaku zasshi = Japanese journal of psychopharmacology

title: Pharmacological imaging as a tool to visualise dopaminergic neurotoxicity.
abstract: Dopaminergic neurotoxicity underlies several neurodegenerative disorders, yet current methods for visualizing and quantifying neuronal damage in vivo remain limited. This study evaluated the utility of pharmacological imaging approaches combining positron emission tomography (PET) with selective radioligands to assess dopaminergic neurotoxicity in real-time. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) received unilateral striatal injections of 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA, 8 μg) or vehicle. Animals underwent longitudinal PET imaging using [¹¹C]raclopride for dopamine D2 receptor availability and [¹¹C]PE2I for dopamine transporter density at 1, 3, 7, and 28 days post-lesion. Behavioral assessment via amphetamine-induced rotation and post-mortem tyrosine hydroxylase immunohistochemistry served as validation measures. 6-OHDA lesioning produced progressive decreases in striatal [¹¹C]PE2I binding, with 45±8% reduction at day 3 and 78±6% reduction at day 28 compared to contralateral striatum (p<0.001). [¹¹C]Raclopride binding showed initial increases (125±12% at day 3) followed by normalization by day 28, consistent with denervation supersensitivity. Strong correlations were observed between [¹¹C]PE2I binding and both rotational behavior (r=-0.84, p<0.001) and tyrosine hydroxylase-positive cell counts (r=0.79, p<0.001). These findings demonstrate that pharmacological PET imaging provides sensitive, quantitative assessment of dopaminergic neurotoxicity progression. The temporal resolution and non-invasive nature of this approach offers significant advantages for monitoring disease progression and evaluating neuroprotective interventions in preclinical models of Parkinson's disease and related disorders.

title: Succi nervorum: a brief history of neurochemistry.
abstract: The study of neurochemistry has evolved dramatically since the early anatomical descriptions of nervous tissue, yet the historical development of our understanding of neural chemical processes remains poorly documented in contemporary literature. This review examines the progression of neurochemical research from the initial observations of "succi nervorum" (nerve juices) in the 18th century through the establishment of modern neurotransmitter theory. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of primary historical sources, including original manuscripts, laboratory notebooks, and published communications from 1750 to 1970, supplemented by bibliometric analysis of key publications in neurochemistry. Our investigation reveals three distinct phases in neurochemical discovery: the pre-electrical period (1750-1850) characterized by humoral theories of nerve function, the electrophysiological era (1850-1920) dominated by purely electrical models of neural transmission, and the modern chemical transmission period (1920-1970) establishing neurotransmitter mechanisms. Critical developments included du Bois-Reymond's early speculation about chemical transmission in 1877, Elliott's recognition of sympathetic nerve-adrenergic relationships in 1904, and Dale's definitive acetylcholine studies beginning in 1914. The transition from vague concepts of "nerve essences" to precise molecular mechanisms required fundamental advances in analytical chemistry, electrophysiology, and pharmacology. Understanding this historical progression illuminates current challenges in neuropsychopharmacology and provides context for emerging fields such as neuroproteomics and metabolomics, suggesting that historical perspective remains essential for interpreting contemporary neurochemical research paradigms.

title: The neuropsychopharmacology of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder.
abstract: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) affects approximately 3-7% of school-age children and represents one of the most common neurodevelopmental disorders. While stimulant medications remain first-line treatments, the precise neurobiological mechanisms underlying ADHD pathophysiology and therapeutic response remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes current knowledge of the neuropsychopharmacological basis of ADHD, examining evidence from neuroimaging, neurochemical, and genetic studies. Converging evidence implicates dysregulation of catecholamine neurotransmission, particularly dopamine and norepinephrine systems, in ADHD symptomatology. Neuroimaging studies demonstrate altered activity in prefrontal cortex, anterior cingulate cortex, and striatal regions, areas rich in catecholamine innervation and critical for executive function and attention. Dopamine transporter (DAT) and receptor polymorphisms show modest associations with ADHD risk and treatment response. Stimulant medications, including methylphenidate and amphetamines, enhance synaptic dopamine and norepinephrine availability through distinct mechanisms of action on monoamine transporters. Non-stimulant alternatives such as atomoxetine selectively inhibit norepinephrine reuptake, supporting the role of noradrenergic dysfunction in ADHD. Recent findings suggest additional involvement of cholinergic and GABAergic systems in attention regulation. While current pharmacological treatments effectively reduce core symptoms in 70-80% of patients, individual variability in treatment response remains substantial. Future research integrating pharmacogenomic approaches, advanced neuroimaging techniques, and novel therapeutic targets may yield more personalized and effective interventions for this heterogeneous disorder.

title: Roles of transmembrane domain 2 and the first intracellular loop in human noradrenaline transporter function: pharmacological and SCAM analysis.
abstract: The human noradrenaline transporter (hNET) mediates reuptake of synaptic noradrenaline and is a primary target for antidepressants and psychostimulants. While transmembrane domain 2 (TM2) and the first intracellular loop (ICL1) are predicted to be critical for transporter function based on homology models, their specific roles in substrate recognition and transport remain poorly characterized. To investigate the functional importance of these regions, we employed site-directed mutagenesis combined with substituted cysteine accessibility method (SCAM) analysis in HEK293 cells stably expressing hNET constructs. Individual residues within TM2 (positions 118-142) and ICL1 (positions 143-156) were systematically mutated to cysteine and analyzed for noradrenaline uptake activity, ligand binding affinity, and accessibility to membrane-impermeant sulfhydryl reagents. Cysteine substitution at positions Ile119, Phe124, and Ser128 within TM2 resulted in 60-85% reduction in noradrenaline uptake without affecting surface expression levels. SCAM analysis revealed that residues Ile119 and Ser128 are accessible to MTSEA modification from the extracellular side, suggesting their location near the substrate permeation pathway. In ICL1, mutation of Arg148 and Glu151 significantly impaired both uptake function and desipramine binding affinity (Kd increased 4-8 fold). Methanethiosulfonate accessibility patterns indicated that ICL1 undergoes conformational changes during the transport cycle. These findings demonstrate that TM2 contributes directly to the substrate translocation pathway while ICL1 is essential for proper transporter conformational dynamics, providing new insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying noradrenaline transport and antidepressant action.

title: Treatment of attention deficits in neurological disorders.
abstract: Attention deficits are prevalent across numerous neurological conditions, including traumatic brain injury, stroke, dementia, and neurodegenerative disorders, significantly impacting patients' functional outcomes and quality of life. While stimulant medications have shown efficacy in attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, their utility in neurological populations remains less established. This review examines current pharmacological approaches for treating attention deficits in neurological disorders, analyzing evidence from clinical trials and case series published between 1990-2005. We systematically reviewed literature on cholinesterase inhibitors, dopaminergic agents, noradrenergic medications, and novel compounds in patients with acquired attention deficits. Cholinesterase inhibitors, particularly donepezil and rivastigmine, demonstrated modest improvements in sustained attention tasks in patients with mild cognitive impairment and early dementia, with effect sizes ranging from 0.3-0.5. Methylphenidate showed promise in traumatic brain injury populations, improving processing speed and divided attention in 60-70% of patients in small controlled trials. Modafinil emerged as a well-tolerated alternative, particularly effective for vigilance deficits in multiple sclerosis and post-stroke populations. Atomoxetine demonstrated benefits in executive attention domains across various neurological conditions, though with variable response rates. However, most studies suffered from small sample sizes, heterogeneous patient populations, and limited follow-up periods. Current evidence suggests that pharmacological interventions can provide meaningful improvements in attention deficits associated with neurological disorders, but treatment selection should be individualized based on specific attention domains affected, underlying pathophysiology, and patient tolerance. Further large-scale, randomized controlled trials are needed to establish definitive treatment guidelines.

title: Emerging trends in in vivo neurochemical monitoring by microdialysis.
abstract: Microdialysis has emerged as a pivotal technique for real-time monitoring of neurotransmitter dynamics in freely moving animals, providing unprecedented insights into neurochemical processes underlying behavior and disease. Recent technological advances have significantly enhanced the temporal resolution, sensitivity, and analytical capabilities of this approach. We review current innovations in microdialysis methodology, including the development of novel probe designs with improved biocompatibility, miniaturized sampling systems, and enhanced perfusion protocols that minimize tissue damage while maximizing recovery rates. Integration with advanced analytical techniques such as capillary electrophoresis, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, and biosensor arrays has enabled simultaneous monitoring of multiple neurotransmitters including dopamine, serotonin, GABA, and glutamate with sub-minute temporal resolution. Recent applications demonstrate the utility of these approaches in characterizing neurochemical alterations in models of Parkinson's disease, depression, and addiction, revealing dynamic patterns of neurotransmitter release that correlate with specific behavioral phenotypes. Furthermore, the combination of microdialysis with optogenetics and pharmacogenetics has provided new opportunities to establish causal relationships between neurochemical changes and neural circuit function. These methodological advances position microdialysis as an increasingly powerful tool for translational neuroscience research, offering potential applications in drug development and personalized medicine approaches for neuropsychiatric disorders. Future developments focusing on wireless telemetry and chronic monitoring capabilities promise to further expand the clinical relevance of in vivo neurochemical assessment.

title: Promising avenues of therapeutics for bipolar illness.
abstract: Bipolar disorder affects approximately 1-2% of the global population and represents a leading cause of disability worldwide, yet current pharmacological treatments remain suboptimal for many patients. Mood stabilizers such as lithium, valproate, and carbamazepine provide therapeutic benefit but are associated with significant side effects and limited efficacy in treatment-resistant cases. This review examines emerging therapeutic approaches that target novel neurobiological pathways implicated in bipolar disorder pathophysiology. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of preclinical and clinical studies published between 2000-2008, focusing on glutamatergic modulators, neuroprotective agents, and circadian rhythm regulators. Glutamate NMDA receptor antagonists, including ketamine and memantine, demonstrate rapid antimanic and antidepressant effects in preliminary trials, with response rates of 60-75% within 72 hours. Neuroprotective compounds such as riluzole and N-acetylcysteine show promise in stabilizing mood episodes through modulation of glial function and oxidative stress pathways. Additionally, melatonin receptor agonists and light therapy interventions targeting circadian dysregulation yield significant improvements in sleep architecture and mood stability. Neuroimaging studies reveal that these novel interventions normalize aberrant activity in prefrontal-limbic circuits implicated in emotional regulation. These findings suggest that therapeutics targeting glutamatergic neurotransmission, cellular resilience mechanisms, and biological rhythm disturbances represent promising avenues for bipolar disorder treatment. Future research should prioritize large-scale randomized controlled trials to establish efficacy and safety profiles of these innovative approaches, potentially revolutionizing bipolar disorder management through precision medicine strategies.

title: The neurochemistry of agitation in Alzheimer's disease: a systematic review.
abstract: Agitation represents one of the most prevalent and distressing behavioral symptoms in Alzheimer's disease (AD), affecting up to 70% of patients and contributing significantly to caregiver burden and institutionalization. Despite its clinical importance, the underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly understood. We conducted a systematic review to synthesize current knowledge on the neurochemical basis of agitation in AD. We searched PubMed, EMBASE, and PsycINFO databases from inception to December 2017 for studies investigating neurochemical correlates of agitation in AD patients. Studies were included if they examined cerebrospinal fluid, neuroimaging, or post-mortem brain tissue analyses in relation to validated agitation measures. Twenty-eight studies met inclusion criteria, encompassing 2,847 AD patients. Converging evidence implicated dysregulation of multiple neurotransmitter systems. Reduced cholinergic activity, particularly in frontal and temporal regions, was consistently associated with increased agitation severity across neuroimaging and post-mortem studies. Dopaminergic dysfunction, evidenced by decreased striatal dopamine transporter binding and altered D2 receptor density, correlated with agitated behaviors. Serotonergic abnormalities, including reduced 5-HT1A receptor binding and elevated inflammatory markers affecting serotonin metabolism, were linked to aggression and irritability. Additionally, increased noradrenergic activity and elevated cortisol levels suggested heightened stress responses in agitated patients. GABAergic deficits in frontal regions were associated with disinhibited behaviors. These findings suggest agitation in AD results from complex interactions between cholinergic depletion, monoaminergic imbalances, and stress system activation. Understanding these mechanisms may inform targeted therapeutic approaches for managing this challenging symptom.

title: Integrative biological analysis for neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: Neuropsychopharmacological research has traditionally relied on reductionist approaches focusing on single molecular targets, yet psychiatric disorders emerge from complex interactions across multiple biological scales. This study presents a comprehensive integrative framework combining genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and behavioral phenotyping to elucidate drug mechanisms in mood disorders. We employed a systems biology approach using chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS) in male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) treated with fluoxetine (10 mg/kg, 21 days) or vehicle. RNA sequencing of prefrontal cortex and hippocampus revealed 1,247 differentially expressed genes following CUMS, with fluoxetine normalizing 68% of stress-induced changes. Quantitative proteomics identified 312 altered proteins, including key synaptic and metabolic regulators. Integration with genome-wide association study data from major depressive disorder patients showed significant overlap (p<0.001) between rodent stress-response genes and human risk variants. Network analysis revealed hub proteins in BDNF, mTOR, and inflammatory signaling pathways. Behavioral improvements correlated with restoration of synaptic protein expression (r=0.72, p<0.01) and normalization of microglial activation markers. Machine learning algorithms successfully predicted treatment response using multi-omics signatures with 84% accuracy. This integrative approach identified novel biomarker panels and demonstrated that antidepressant efficacy results from coordinated molecular network restoration rather than single-target modulation. These findings provide a roadmap for precision neuropsychopharmacology and highlight the necessity of multi-scale biological integration in understanding psychiatric drug mechanisms. Future therapeutic development should embrace systems-level complexity to improve treatment outcomes.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: In vivo monitoring of extracellular glutamate in the brain with a microsensor.
abstract: Dysregulation of glutamate neurotransmission has been implicated in numerous neurological disorders including stroke, epilepsy, and neurodegenerative diseases. Current methods for measuring extracellular glutamate in vivo, such as microdialysis, suffer from poor temporal resolution and tissue damage. We developed a novel glutamate-selective microsensor for real-time monitoring of extracellular glutamate concentrations in the living brain. The microsensor consists of a platinum microelectrode (25 μm diameter) coated with glutamate oxidase and poly(o-phenylenediamine) to confer selectivity and minimize interferents. The sensor was calibrated in vitro and demonstrated linear response to glutamate (10 μM to 1 mM, r² = 0.98) with minimal interference from ascorbic acid, dopamine, and GABA. Response time was < 2 seconds with detection limit of 5 μM. In anesthetized Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8), microsensors were stereotaxically implanted into the striatum and hippocampus. Baseline glutamate levels were 12.3 ± 2.1 μM in striatum and 8.7 ± 1.8 μM in hippocampus. Local application of potassium chloride (100 mM) produced rapid glutamate increases of 340 ± 45% in striatum and 280 ± 38% in hippocampus, returning to baseline within 5 minutes. Focal ischemia induced by middle cerebral artery occlusion resulted in sustained glutamate elevation (peak: 850 ± 120% of baseline) in the ischemic penumbra. This microsensor technology provides unprecedented temporal resolution for monitoring glutamate dynamics and offers valuable insights into excitotoxic mechanisms underlying neurological pathology.

title: Neurochemically distinct myenteric neurone populations containing calbindin have specific distribution patterns around the circumference of the gastric corpus.
abstract: The myenteric plexus of the stomach contains neurochemically diverse populations of neurons that regulate gastric motility and secretion. While calbindin-D28k (CB) has been identified as a calcium-binding protein expressed in enteric neurons, the precise distribution and neurochemical phenotypes of CB-immunoreactive neurons in different regions of the gastric wall remain poorly characterized. We investigated the circumferential distribution patterns of CB-containing myenteric neurons in the gastric corpus of adult Wistar rats using immunohistochemistry and confocal microscopy. Whole-mount preparations of the gastric corpus were processed for CB immunoreactivity and double-labeled with markers for cholinergic (choline acetyltransferase), nitrergic (neuronal nitric oxide synthase), and peptidergic (vasoactive intestinal peptide, substance P) neurons. CB-immunoreactive neurons comprised 23.4 ± 2.1% of total myenteric neurons and exhibited distinct regional variations around the gastric circumference. The density of CB-positive neurons was significantly higher along the greater curvature (31.2 ± 3.4%) compared to the lesser curvature (16.8 ± 2.2%) and anterior wall (19.1 ± 2.6%). Double-labeling revealed three neurochemically distinct CB-positive populations: CB/choline acetyltransferase-positive neurons (42% of CB cells), CB/neuronal nitric oxide synthase-positive neurons (28%), and CB/vasoactive intestinal peptide-positive neurons (18%). Each subpopulation showed specific circumferential distribution patterns, with CB/cholinergic neurons predominating along the greater curvature. These findings demonstrate that calbindin-containing myenteric neurons comprise functionally distinct subpopulations with region-specific distributions in the gastric corpus, suggesting specialized roles in the neural control of gastric function that vary according to anatomical location.

title: The rotational model and microdialysis: Significance for dopamine signalling, clinical studies, and beyond.
abstract: The rotational model of Parkinson's disease, first described by Ungerstedt in the 1970s, remains a cornerstone for investigating dopaminergic dysfunction and therapeutic interventions. When combined with in vivo microdialysis, this model provides unprecedented insights into real-time neurotransmitter dynamics within the basal ganglia circuitry. This review synthesizes three decades of research utilizing the rotational model-microdialysis paradigm to examine dopamine signalling mechanisms, evaluate therapeutic strategies, and translate findings to clinical applications. The unilateral 6-hydroxydopamine lesion creates a reproducible model of asymmetric dopaminergic denervation, enabling quantitative assessment of rotational behavior while simultaneously monitoring extracellular dopamine, its metabolites, and other neurotransmitters through microdialysis probes implanted in the striatum. Studies demonstrate that amphetamine-induced rotation correlates inversely with residual dopamine content, while L-DOPA-induced rotation reflects the degree of denervation supersensitivity. Microdialysis reveals that effective therapeutic interventions, including L-DOPA, dopamine agonists, and deep brain stimulation, normalize aberrant dopamine release patterns and reduce rotational asymmetry. Furthermore, this combined approach has elucidated the roles of dopamine transporters, vesicular storage, and glial uptake in parkinsonian pathophysiology. The model's predictive validity for clinical outcomes has been demonstrated through successful translation of dopamine replacement strategies, continuous drug delivery systems, and neuroprotective agents from preclinical studies to human trials. Contemporary applications extend beyond Parkinson's disease to include investigations of addiction, schizophrenia, and neurodevelopmental disorders, establishing this methodology as an indispensable tool for advancing our understanding of dopaminergic neurotransmission and developing targeted therapeutic interventions.

title: Microdialysis in freely moving mice: determination of acetylcholine, serotonin and noradrenaline release in galanin transgenic mice.
abstract: The neuropeptide galanin has been implicated in the modulation of cholinergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic neurotransmission, yet its precise effects on neurotransmitter release in vivo remain poorly characterized. We employed intracerebral microdialysis in freely moving mice to investigate the effects of galanin overexpression on basal and stimulated release of acetylcholine (ACh), serotonin (5-HT), and noradrenaline (NA) in the hippocampus and frontal cortex. Male transgenic mice overexpressing galanin under the platelet-derived growth factor-β promoter and wild-type littermates were implanted with CMA/7 microdialysis probes. Following a 24-hour recovery period, dialysate samples were collected at 20-minute intervals and analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection for monoamines and radioenzymatic assay for ACh. Potassium-evoked neurotransmitter release was assessed by perfusion with 100 mM KCl for 20 minutes. Galanin transgenic mice exhibited significantly reduced basal ACh release in both hippocampus (-32%, p<0.01) and frontal cortex (-28%, p<0.05) compared to controls. Basal 5-HT release was decreased by 24% in the hippocampus (p<0.05) but unchanged in frontal cortex. NA release remained unaffected in both regions. Potassium-stimulated ACh release was markedly attenuated in transgenic mice (hippocampus: -45%, frontal cortex: -38%, both p<0.01), while 5-HT and NA responses were preserved. These findings demonstrate that chronic galanin overexpression selectively impairs cholinergic neurotransmission in vivo, providing insights into galanin's role in cognitive dysfunction and supporting its potential as a therapeutic target for cholinergic disorders.

title: S32504, a novel naphtoxazine agonist at dopamine D3/D2 receptors: III. Actions in models of potential antidepressive and anxiolytic activity in comparison with ropinirole.
abstract: The novel naphtoxazine derivative S32504 displays preferential affinity for dopamine D3 vs D2 receptors and shows promise as a potential therapeutic agent. This study evaluated the antidepressant and anxiolytic properties of S32504 in comparison with the D2/D3 agonist ropinirole using established rodent behavioral models. Male Wistar rats and Swiss mice received S32504 (0.16-10.0 mg/kg, s.c.), ropinirole (0.63-10.0 mg/kg, s.c.), or vehicle 30 min prior to testing. In the forced swim test, S32504 dose-dependently reduced immobility time in both rats (ED50 = 2.1 mg/kg) and mice (ED50 = 1.8 mg/kg), with efficacy comparable to imipramine (15 mg/kg, i.p.). Ropinirole showed weaker antidepressant-like activity (ED50 = 6.4 mg/kg in rats). In the elevated plus-maze, S32504 (0.63-2.5 mg/kg) significantly increased open arm entries and time spent in open arms, indicating anxiolytic effects. These actions were blocked by the D3-preferring antagonist S33084 (10 mg/kg, s.c.) but only partially attenuated by the D2-selective antagonist L-741,626 (10 mg/kg, s.c.). Ropinirole produced less pronounced anxiolytic effects that were fully reversed by both antagonists. S32504 did not impair motor coordination at active doses, unlike ropinirole which caused rotational behavior at higher doses. These findings suggest that S32504's preferential D3 receptor activation underlies its robust antidepressant and anxiolytic properties, supporting the therapeutic potential of selective D3 receptor agonists for mood and anxiety disorders.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: Determination of high-affinity choline uptake (HACU) and choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) activity in the same population of cultured cells.
abstract: Cholinergic neurotransmission relies on the coordinated function of high-affinity choline uptake (HACU) and choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) activity, yet technical limitations have prevented simultaneous measurement of these parameters in individual cell populations. We developed a novel dual-assay protocol to assess both HACU and ChAT activity sequentially in the same cultured cholinergic cells, enabling direct correlation of these functionally related processes. Primary septal cholinergic neurons were cultured from embryonic day 18 rat fetuses and maintained for 14-21 days in vitro. HACU was measured using [³H]choline uptake assays with hemicholinium-3 inhibition to confirm specificity, followed by cell harvesting and ChAT activity determination using [¹⁴C]acetyl-CoA as substrate. Muscarinic receptor modulation was examined using carbachol and atropine treatments. The dual-assay protocol successfully quantified both parameters without methodological interference. Baseline HACU rates averaged 45.2 ± 3.8 pmol/min/mg protein, while ChAT activity measured 12.7 ± 1.9 nmol/h/mg protein. Carbachol treatment (100 μM) significantly increased both HACU (168% of control) and ChAT activity (142% of control), effects blocked by atropine pretreatment. Strong positive correlation was observed between HACU and ChAT activity across individual culture wells (r = 0.78, p < 0.001). This methodology provides a valuable tool for investigating cholinergic function at the cellular level and demonstrates coordinated regulation of choline uptake and acetylcholine synthesis mechanisms. The approach will facilitate studies of cholinergic dysfunction in neurodegenerative diseases and therapeutic drug development.

title: Extracellular trypsin increases ASIC1a selectivity for monovalent versus divalent cations.
abstract: Acid-sensing ion channels (ASICs) are proton-gated cation channels widely distributed throughout the nervous system and implicated in synaptic transmission, mechanosensation, and pathological conditions including ischemic brain injury. ASIC1a, the predominant brain isoform, exhibits relatively low selectivity between monovalent and divalent cations under physiological conditions. Previous studies have shown that extracellular proteases can modulate ASIC function, though the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. Here we investigated whether trypsin treatment affects the ionic selectivity of recombinant human ASIC1a channels expressed in Chinese hamster ovary (CHO) cells using whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology. Cells were treated with bovine pancreatic trypsin (10-100 μg/ml) for 2-15 minutes before recording acid-evoked currents (pH 6.0) in solutions containing various concentrations of Na+, K+, Ca2+, or Mg2+. Relative permeability ratios were calculated from reversal potential measurements using the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation. Trypsin treatment (50 μg/ml, 5 min) significantly increased PNa+/PCa2+ from 2.1 ± 0.3 to 8.7 ± 1.2 (n=12, p<0.001) and PK+/PMg2+ from 1.8 ± 0.4 to 6.9 ± 0.8 (n=10, p<0.001). The effect was dose- and time-dependent, reversible upon trypsin removal, and blocked by the serine protease inhibitor PMSF. Current amplitude and pH sensitivity remained unchanged. These findings demonstrate that extracellular trypsin selectively enhances ASIC1a permeability to monovalent cations while reducing divalent cation flux, suggesting proteolytic modification of channel selectivity filter regions. This mechanism may be physiologically relevant during tissue injury when both acidosis and protease release occur simultaneously.

title: The psychopharmacological management of RLS in psychiatric conditions: a review of the literature.
abstract: Restless legs syndrome (RLS) is a common neurological disorder characterized by uncomfortable sensations in the legs and an irresistible urge to move them, particularly during periods of rest. The prevalence of RLS is significantly elevated in patients with psychiatric conditions, including depression, anxiety disorders, and attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, yet optimal treatment strategies for this comorbid population remain poorly defined. This comprehensive literature review examined current evidence regarding psychopharmacological management of RLS in psychiatric patients. A systematic search of PubMed, CINAHL, and PsycINFO databases was conducted covering publications from 2000-2012, yielding 847 initial articles, of which 42 met inclusion criteria for analysis. Studies consistently demonstrated that traditional psychiatric medications, particularly selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and tricyclic antidepressants, can exacerbate RLS symptoms in up to 65% of patients. Conversely, dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) showed efficacy for both RLS and depressive symptoms, with response rates of 70-85% for RLS improvement and significant mood benefits in 60% of patients. Anticonvulsants, especially gabapentin and pregabalin, demonstrated dual benefits for RLS and anxiety symptoms while showing minimal psychiatric side effects. Iron supplementation proved particularly effective in psychiatric patients with concurrent iron deficiency, present in 40% of the reviewed cases. These findings suggest that integrated treatment approaches considering both psychiatric and RLS symptomatology may optimize patient outcomes. Psychiatric nurses should routinely screen for RLS symptoms and collaborate with prescribers to select medications that address both conditions simultaneously.
journal: Journal of the American Psychiatric Nurses Association

title: Evaluation of an osmotic pump for microdialysis sampling in an awake and untethered rat.
abstract: Microdialysis is a widely used technique for monitoring neurotransmitter dynamics in freely moving animals, but conventional systems require tethering to external pumps, which can alter natural behavior and introduce movement artifacts. We developed and evaluated a miniaturized osmotic pump system for delivering perfusion fluid in awake, untethered rats during microdialysis sampling. The system consisted of a subcutaneously implanted ALZET osmotic pump (Model 2ML1) connected to a microdialysis probe in the striatum via PE-10 tubing, with dialysate collection facilitated by a small, lightweight collection vial attached to the animal. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with microdialysis probes and either tethered to conventional syringe pumps or fitted with the osmotic pump system. Basal dopamine levels, locomotor activity, and probe recovery rates were compared between groups over 24-hour periods. The osmotic pump system maintained stable flow rates (1.0 ± 0.1 μL/min) and produced comparable dopamine recovery (18.2 ± 2.1% vs 19.7 ± 1.8% for conventional systems). Untethered animals showed significantly increased spontaneous locomotor activity and more natural circadian behavioral patterns compared to tethered controls. Amphetamine-induced dopamine release profiles were similar between groups, confirming system reliability for pharmacological studies. The osmotic pump approach offers several advantages including reduced stress artifacts, elimination of tubing occlusion from animal movement, and the ability to conduct truly naturalistic behavioral studies. This system represents a valuable improvement for long-term microdialysis studies requiring unrestricted animal movement.

title: Neuropsychopharmacology and the genetics of schizophrenia: a history of the diagnosis of schizophrenia.
abstract: The diagnostic conceptualization of schizophrenia has undergone substantial evolution since Kraepelin's initial description of dementia praecox, with profound implications for contemporary neuropsychopharmacological research and genetic studies. This historical review examines the progression of diagnostic criteria from classical descriptive approaches through modern operational definitions, and their impact on pharmacogenetic investigations. We analyzed key diagnostic frameworks from 1896 to 2000, including contributions by Kraepelin, Bleuler, Schneider, and the DSM/ICD systems, correlating these with major pharmacological discoveries and genetic findings. The transition from Kraepelin's deterioration-based model to Bleuler's broader concept of "split mind" fundamentally altered research populations and therapeutic approaches. Schneider's first-rank symptoms provided operational clarity but potentially narrowed the phenotypic scope for genetic studies. The introduction of chlorpromazine in the 1950s marked the beginning of rational psychopharmacology, while subsequent dopamine hypothesis development directly influenced both diagnostic conceptualization and drug discovery. Modern neuropsychopharmacological research has been significantly shaped by DSM-III's operational criteria, enabling more homogeneous study populations for pharmacogenetic investigations. However, this diagnostic refinement may have inadvertently excluded important phenotypic variants relevant to treatment response and genetic susceptibility. The evolution of schizophrenia diagnosis reflects the complex interplay between clinical observation, pharmacological discovery, and genetic understanding. Current neuropsychopharmacological research would benefit from reconsidering broader phenotypic definitions that encompass the full spectrum of schizophrenia-related disorders, particularly as pharmacogenetic studies reveal the heterogeneous nature of treatment response within traditional diagnostic boundaries.

title: Applying small quantities of multiple compounds to defined locations of in vitro brain slices.
abstract: Local application of pharmacological agents to specific brain regions in slice preparations has traditionally been limited by drug diffusion and imprecise targeting. We developed a novel microinjection system for simultaneous delivery of multiple compounds to defined anatomical locations within acute brain slices. The apparatus consists of borosilicate micropipettes (tip diameter 2-5 μm) mounted on independent micromanipulators, connected to a pressure-controlled delivery system capable of ejecting 10-100 picoliter volumes. Adult rat hippocampal slices (400 μm) were prepared using standard techniques and maintained in artificial cerebrospinal fluid. Fluorescent dyes (rhodamine, FITSC) were used to validate spatial precision and diffusion characteristics. We tested the system by applying GABA (100 μM), bicuculline (20 μM), and glutamate (50 μM) to CA1 pyramidal cell dendrites while recording from soma via whole-cell patch clamp. Confocal microscopy confirmed drug application within 50 μm radius of target sites with minimal cross-contamination between injection sites separated by >200 μm. Electrophysiological recordings demonstrated rapid onset of pharmacological effects (200-500 ms) with duration proportional to injection volume. Sequential application of antagonists effectively blocked agonist responses, confirming maintained drug potency. This method enables precise spatiotemporal control over multiple drug applications in brain slices, facilitating investigation of localized neurotransmitter actions and receptor interactions. The system provides superior spatial resolution compared to bath application while maintaining tissue viability and allowing reversible pharmacological manipulations essential for mechanistic studies of synaptic transmission and plasticity.

title: On the prescribing analyst.
abstract: The integration of psychopharmacological interventions within psychoanalytic practice has generated considerable debate regarding therapeutic boundaries and analytic neutrality. This study examined the clinical outcomes and transference dynamics when psychoanalysts directly prescribe psychotropic medications to their analysands, compared to traditional referral models. We conducted a retrospective analysis of 127 patients in psychoanalysis over a 5-year period (2008-2013), comparing three groups: patients whose analysts prescribed medications directly (n=41), patients referred to psychiatrists for medication management (n=43), and medication-naive controls (n=43). Treatment outcomes were assessed using standardized measures including the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale, Global Assessment of Functioning, and detailed process notes analyzing transference manifestations. Patients with prescribing analysts demonstrated significantly greater treatment retention (89% vs. 67%, p<0.01) and showed equivalent symptomatic improvement compared to the referral group. However, distinct transference patterns emerged, including heightened idealization (43% vs. 18%) and increased somatic preoccupations during sessions (62% vs. 31%). Process analysis revealed that the dual role created unique opportunities for exploring the patient's relationship to dependency and authority, though it also generated more complex countertransference reactions in analysts. These findings suggest that the prescribing analyst model may enhance therapeutic alliance and retention while introducing novel transference configurations that require careful analytic attention. The integration of prescribing responsibilities appears clinically viable when accompanied by rigorous self-analysis and appropriate training in psychopharmacology.

title: Serotonin receptors of type 6 (5-HT6): what can we expect from them?
abstract: Serotonin 5-HT6 receptors represent one of the most recently discovered members of the serotonin receptor family and have emerged as promising therapeutic targets for cognitive disorders and neuropsychiatric conditions. Despite their restricted distribution primarily in the central nervous system, particularly in brain regions associated with learning and memory such as the hippocampus, cortex, and striatum, their precise physiological roles remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding 5-HT6 receptor pharmacology, distribution, and therapeutic potential based on preclinical and early clinical findings through 2011. 5-HT6 receptors are positively coupled to adenylyl cyclase and exhibit high constitutive activity, distinguishing them from other serotonin receptor subtypes. Accumulating evidence suggests these receptors modulate cholinergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic neurotransmission, positioning them as important regulators of cognitive function. Selective 5-HT6 receptor antagonists, including SB-271046, SB-399885, and Ro 04-6790, have demonstrated procognitive effects in various animal models of learning and memory, including novel object recognition, Morris water maze, and attentional set-shifting tasks. Additionally, these compounds show promise in models of Alzheimer's disease, schizophrenia, and obesity. Early clinical trials with 5-HT6 antagonists have yielded mixed but encouraging results in cognitive enhancement and weight reduction. The unique pharmacological profile and therapeutic potential of 5-HT6 receptors suggest they may offer novel approaches for treating cognitive impairment associated with aging, dementia, and psychiatric disorders, warranting continued investigation and development of selective modulators.

title: Pharmacotherapy of alcohol dependence: a review of the clinical data.
abstract: Alcohol dependence represents a major public health concern affecting millions worldwide, yet therapeutic options remain limited and show variable efficacy. This review examines the current state of pharmacological interventions for alcohol dependence based on available clinical trial data through 2003. We conducted a comprehensive literature search of randomized controlled trials evaluating FDA-approved medications and emerging therapeutic agents for alcohol dependence treatment. Three medications currently hold FDA approval: disulfiram, naltrexone, and acamprosate. Disulfiram demonstrates efficacy primarily through supervised administration, with studies showing 60-80% abstinence rates under monitored conditions versus 20-30% with unsupervised use. Naltrexone, an opioid receptor antagonist, consistently reduces heavy drinking days by 15-25% and craving scores by 20-40% across multiple placebo-controlled trials involving over 2,000 patients. Acamprosate shows promise in European studies, with abstinence rates of 35-45% versus 15-25% for placebo at 6-12 months, though US trial results have been mixed. Emerging agents including topiramate, ondansetron, and baclofen show preliminary efficacy in small-scale studies. Combination therapy approaches demonstrate enhanced outcomes compared to monotherapy in several trials. Patient factors including genetic polymorphisms, particularly in ALDH2 and OPRM1 genes, appear to influence treatment response. Current pharmacotherapies represent important advances but show modest effect sizes, highlighting the need for personalized treatment approaches and continued development of novel therapeutic targets including glutamate and GABA systems.

title: Toward a neuropsychological theory of antidepressant drug action: increase in positive emotional bias after potentiation of norepinephrine activity.
abstract: Major depressive disorder is characterized by negative cognitive biases and blunted positive emotional processing, yet the mechanisms by which antidepressants restore normal emotional cognition remain poorly understood. While current theories focus primarily on monoamine receptor changes, emerging evidence suggests that antidepressants may act by modulating emotional information processing biases. This study examined whether acute potentiation of norepinephrine activity influences emotional bias in healthy volunteers using a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled design. Twenty-four healthy adults received either reboxetine (4mg) or placebo before completing computerized tasks measuring emotional word recognition, facial expression categorization, and attentional bias toward positive versus negative stimuli. Participants also underwent functional magnetic resonance imaging during an emotional faces paradigm. Compared to placebo, reboxetine administration significantly increased recognition accuracy for positive emotional words (p<0.01) and shifted the threshold for categorizing ambiguous facial expressions toward happy rather than sad interpretations (p<0.05). Attentional bias measures revealed faster orienting toward positive compared to negative images following reboxetine treatment (p<0.05). Neuroimaging data showed increased amygdala-prefrontal coupling during positive emotion processing and reduced amygdala reactivity to negative faces in the reboxetine group. These findings demonstrate that acute norepinephrine enhancement produces measurable shifts toward positive emotional bias in healthy individuals, suggesting that antidepressants may exert therapeutic effects by correcting the negative cognitive biases characteristic of depression rather than simply through direct mood elevation. This neuropsychological model of antidepressant action has important implications for understanding therapeutic mechanisms and optimizing treatment approaches.

title: Looking at neurotransmitters in the microscope.
abstract: The visualization of neurotransmitter distribution and dynamics within neural circuits has remained a fundamental challenge in neurobiology, with traditional electrophysiological and biochemical approaches providing limited spatial resolution. Recent advances in fluorescence microscopy and genetically-encoded biosensors have opened new avenues for direct observation of neurotransmitter release and uptake at synaptic terminals. Here we review current methodologies for microscopic detection of major neurotransmitters including glutamate, GABA, dopamine, and acetylcholine in live brain tissue. We examined studies utilizing FM dyes for vesicle tracking, pH-sensitive indicators for exocytosis detection, and newly developed FRET-based sensors that provide real-time measurements of extracellular neurotransmitter concentrations with submicron spatial resolution. Two-photon microscopy has enabled imaging depths of up to 300 μm in acute brain slices, while confocal approaches have achieved temporal resolution below 100 milliseconds for synaptic events. Quantitative analysis reveals that these optical methods can detect neurotransmitter changes in the nanomolar to micromolar range, comparable to physiologically relevant concentrations. Correlation with patch-clamp recordings demonstrates high fidelity between optical signals and synaptic currents (r > 0.85). These microscopic approaches have revealed previously unappreciated heterogeneity in neurotransmitter release probability across individual synapses and have identified novel patterns of extrasynaptic spillover. The integration of optical neurotransmitter detection with cellular resolution represents a paradigm shift toward understanding chemical transmission in intact neural networks, providing unprecedented insights into synaptic plasticity mechanisms and neurotransmitter dysfunction in neurological disorders.

title: Can psychopharmacological treatment change personality traits in patients with panic disorder?
abstract:
**Background:** Panic disorder is characterized by recurrent panic attacks and anticipatory anxiety that significantly impair quality of life. While psychopharmacological interventions effectively reduce panic symptoms, their potential impact on personality traits remains poorly understood. This study investigated whether successful pharmacological treatment of panic disorder induces changes in personality dimensions as measured by standardized assessments.

**Methods:** Sixty-four patients with DSM-IV-TR diagnosed panic disorder were enrolled in a 12-week, open-label trial with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs). Participants received either sertraline (n=32, 50-150mg/day) or paroxetine (n=32, 20-40mg/day). Personality traits were assessed using the NEO Five-Factor Inventory (NEO-FFI) at baseline, week 6, and week 12. Panic severity was evaluated using the Panic Disorder Severity Scale (PDSS) and Clinical Global Impression-Severity (CGI-S). Treatment response was defined as ≥40% reduction in PDSS scores.

**Results:** Fifty-seven patients completed the study (89% completion rate). Treatment responders (n=42, 73.7%) demonstrated significant reductions in neuroticism scores (baseline: 42.3±8.1 vs. week 12: 35.7±7.4, p<0.001) and increases in extraversion (28.4±6.9 vs. 32.1±7.2, p=0.002) and conscientiousness (31.2±5.8 vs. 34.6±6.1, p=0.001). Non-responders showed no significant personality changes. Changes in neuroticism correlated significantly with PDSS improvement (r=0.58, p<0.001).

**Conclusions:** Successful pharmacological treatment of panic disorder was associated with favorable personality trait modifications, particularly reduced neuroticism and enhanced extraversion and conscientiousness. These findings suggest that effective psychopharmacological intervention may produce broader psychological benefits beyond symptom reduction, potentially reflecting neuroplasticity-mediated changes in underlying temperamental dimensions.
journal: Revista brasileira de psiquiatria (Sao Paulo, Brazil : 1999)

title: Current and Future Neuropharmacological Options for the Treatment of Essential Tremor.
abstract: Essential tremor (ET) is the most prevalent movement disorder, affecting approximately 1% of the global population and up to 5% of individuals over 65 years of age. Despite its high prevalence, current pharmacological treatments remain limited and often provide suboptimal symptom control. This comprehensive review examines the current neuropharmacological landscape for ET management and explores emerging therapeutic targets. Traditional first-line treatments include propranolol, a non-selective β-adrenergic antagonist, and primidone, an anticonvulsant, which demonstrate moderate efficacy in 50-70% of patients but are associated with significant side effects limiting long-term adherence. Second-line options such as topiramate, gabapentin, and benzodiazepines show variable effectiveness with considerable inter-individual variability. Recent mechanistic insights into ET pathophysiology have identified cerebellar dysfunction and altered GABAergic/glutamatergic neurotransmission as key therapeutic targets. Novel approaches under investigation include T-type calcium channel modulators, particularly zonisamide and 3,4-diaminopyridine, which have shown promising results in early-phase clinical trials. GABA-A receptor positive allosteric modulators and metabotropic glutamate receptor antagonists represent additional emerging strategies. Furthermore, personalized medicine approaches utilizing pharmacogenomic profiling may optimize treatment selection and dosing strategies. Deep brain stimulation remains the most effective intervention for medication-refractory cases, though surgical risks limit widespread application. The integration of precision medicine principles with novel pharmacological targets offers considerable promise for improving therapeutic outcomes in ET patients, potentially transforming the treatment paradigm for this common yet undertreated neurological condition.

title: S32504, a novel naphtoxazine agonist at dopamine D3/D2 receptors: I. Cellular, electrophysiological, and neurochemical profile in comparison with ropinirole.
abstract: Dopamine D3 receptors represent promising therapeutic targets for neurological disorders, yet selective pharmacological tools remain limited. This study characterized S32504, a novel naphtoxazine derivative, as a potential D3/D2 receptor agonist and compared its profile to the established antiparkinsonian agent ropinirole. Binding affinity was determined using radioligand displacement assays in CHO cells expressing human D2 and D3 receptors. Functional activity was assessed via cAMP inhibition assays and patch-clamp electrophysiology in primary rat striatal neurons. In vivo neurochemical effects were evaluated using microdialysis in freely moving rats. S32504 displayed high affinity for D3 receptors (Ki = 0.8 nM) with 15-fold selectivity over D2 receptors, compared to ropinirole which showed 3-fold D3 selectivity. In cAMP assays, S32504 acted as a full agonist at D3 receptors (EC50 = 2.1 nM) and partial agonist at D2 receptors. Electrophysiological recordings revealed that S32504 (0.1-10 μM) dose-dependently reduced neuronal firing rates and inhibited spontaneous excitatory postsynaptic currents, effects blocked by the D2/D3 antagonist sulpiride. Microdialysis studies demonstrated that S32504 (0.1-1.0 mg/kg s.c.) decreased extracellular dopamine levels in the nucleus accumbens more potently than ropinirole, consistent with preferential D3 autoreceptor activation. These findings establish S32504 as a novel D3-preferring agonist with distinct pharmacological properties compared to ropinirole, suggesting potential therapeutic applications in conditions associated with dopaminergic dysfunction where enhanced D3 receptor selectivity may provide clinical advantages.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: Name that neurotransmitter: using music to teach psychopharmacology concepts.
abstract:
Background: Teaching psychopharmacology concepts to nursing students presents significant pedagogical challenges, particularly in helping students memorize neurotransmitter functions, pathways, and associated medications. Traditional lecture-based approaches often result in poor retention and difficulty applying concepts to clinical practice. Music-based learning strategies have demonstrated efficacy in various educational contexts but have not been systematically evaluated for psychopharmacology education.

Methods: We implemented a novel educational intervention called "Name that Neurotransmitter" with 84 undergraduate nursing students enrolled in a psychopharmacology course. Students were randomly assigned to either a traditional lecture group (n=42) or a music-enhanced learning group (n=42). The intervention group participated in sessions where neurotransmitter functions, synthesis pathways, and drug mechanisms were taught through original songs set to familiar melodies. Pre- and post-intervention assessments measured knowledge retention using a validated 50-item psychopharmacology examination. Student satisfaction and perceived learning effectiveness were evaluated using Likert-scale surveys.

Results: Students in the music-enhanced group demonstrated significantly higher post-test scores compared to controls (82.3 ± 8.1 vs. 74.6 ± 9.4, p<0.001). Retention testing at 4 weeks showed sustained knowledge improvement in the intervention group (79.1 ± 7.8 vs. 71.2 ± 8.9, p<0.01). Music group participants reported higher engagement levels (4.6 ± 0.5 vs. 3.2 ± 0.7, p<0.001) and increased confidence in applying psychopharmacology concepts clinically.

Conclusions: Music-based teaching strategies significantly enhance learning and retention of complex psychopharmacology concepts among nursing students, suggesting broader applications for neuroscience education in healthcare curricula.

title: Commentary: physician-scientist's frustrations fester.
abstract: The declining population of physician-scientists in neuropharmacology research represents a critical threat to translational medicine and drug discovery for neuropsychiatric disorders. This commentary examines the systemic barriers contributing to career dissatisfaction among physician-scientists in academic neuropharmacology from 2005-2008. We conducted structured interviews with 47 physician-scientists at various career stages across 12 academic medical centers, supplemented by analysis of NIH funding patterns and publication metrics. Participants reported increasing administrative burdens (89%), inadequate protected research time (83%), and insufficient institutional support for translational research (76%) as primary sources of frustration. Clinical revenue pressures resulted in mean research time declining from 65% to 41% over the study period. Early-career physician-scientists expressed particular concern about competing with PhD scientists for limited grant funding while maintaining clinical responsibilities. Senior investigators noted decreased mentorship capacity due to expanding administrative duties. NIH R01 success rates for physician-scientists in neuropharmacology fell from 23% to 17% during this period, compared to relatively stable rates for PhD investigators. Publication output per physician-scientist decreased by 28%, while time from bench discovery to clinical application increased significantly. These findings highlight the urgent need for institutional reforms including enhanced protected time policies, dedicated physician-scientist funding mechanisms, and reduced administrative burdens. Without systematic intervention, the continued exodus of physician-scientists threatens to create an irreversible gap between neuroscience discovery and clinical application, ultimately compromising patient care and therapeutic innovation in neuropsychiatric medicine.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Metabolic changes detected by proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy in vivo and in vitro in a murin model of Parkinson's disease, the MPTP-intoxicated mouse.
abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is characterized by selective dopaminergic neuronal loss in the substantia nigra, yet the underlying metabolic alterations remain poorly understood. The 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP) mouse model reproduces key features of PD pathology and provides an opportunity to investigate neurochemical changes associated with dopaminergic degeneration. We employed proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (¹H-MRS) to characterize metabolic profiles in MPTP-treated C57BL/6 mice both in vivo and in vitro. Adult male mice received four subcutaneous injections of MPTP (20 mg/kg) at 2-hour intervals. In vivo ¹H-MRS was performed at 9.4T focusing on striatal regions at 1, 7, and 21 days post-treatment. Ex vivo high-resolution magic angle spinning ¹H-MRS was conducted on striatal and nigral tissue extracts. MPTP treatment resulted in significant reductions in N-acetylaspartate (NAA) levels in the striatum by day 7 (23% decrease, p<0.01), which persisted through day 21. Lactate concentrations were elevated 2-fold at day 1 but normalized by day 7. In vitro analysis revealed decreased glutamate and increased taurine levels in both striatal and nigral regions. Creatine and choline-containing compounds showed region-specific alterations, with striatal creatine decreasing by 18% while nigral levels remained unchanged. These findings demonstrate that MPTP-induced dopaminergic toxicity produces distinct metabolic signatures detectable by ¹H-MRS, with NAA reduction likely reflecting neuronal dysfunction or loss. The metabolic profiles provide insights into the temporal progression of MPTP toxicity and may serve as biomarkers for monitoring disease progression and therapeutic interventions in experimental PD.

title: Measurement of plasmalemmal dopamine transport, vesicular dopamine transport, and K(+)-stimulated dopamine release in frozen rat brain tissue.
abstract: Dopamine neurotransmission plays a critical role in motor function, reward processing, and cognition, with dysfunction implicated in Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, and addiction. Traditional methods for measuring dopaminergic function require fresh tissue preparations, limiting experimental flexibility and multicenter studies. We developed and validated a novel protocol for simultaneous assessment of three key dopaminergic parameters—plasmalemmal dopamine transporter (DAT) activity, vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) function, and potassium-stimulated dopamine release—using frozen rat brain tissue. Striatal tissue from adult Sprague-Dawley rats was sectioned at 10 μm thickness and stored at -80°C for up to 12 weeks. DAT activity was measured using [³H]dopamine uptake assays in the presence and absence of GBR12909. VMAT2 function was assessed via [³H]dopamine accumulation into synaptic vesicles isolated from thawed tissue homogenates, with reserpine as a selective inhibitor. K⁺-stimulated dopamine release was evaluated using high-performance liquid chromatography following depolarization with 30 mM KCl. Frozen tissue retained 82±5% of DAT activity, 76±7% of VMAT2 function, and 71±9% of K⁺-stimulated release capacity compared to fresh preparations (n=8-12 per group). All three measures remained stable for at least 8 weeks of storage and showed excellent correlation with fresh tissue values (r>0.85, p<0.001). This methodology provides a practical approach for comprehensive dopaminergic assessment in archived samples, facilitating retrospective studies and enabling standardized protocols across multiple laboratories investigating dopamine-related neurological and psychiatric disorders.

title: Antipsychotic drugs: evolving mechanisms of action with improved therapeutic benefits.
abstract: The therapeutic landscape of antipsychotic medications has undergone significant evolution since the introduction of chlorpromazine, with newer agents demonstrating improved efficacy and reduced adverse effects through novel mechanisms of action. This review examines the pharmacological profiles of second-generation antipsychotics and their enhanced therapeutic benefits compared to conventional neuroleptics. We analyzed the receptor binding profiles, clinical efficacy data, and adverse effect profiles of risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, and ziprasidone relative to haloperidol and chlorpromazine using published clinical trials and preclinical studies from 1990-2003. Second-generation antipsychotics demonstrate broader receptor antagonism beyond dopamine D2 receptors, including significant activity at serotonin 5-HT2A, 5-HT2C, α1-adrenergic, and histamine H1 receptors. This polypharmacological approach correlates with superior efficacy in treating negative symptoms and cognitive deficits in schizophrenia, with effect sizes ranging from 0.3-0.6 compared to conventional agents. Additionally, the reduced D2 receptor occupancy threshold (65-70% vs 80-85% for typical antipsychotics) and enhanced 5-HT2A/D2 antagonism ratio contribute to significantly lower incidence of extrapyramidal side effects and tardive dyskinesia. Clinical studies demonstrate 40-60% reduction in treatment discontinuation rates and improved quality of life measures. These findings support the hypothesis that balanced neurotransmitter modulation, rather than selective dopamine blockade, provides superior therapeutic outcomes in psychotic disorders. The evolving understanding of antipsychotic mechanisms continues to guide rational drug development toward more effective and tolerable treatments for schizophrenia and related conditions.
journal: Current drug targets. CNS and neurological disorders

title: Psychoneuroimmunology meets neuropsychopharmacology: translational implications of the impact of inflammation on behavior.
abstract: The bidirectional communication between the immune system and central nervous system has emerged as a critical pathway linking peripheral inflammation to neuropsychiatric disorders. Growing evidence indicates that pro-inflammatory cytokines can access the brain and alter neurotransmitter metabolism, neuroendocrine function, and synaptic plasticity, ultimately leading to behavioral changes reminiscent of depression and anxiety. This review synthesizes current understanding of inflammatory mechanisms underlying mood disorders and examines therapeutic implications for neuropsychopharmacology. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of preclinical and clinical studies investigating cytokine-induced behavioral alterations and anti-inflammatory treatment strategies. Animal models utilizing lipopolysaccharide, cytokine administration, and chronic stress paradigms consistently demonstrate that peripheral immune activation produces depressive-like behaviors including anhedonia, social withdrawal, and cognitive impairment. Mechanistically, inflammatory mediators disrupt monoaminergic signaling through enhanced tryptophan catabolism via indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase, reduced tetrahydrobiopterin availability, and increased monoamine transporter expression. Clinical studies reveal elevated inflammatory markers in subsets of patients with major depression, particularly those with treatment-resistant illness. Importantly, anti-inflammatory interventions including cyclooxygenase-2 inhibitors, tumor necrosis factor-α antagonists, and minocycline show therapeutic efficacy in both preclinical models and preliminary clinical trials. These findings suggest that inflammatory biomarkers may identify patient subgroups most likely to respond to anti-inflammatory augmentation strategies. The convergence of psychoneuroimmunology and neuropsychopharmacology offers promising avenues for developing personalized medicine approaches targeting immune-mediated mechanisms in psychiatric disorders, potentially improving outcomes for treatment-refractory patients.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: The stanley neuropathology consortium integrative database: a novel, web-based tool for exploring neuropathological markers in psychiatric disorders and the biological processes associated with abnormalities of those markers.
abstract: The Stanley Neuropathology Consortium has generated extensive postmortem brain tissue data from individuals with schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, major depression, and matched controls over the past decade. However, the integration and accessibility of these diverse datasets has remained a significant challenge for the research community. We developed a comprehensive, web-based integrative database to facilitate exploration of neuropathological markers across psychiatric disorders and enable systematic investigation of associated biological processes. The database incorporates quantitative neuropathological data from over 400 subjects, including measurements of neuronal density, glial cell populations, synaptic proteins, and inflammatory markers across multiple brain regions. We implemented advanced search algorithms and data visualization tools, allowing users to query specific markers, compare across diagnostic groups, and examine correlations with demographic and clinical variables. The platform integrates pathway analysis tools to identify enriched biological processes associated with observed neuropathological abnormalities. Our analysis revealed significant alterations in oligodendrocyte populations in the prefrontal cortex across all three psychiatric disorders, with the most pronounced deficits observed in schizophrenia. Microglial activation markers showed disorder-specific patterns, with elevated CD68 immunoreactivity in bipolar disorder and increased HLA-DR expression in major depression. Pathway analysis identified disrupted myelination, synaptic transmission, and inflammatory response processes as common themes across disorders. This integrative database represents a valuable resource for the psychiatric research community, enabling data-driven hypothesis generation and facilitating the identification of convergent neuropathological mechanisms underlying major mental illness.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Psychopharmacology of the endocannabinoids: far beyond anandamide.
abstract: The endocannabinoid system has emerged as a critical neuromodulatory network, yet research has predominantly focused on anandamide (AEA) while neglecting other bioactive lipid mediators. This review synthesizes current understanding of the broader endocannabinoid pharmacology beyond classical eCB signaling. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of recent literature examining 2-arachidonoylglycerol (2-AG), palmitoylethanolamide (PEA), oleoylethanolamide (OEA), and N-arachidonoyldopamine (NADA) across preclinical models of neuropsychiatric disorders. Unlike AEA's predominant CB1 receptor activity, 2-AG demonstrates distinct biosynthetic pathways via diacylglycerol lipase and enhanced synaptic plasticity modulation. PEA and OEA, while lacking direct cannabinoid receptor affinity, exhibit potent anti-inflammatory and feeding regulatory properties through peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-α (PPAR-α) and GPR119 mechanisms. NADA uniquely activates both cannabinoid and vanilloid receptor systems, suggesting novel therapeutic targets. Pharmacokinetic analyses reveal differential enzymatic degradation patterns, with fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH) and monoacylglycerol lipase (MAGL) displaying substrate selectivity that impacts tissue-specific endocannabinoid tone. Behavioral studies demonstrate that selective modulation of non-AEA pathways produces distinct anxiolytic, analgesic, and cognitive effects, often with improved therapeutic windows compared to direct CB1 agonism. These findings highlight the endocannabinoid system's remarkable pharmacological diversity and suggest that therapeutic interventions targeting specific eCB metabolic pathways may offer superior clinical outcomes with reduced psychoactive liability compared to traditional cannabinoid approaches.

title: Stereochemistry in clinical medicine: a neurological perspective.
abstract: Stereochemistry plays a crucial role in neuropharmacology, yet its clinical implications remain incompletely understood in neurological practice. Many neurotropic medications exist as stereoisomers with potentially different pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties. We conducted a comprehensive review of stereochemical considerations in commonly prescribed neurological medications and analyzed clinical outcomes data from 847 patients treated at our tertiary neurology center between 1998-2002. We examined racemic versus enantiopure formulations of key drug classes including anticonvulsants, anti-parkinsonian agents, and psychotropic medications, correlating stereochemical properties with therapeutic efficacy and adverse event profiles. Our analysis revealed significant differences in clinical outcomes between stereoisomeric formulations. S-citalopram demonstrated superior efficacy compared to racemic citalopram in treating post-stroke depression (response rate 73% vs 58%, p<0.05). Levodopa combined with carbidopa showed enhanced bioavailability when the L-enantiomer was used exclusively versus racemic preparations. Among anticonvulsants, the S-enantiomer of phenytoin exhibited reduced cognitive side effects while maintaining seizure control in 89% of patients switched from racemic formulations. Pharmacokinetic modeling revealed that hepatic metabolism, protein binding, and blood-brain barrier penetration varied significantly between enantiomers of the same compound. These findings suggest that stereochemical optimization of neurotropic medications could improve therapeutic outcomes while minimizing adverse effects. Greater attention to stereochemistry in drug development and clinical practice may enhance precision in neurological therapeutics, though larger controlled trials are needed to establish definitive treatment guidelines.
journal: Journal of clinical neuroscience : official journal of the Neurosurgical Society of Australasia

title: Beyond the reward hypothesis: alternative functions of nucleus accumbens dopamine.
abstract: The nucleus accumbens (NAcc) has been predominantly studied within the framework of reward processing and addiction, where dopamine release is viewed as encoding reward prediction error and motivational salience. However, accumulating evidence suggests that NAcc dopamine serves broader computational functions beyond hedonic processing. This review synthesizes recent findings challenging the traditional reward hypothesis and proposes alternative roles for accumbens dopamine signaling. We examined literature from 2000-2005 encompassing electrophysiological recordings, microdialysis studies, and behavioral pharmacology experiments in rodents and non-human primates. Lesion studies utilizing 6-OHDA and pharmacological manipulations with dopamine receptor antagonists were analyzed alongside emerging optogenetic approaches. Our analysis reveals that NAcc dopamine responds robustly to aversive stimuli, uncertainty, and novelty independent of reward value. Dopaminergic signaling in the shell and core subregions exhibits distinct temporal dynamics during action selection, with phasic release preceding motor initiation rather than following reward delivery. Furthermore, dopamine depletion impairs effort-based decision making and behavioral switching without abolishing reward preference. These findings indicate that NAcc dopamine functions as a general motivational signal that energizes behavior and facilitates adaptive responding to environmental demands. Rather than simply encoding "wanting" or reward prediction, accumbens dopamine appears to regulate behavioral activation, attention allocation, and cognitive flexibility. This reconceptualization has important implications for understanding psychiatric disorders characterized by motivational deficits, including depression and schizophrenia, and suggests novel therapeutic targets beyond traditional reward-based frameworks.

title: Neurochemical and physiological correlates of a critical period of respiratory development in the rat.
abstract: Respiratory control undergoes dramatic reorganization during early postnatal development, with disruptions during critical periods potentially leading to sudden infant death syndrome and other breathing disorders. This study investigated the neurochemical and physiological changes occurring during the critical period of respiratory development in Sprague-Dawley rats between postnatal days 10-14 (P10-P14). We recorded respiratory frequency, tidal volume, and hypoxic ventilatory responses using whole-body plethysmography in conscious pups. Brainstem sections from the pre-Bötzinger complex and rostral ventrolateral medulla were analyzed for neurotransmitter receptor expression using quantitative immunohistochemistry and in situ hybridization. GABA-A and glycine receptor subunit mRNA levels were measured by real-time PCR. Respiratory frequency decreased significantly from P10 to P14 (184±12 to 156±8 breaths/min, p<0.01), while tidal volume increased proportionally with body weight. Hypoxic ventilatory responses showed biphasic changes, with peak sensitivity at P12 followed by depression at P14. GABA-A receptor α1 subunit expression increased 2.3-fold between P10-P14 (p<0.001), while glycine receptor α1 subunits showed more modest increases (1.4-fold, p<0.05). Serotonin 2A receptor density decreased by 35% during this period (p<0.01). These findings demonstrate that the P10-P14 period represents a critical developmental window characterized by enhanced inhibitory neurotransmission and altered chemosensitivity in brainstem respiratory circuits. The coordinated changes in GABAergic inhibition and serotoninergic modulation may contribute to the vulnerability of respiratory control during early postnatal life.

title: Neuropharmacology of performance monitoring.
abstract: Performance monitoring, the ability to detect and respond to errors and conflicts during goal-directed behavior, represents a fundamental cognitive control mechanism mediated by the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) and associated neural networks. While extensive neuroimaging and electrophysiological studies have characterized the neural correlates of performance monitoring, including the error-related negativity (ERN) and conflict-related activity, the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding the neurochemical basis of performance monitoring, with particular emphasis on dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems. We examine evidence from pharmacological challenge studies in humans and animals, focusing on how acute manipulations of neurotransmitter systems affect behavioral and neural indices of error detection and conflict monitoring. Dopamine appears to play a critical role in signaling prediction errors and modulating ACC activity during performance monitoring tasks, with D2 receptor blockade consistently impairing error detection. GABAergic modulation through benzodiazepine administration reduces conflict-related ACC activation and impairs behavioral adjustments following errors. Glutamatergic NMDA receptor antagonism similarly disrupts performance monitoring, suggesting involvement of cortical excitatory networks. Additionally, we review emerging evidence for acetylcholine and norepinephrine contributions to attention-related aspects of performance monitoring.
