The convergent findings indicate that performance monitoring depends on complex interactions between multiple neurotransmitter systems, with dopamine and GABA playing particularly prominent roles. Understanding these neurochemical mechanisms has important implications for psychiatric disorders characterized by performance monitoring deficits, including schizophrenia, ADHD, and obsessive-compulsive disorder.

title: Towards a clinical methodology for neuropsychopharmacological research.
abstract: Neuropsychopharmacological research faces significant methodological challenges that limit the translation of preclinical findings to clinical applications. Current approaches often lack standardized protocols for assessment of cognitive and behavioral endpoints, leading to inconsistent results across studies. We propose a comprehensive clinical methodology framework designed to enhance reliability and validity in neuropsychopharmacological investigations. Our approach integrates standardized neurocognitive test batteries, validated rating scales, and objective biomarkers including electroencephalography and neuroimaging parameters. We evaluated this methodology in a pilot study involving 45 healthy volunteers and 38 patients with major depressive disorder, examining the effects of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors on cognitive function over 8 weeks. The proposed battery demonstrated excellent test-retest reliability (ICC = 0.84-0.92) and sensitivity to drug-induced changes in attention, working memory, and executive function. Significant correlations were observed between subjective mood ratings and objective cognitive measures (r = 0.67, p < 0.001). EEG theta/beta ratios showed predictive value for treatment response (AUC = 0.78). Inter-rater reliability for clinical assessments exceeded 0.90 across all measures. The methodology successfully detected subtle cognitive improvements that emerged after 4 weeks of treatment, preceding clinical mood improvements by approximately 2 weeks. This standardized approach provides a robust framework for future neuropsychopharmacological studies, potentially accelerating drug development and improving clinical outcomes through more precise measurement of therapeutic effects.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacologia Hungarica : a Magyar Pszichofarmakologiai Egyesulet lapja = official journal of the Hungarian Association of Psychopharmacology

title: Neuropsychopharmacology at the new millennium: new industry directions.
abstract: The neuropharmaceutical industry stands at a critical juncture as we enter the new millennium, with traditional drug discovery approaches yielding diminishing returns and escalating development costs threatening innovation in central nervous system therapeutics. This review examines emerging paradigms in neuropsychopharmacology research and development based on analysis of industry trends, patent filings, and clinical trial data from 1990-1999. We surveyed major pharmaceutical companies (n=25) and biotechnology firms (n=47) regarding research priorities, technological adoptions, and strategic directions for the next decade. The genomics revolution has fundamentally altered target identification strategies, with 73% of surveyed companies now incorporating pharmacogenomic approaches compared to 12% in 1995. High-throughput screening capabilities have expanded 50-fold on average, while companies are increasingly focusing on mechanism-based drug design rather than serendipitous discovery. Neuroinformatics and computational modeling have emerged as essential tools, with 68% of firms establishing dedicated bioinformatics divisions. Clinical development strategies are shifting toward earlier proof-of-concept studies using biomarkers and surrogate endpoints, potentially reducing late-stage failures. Therapeutic areas showing greatest industry investment include Alzheimer's disease, depression-resistant conditions, and schizophrenia cognitive deficits. Strategic alliances between pharmaceutical companies and academic institutions have increased 340% since 1995. These findings suggest that successful neuropsychopharmacology in the coming decade will require integration of genomics, advanced screening technologies, and novel clinical trial designs to address the growing burden of neuropsychiatric disorders while maintaining economic viability.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Alexander George Karczmar (1917-2017).
abstract: Alexander George Karczmar (1917-2017) was a pioneering figure in neuropharmacology whose six-decade career fundamentally shaped our understanding of cholinergic neurotransmission and neurotoxicology. Born in Poland and educated at the University of Chicago, Karczmar established himself as a leading authority on acetylcholine systems, contributing over 300 publications and mentoring generations of neuroscientists. His early work in the 1950s helped elucidate the mechanisms of acetylcholinesterase inhibition and established foundational principles for understanding anticholinesterase toxicity, particularly relevant to organophosphate poisoning. Karczmar's research group made seminal contributions to characterizing nicotinic and muscarinic receptor subtypes, developing novel cholinergic agonists and antagonists, and mapping cholinergic pathways in the central nervous system. His investigations into the role of acetylcholine in learning and memory preceded and informed modern research on cholinergic dysfunction in Alzheimer's disease. Beyond his scientific contributions, Karczmar served as Editor-in-Chief of Progress in Brain Research and was instrumental in founding the International Society for Neurochemistry. His work on chemical warfare agents and neuroprotective strategies had significant implications for both military and civilian medicine. Karczmar's interdisciplinary approach, combining behavioral pharmacology, biochemistry, and electrophysiology, exemplified the integrative methodology that became standard in modern neuropharmacology. His legacy endures through his students, many of whom became prominent neuroscientists, and through the continued relevance of his fundamental discoveries in cholinergic neurobiology to contemporary therapeutic development.

title: The discovery of chemical neurotransmitters.
abstract: The elucidation of chemical neurotransmission represents one of the most fundamental paradigm shifts in neuroscience, transitioning from purely electrical models of neural communication to our current understanding of synaptic transmission. This historical review examines the key experimental discoveries and methodological advances that established the chemical basis of interneuronal signaling from the early 20th century through the molecular era. We analyzed seminal studies beginning with Loewi's 1921 frog heart experiments demonstrating "Vagusstoff," through Dale's acetylcholine work, Eccles' intracellular recordings at the neuromuscular junction, and subsequent identification of major neurotransmitter systems including catecholamines, amino acids, and neuropeptides. Critical methodological developments included bioassay techniques, electron microscopy revealing synaptic vesicles, subcellular fractionation methods, and receptor binding assays. The convergence of evidence from these approaches definitively established that neurons communicate primarily through calcium-dependent vesicular release of specific chemical messengers that bind to postsynaptic receptors. Key findings included the quantal nature of transmitter release, the existence of distinct receptor subtypes, and the complex machinery governing synaptic vesicle fusion and recycling. These discoveries fundamentally transformed our understanding of neural computation, learning and memory, and neurological disease mechanisms. The identification of chemical neurotransmitters provided the conceptual framework for modern psychopharmacology and continues to drive therapeutic drug development. This historical perspective illuminates how methodological innovation and cross-disciplinary collaboration established the chemical foundations of nervous system function.

title: Neuropsychopharmacology: are we ready for a paradigm shift?
abstract: The field of neuropsychopharmacology has traditionally relied on monoaminergic hypotheses to explain psychiatric disorders and guide drug development. However, accumulating evidence suggests that current paradigms may be insufficient to address the complexity of neuropsychiatric conditions. This review examines emerging conceptual frameworks that may fundamentally alter our approach to understanding brain-behavior relationships and psychopharmacological interventions. We analyzed recent advances in molecular neurobiology, receptor pharmacology, and clinical psychopharmacology from 1990-1996, focusing on developments that challenge existing theoretical models. Key areas examined include glutamatergic and GABAergic neurotransmission, neuropeptide systems, intracellular signaling cascades, and gene expression regulation. Our analysis reveals several paradigm-shifting developments: the recognition of NMDA receptor hypofunction in schizophrenia, the role of neurotrophic factors in depression, the importance of neuroplasticity in therapeutic responses, and the emergence of pharmacogenomics. Additionally, advances in neuroimaging and electrophysiology have provided new insights into circuit-based dysfunction underlying psychiatric symptoms. Clinical evidence suggests that novel therapeutic targets, including metabotropic glutamate receptors, sigma receptors, and second messenger systems, may offer superior efficacy compared to traditional monoaminergic agents. These findings indicate that the field is approaching a critical juncture where fundamental assumptions about psychiatric pathophysiology and drug action require re-evaluation. We conclude that neuropsychopharmacology is indeed ready for a paradigm shift toward more sophisticated, multi-target approaches that integrate molecular, cellular, and systems-level perspectives. This transition will likely accelerate the development of more effective treatments for neuropsychiatric disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Partial response, nonresponse, and relapse with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in major depression: a survey of current "next-step" practices.
abstract: Despite the widespread use of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) as first-line treatment for major depressive disorder, substantial numbers of patients experience partial response, nonresponse, or relapse during treatment. However, limited data exist regarding clinicians' approaches to these treatment challenges in routine clinical practice. We conducted a nationwide survey of 847 psychiatrists and primary care physicians to examine current "next-step" strategies when patients fail to achieve or maintain adequate response to SSRI therapy. Participants completed a structured questionnaire detailing their management approaches for three clinical scenarios: partial response after 8-12 weeks of adequate SSRI dosing, complete nonresponse after 8-12 weeks, and relapse during maintenance therapy. Response rate was 73% (n=618). For partial responders, 54% of clinicians reported dose optimization as their preferred strategy, followed by augmentation with bupropion (23%) or lithium (18%). In cases of nonresponse, 67% favored switching to another SSRI, while 28% preferred switching to a non-SSRI antidepressant. For relapse scenarios, 71% increased the SSRI dose, 19% added augmentation therapy, and 10% switched medications. Significant differences emerged between psychiatrists and primary care physicians, with psychiatrists more likely to utilize augmentation strategies and combination therapies. Geographic variations were also observed, with urban practitioners showing greater willingness to employ complex regimens. These findings highlight substantial heterogeneity in clinical decision-making for SSRI treatment failures and underscore the need for evidence-based guidelines to optimize outcomes in this challenging patient population.
